Bloomberg Surveillance 6/18/2026144 min33 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg Surveillance's 6/18/2026 broadcast covers the dual market shock of Kevin Warsh's unexpectedly hawkish Fed debut — rate hike bets baked in for October, biggest 2-year yield jump on a Fed day since 2008 — against a US-Iran 60-day ceasefire MOU that crashed oil prices and reopened the Strait of Hormuz, with equity markets taking their cue from crude rather than the central bank.
Kevin Warsh's Hawkish Fed Debut · 7US-Iran MOU: 60-Day Ceasefire Structure and Risks · 7Global Central Bank Divergence · 3Equity Valuation Risk & Market Structure · 6AI/Tech: Export Controls, Microsoft China, and Regulation · 5SpaceX Post-IPO & Private Market Access · 5
Kevin Warsh's Hawkish Fed Debut 7
Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting was unambiguously hawkish: inflation was mentioned 19 times versus 4 times for the labor market, the statement was shorter with significantly fewer words, and Warsh himself declined to submit a dot-plot forecast.
data_point🗣 Michael McKee / Annmarie Hordernconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshFOMC
So what: Warsh is effectively running a single-mandate Fed focused on price stability, echoing the ECB model; the dual mandate is operationally deprioritized — bond volatility will structurally increase in this regime.
“The commitment to deliver is strong, unanimous, and unambiguous.” ▶ 38:58
The 2-year Treasury yield jumped 14 basis points on the FOMC day — the biggest single-day increase on a Fed meeting day going back to 2008 — with a rate hike now baked in by the October meeting.
data_point🗣 Lisa Abramowiczconviction: highUS Treasuries2-year yieldFederal Reserve
So what: The SOFR futures strip is re-pricing rapidly; traders expecting rate cuts should reassess positioning — this is a structural regime change, not a one-meeting event.
Nine of 18 FOMC members believe the next rate move will be a hike later in 2026 — a majority calling for tightening is unprecedented in the post-2022 disinflation cycle and signals they are prepared to hike through a supply shock.
data_point🗣 Claudia (New Century Advisors)conviction: highFOMCFederal Reserve
So what: This is not decorative: a committee majority that explicitly prices in hikes through energy supply shocks is a qualitatively different animal from the Powell Fed; bond investors should not rely on the traditional 'Fed put' in inflation scares.
Warsh announced five task forces on: (1) communications, (2) balance sheet, (3) inflation framework, (4) productivity in the AI era, and (5) other sources of inflation — with conclusions expected by year-end, signaling the dot plot could be eliminated or restructured.
data_point🗣 Kevin Warshconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: The task force on 'productivity in the AI era' is non-trivial — it means the Fed is explicitly examining whether AI-driven productivity growth could allow a different long-run neutral rate, which has direct implications for duration.
Warsh's deliberate silence on the dot plot and lack of explanation for why the committee held rates despite declaring a price-stability mission creates a communication credibility problem — markets will fill the blanks with more volatility, demanding a higher term premium.
causal🗣 Claudia (New Century Advisors)conviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Without forward guidance, each data release (especially CPI and PCE) becomes a standalone volatility event — implied vol on short-dated Treasuries should remain structurally elevated.
Warsh committed to reducing the Fed's mortgage-backed securities (MBS) holdings to zero, a tighter stance than Powell's — this will not help the housing market and will structurally remove a bid from the MBS market.
thesis🗣 Ed Hymanconviction: highFederal ReserveMBShousing market
So what: MBS spreads likely widen structurally as the Fed exits; this is a headwind for housing affordability and a risk for financial institutions with large MBS exposure.
Deutsche Bank raised rate-hike expectations and Citigroup pushed back potential rate cuts after the Warsh meeting; the market now prices a hike as soon as next month (July).
data_point🗣 Lisa Abramowiczconviction: highDeutsche BankCitigroupFederal Reserve
So what: Street consensus is rapidly converging on a hike — contrarian opportunity is in duration if oil continues to fall and headline CPI prints lower in the next 2 cycles.
US-Iran MOU: 60-Day Ceasefire Structure and Risks 7
The US-Iran interim deal is a 14-point MOU providing a 60-day military pause — not a durable strategic settlement. Iran gets no-charge Hormuz passage for 60 days, sanctions relief, and a $300B economic development fund (as a carrot); the US gets the strait reopened and 60 days of negotiating space.
data_point🗣 Norman (Bloomberg analyst)conviction: highUSIranStrait of HormuzMOU
So what: The $300B fund is largely fictional — Gulf states that were attacked by Iran have no incentive to donate; the real deliverable is oil market normalization and time for the Trump administration to claim a win ahead of midterms.
≈ “The U.S.-Iran deal is best understood as a high-value pause on the current military phase of the conflict. Not a durable strategic setup. The deal buys time it does not build trust.” paraphrase
Saudi tankers were observed traversing the Strait of Hormuz with their AIS transponders on within hours of the MOU announcement — the first confirmed safe transit, confirming the deal has immediate operational effect on oil flows.
data_point🗣 Annmarie Hordernconviction: highSaudi ArabiaStrait of HormuzIran
So what: The physical oil relief signal is confirmed; however, ~600 vessels remain displaced from the Strait and will take time to clear — the full oil price normalization is weeks away, not days.
Oil prices (WTI) fell back to ~$74–75/barrel — the lowest levels since the start of Operation Epic Fury — while December Brent fell to ~$76–80 from a high of ~$93; gasoline dropped below $4/gallon for the first time since March but remains above pre-war levels and is not expected to return to those levels until next year.
data_point🗣 Lisa Abramowicz / Bloomberg dataconviction: highWTIBrentgasolineUS energy market
So what: The oil relief is real but partial; refiners, petrochemicals, and airline hedging positions need to reprice; gasoline still ~$0.40/gallon above pre-war levels means consumer relief is incomplete ahead of midterms.
Iran's nuclear program was devastated and its missile program severely damaged, but the regime remains in place and its militias (Hezbollah, Houthis) are no more restricted than before the war — a deeply asymmetric outcome for the US.
thesis🗣 Norman (Bloomberg analyst)conviction: highIranIRGCHezbollahHouthis
So what: The regime's survival means the fundamental threat architecture in the Middle East is unchanged; defense spending elevated, Gulf state security cooperation enhanced — long defense, neutral oil.
Lebanon is the key spoiler risk: neither Israel nor Hezbollah are signatories to the MOU, and a single high-casualty event in Lebanon could instantly collapse the 60-day window.
prediction🗣 Norman (Bloomberg analyst)conviction: highLebanonHezbollahIsraelIran MOU
So what: Lebanon/Hezbollah is the tail risk that could re-open the Strait of Hormuz conflict; oil volatility puts are cheap relative to this binary risk.
The IEA flipped from alarm to forecasting a 2027 oil supply glut — within weeks of the MOU announcement — indicating that forward oil markets are pricing a sustained Hormuz reopening even before the 60-day clock expires.
data_point🗣 Annmarie Hordernconviction: mediumIEAoil marketStrait of Hormuz
So what: If the 60-day window fails (Lebanon spoiler, Iran backtracking), oil will snap back violently from levels priced for a durable peace; put spreads on USO are worth considering.
Trump's Iran deal was partially forced by political necessity: US oil SPR hit its lowest level since 1983, gasoline at $5+/gallon was politically unsustainable with midterm elections approaching, and the administration had exhausted its military options for keeping the strait open at low cost.
causal🗣 Annmarie Hordern / Normanconviction: highTrumpUS SPRmidterm electionsIran
So what: The political calendar (midterms) is now the key variable for deal sustainability — the administration needs the oil relief to hold through November; any deal collapse before then would be catastrophic for Republican incumbents.
Global Central Bank Divergence 3
The Bank of England held rates at 3.75% in a 7-2 decision (two members voted for 25bp hike), with Governor Andrew Bailey explicitly warning that 'pressures from the war in Iran are still in the pipeline despite the truce' — signaling the BOE will not cut rates before April 2027 (per TD Securities' revised call).
data_point🗣 Lizzy Burden / Pooja Kumra (TD Securities)conviction: highBank of EnglandAndrew BaileyUKGBP
So what: GBP/USD at 1.3219 (down 0.5%) reflects the BOE's dovish-relative-to-Fed stance; UK gilts face a stagflationary backdrop with fiscal pressure added — underweight UK duration.
The ECB hiked last week despite the Iran truce, and TD Securities believes the ECB's minimum rate path was 'never one and done' — two hikes minimum — with September a live meeting for a second hike if energy disruption persists.
prediction🗣 Pooja Kumra (TD Securities)conviction: mediumECBChristine Lagardeeurozone
So what: EUR rates diverge from GBP rates — ECB more hawkish than BOE; EUR/GBP may appreciate if ECB follows through on a second hike while BOE stays on hold.
The Warsh Fed's hawkish turn has raised the bar for other central banks to be dovish — dollar strength was spreading across all G10 currencies, with USD/JPY flirting with the weakest yen levels since 2024.
causal🗣 Lisa Abramowiczconviction: highUSDJPYG10 currenciesDXY
So what: Dollar bull trade is back in play via Fed hawkishness + oil relief (yen carry re-emerging); USD/JPY testing 160 is the key trigger for BOJ intervention risk.
Equity Valuation Risk & Market Structure 6
Equity markets rallied +0.8–1.6% despite the hawkish Fed because the oil price signal dominated — traders took their cue from falling crude rather than rising 2-year yields, but an equity strategist warned the market is 'not leaving any room for any mistakes.'
thesis🗣 Jessica (equity strategist, Bloomberg guest)conviction: highS&P 500NasdaqoilFederal Reserve
So what: The market's resilience is conditional on oil continuing to fall AND earnings continuing to deliver AND no Lebanon spoiler — a three-way bet with compounding tail risk.
“When no one else is concerned about the risks, that makes me feel more concerned about the risks.” ▶ 2:18:35
2027 earnings estimates have increased more year-to-date than 2026 estimates — the market is pricing AI-driven earnings acceleration for at least two more years, setting an extremely high bar for any hiccup.
data_point🗣 Jessica (equity strategist)conviction: highS&P 500AI earningsequity multiples
So what: If the AI capex cycle stumbles or the credit cycle tightens from Warsh rate hikes, the 2027 estimate revision cycle could be brutal; options skew for 2027 calls is worth monitoring.
The 2/10 yield curve was at its narrowest level going back to March 2025 on the day — the flat/inverted curve signals the market expects near-term hawkishness to produce eventual disinflation and long-end stability.
data_point🗣 Lisa Abramowiczconviction: highyield curveUS Treasuries2/10 spread
So what: A flat yield curve with rate hike expectations priced in is a classic late-cycle signal; financials (net interest margin) benefit if hikes deliver without a recession, but are severely hurt if a recession follows.
The Philadelphia Fed business survey jumped to 10.3 from -0.4, but the prices-paid index rose to 53.2 from 47.9 — confirming the war drove inflation into the real economy and the pipeline remains hot even as oil falls.
data_point🗣 Michael McKeeconviction: highPhiladelphia FedPPIinflation
So what: Services and manufacturing inflation remain sticky even with oil falling — validates the Warsh focus on second and third-order effects; the inflation pipeline will not clear quickly.
Initial jobless claims came in at 226,000 (above the 225,000 estimate) with continuing claims rising to 1.81 million — the labor market is stable but not tight; Warsh gets a pass on employment for now, cementing his inflation-only focus.
data_point🗣 Michael McKeeconviction: highlabor marketjobless claimsFederal Reserve
So what: With a stable labor market, the Fed has no political cover to cut rates on employment grounds — the October rate hike scenario is credible; positioning for a hike and eventual long-end rally is the trade.
A private credit fund manager reported falling within the 5% redemption limit, allowing full satisfaction of investor demand — flagged as the first major firm to stem a growing exodus from the industry.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg Briefconviction: mediumprivate creditalternative asset management
So what: Private credit redemption pressures are easing at the margin; however, one data point doesn't confirm systemic stabilization — monitor closely as rate hike expectations re-price leverage costs in the portfolio.
AI/Tech: Export Controls, Microsoft China, and Regulation 5
Microsoft is selling AI services to top Chinese firms including over $1B in orders from ByteDance — creating a direct conflict with the administration's export controls on Anthropic and raising questions about whether the administration will tell Microsoft to dial back its China AI business.
thesis🗣 Annmarie Hordernconviction: highMicrosoftByteDanceAnthropicChina AIexport controls
So what: Microsoft is operationally taking the opposite regulatory bet from Anthropic; if the administration expands China AI controls to MSFT, it would be a major negative for Azure revenues in China (~1.5% of total but rapidly growing).
OpenAI is worried that Microsoft's ByteDance relationship allows China to 'distill' US frontier models — i.e., extract capabilities from Microsoft's cloud-served AI into Chinese-controlled models, bypassing US export controls.
thesis🗣 Lisa Abramowiczconviction: mediumOpenAIMicrosoftByteDancemodel distillationChina
So what: Model distillation via API access is the primary evasion route for US AI export controls — it is a structural gap in the current regulatory framework and a key risk to US AI leadership.
There are active discussions within the Trump administration about taking an equity stake in major AI companies — a nationalization-adjacent policy that would blur the line between government and private AI development.
prediction🗣 Annmarie Hordernconviction: exploratoryUS governmentAnthropicOpenAIAI regulation
So what: A government equity stake would give the administration direct governance rights and revenue share in AI winners; this is the 'golden share' model and would be a precedent-setting structural change for AI company governance.
The EU AI Markets Act is already preventing the most advanced AI iPhone features from launching in Europe at initial availability — the regulatory divergence between the US (light-touch) and EU (precautionary) is materializing into real commercial disadvantages for Apple in Europe.
data_point🗣 Jennifer Huddleston (Cato Institute)conviction: highAppleEU AI ActiPhoneEU tech regulation
So what: Apple's EU revenue risk from AI feature restrictions is real; the EU's regulatory approach is creating a two-tier AI product market — watch for EU pressure on Apple's premium pricing power.
The G7 debate on AI centered on whether industry-wide voluntary norms (analogous to self-regulation) can substitute for mandatory regulation — the US is pushing the light-touch model while Europe pushes heavy-handed regulation, with no consensus achieved.
thesis🗣 Jennifer Huddleston (Cato Institute)conviction: mediumG7AI governanceUSEUAI regulation
So what: Without international regulatory consensus, US AI companies face fragmented compliance regimes globally — a structural overhead cost that smaller AI competitors cannot absorb.
SpaceX Post-IPO & Private Market Access 5
Schwab handled 140,000 client inquiries related to the SpaceX IPO and saw demand across all demographics (young and old) — SpaceX was trading at ~$185/share, still 40%+ above its IPO price despite a volatile session that saw it down 7% at one point.
data_point🗣 Charles Schwab CEOconviction: highSpaceXCharles Schwabretail investors
So what: Retail demand is real and durable even at elevated prices — the next Anthropic or OpenAI IPO will see the same retail FOMO dynamic; Schwab and Robinhood are the primary beneficiaries of IPO distribution.
SpaceX first traded on the Forge private platform at ~$60B valuation; those early investors are now seeing 35x returns. More retail money was 'repositioned' out of existing holdings to fund SpaceX IPO purchases — a portfolio rotation signal, not just incremental buying.
data_point🗣 Rick (Forge platform CEO)conviction: highSpaceXForgeprivate marketsretail investors
So what: The SpaceX IPO sucked capital out of existing equity positions — watch for selling pressure in mid-cap tech as retail investors rebalance; Forge-type private market platforms are structural beneficiaries of the trend toward late-stage private unicorn exposure.
Apple will raise iPhone prices due to memory chip cost increases — Tim Cook cited the unsustainability of absorbing rising memory prices; memory chipmakers (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron) rallied on the news.
data_point🗣 Tim Cook (Apple) / Vonnie Quinnconviction: highAppleSamsungSK HynixMicronDRAMNAND
So what: Apple is passing memory cost inflation to consumers — confirms DRAM/NAND pricing power for manufacturers; memory is no longer the commodity cycle loser in the AI capex era.
Intel surged 9% intraday after Trump announced Apple will work with Intel on domestic chip production — Trump cited NVIDIA's Terrfab project and Apple discussions as evidence of re-shoring momentum.
data_point🗣 Vonnie Quinnconviction: highIntelAppleNVIDIATerrfabdomestic semiconductor manufacturing
So what: Intel's foundry strategy is now politically supported at the highest level; Apple's diversification away from TSMC dependency is accelerating — a structural positive for Intel if execution matches rhetoric.
Enphase Energy was upgraded and rallied 5.76% after announcing a pivot into AI data center energy solutions, having been squeezed out of the residential solar market by Chinese competition.
data_point🗣 Vonnie Quinnconviction: mediumEnphasesolar energyAI data centersChina competition
So what: The AI data center power demand is providing a lifeline to residential solar/storage companies being undercut by Chinese competitors — watch for similar pivots by SunPower, Sunnova, and others.
US Concerned with ASML Over China Chip Tool | The China Show 6/19/202693 min45 insights2026-06-19
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's China Show covers the fragile US-Iran ceasefire and Strait of Hormuz reopening, a Bloomberg scoop alleging ASML illegally shipped EUV equipment to China, the hawkish pivot under new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, China's oil demand peak driven by EV penetration and plastics substitution, and escalating China-Japan defense tensions — all against a backdrop of a 35% Nasdaq rally that now faces valuation questions.

US-Iran Ceasefire & Strait of Hormuz 10
Bloomberg Economics scored the US-Iran ceasefire MOU 10 points in Iran's favor, 1 for the US, and 3 neutral — characterizing the exchange as 'uneven' and saying Washington merely recovers pre-war benefits.
data_point🗣 Bloomberg Economics / anchor commentaryconviction: highIranUSStrait of Hormuz
So what: If Iran structurally gained ground in the ceasefire, the 60-day negotiation period starts from a weak US position, raising tail risk that the deal collapses and oil spikes back above pre-war highs.
“10 of those points in favor of Iran, one favors the U.S., and three are neutral. So the exchange they say is uneven.” ▶ 29:23
John Bolton, a former Trump NSA, called the deal 'a huge disappointment' and 'a real defeat for the United States,' arguing the US fell into a trap of Iran's making and that the deal does not clearly open the Strait or address the nuclear and missile programs.
contrarian🗣 John Boltonconviction: highIranUSJohn BoltonStrait of Hormuz
So what: Bipartisan hawkish criticism — including from Trump's own former advisers — suggests the political window for Trump to accept a weak deal is narrowing, increasing volatility risk before the November midterms.
“It's a huge disappointment, constitutes a real defeat for the United States.” ▶ 28:20
Former Obama deputy NSA Mara Rudman argued Iran proved to be 'masters of asymmetric warfare' by using Strait of Hormuz closure as a lever 'as powerful as having a nuclear weapon,' catching the US administration off-guard strategically.
framework🗣 Mara Rudmanconviction: highIranUSStrait of Hormuz
So what: The Strait-as-weapon framework implies Iran retains enormous optionality to re-escalate economically without nuclear escalation — a non-obvious ongoing risk premium for oil even post-ceasefire.
“Some would argue as powerful as having a nuclear weapon, which was their ability to control and block traffic going in and out of the Strait of Hormuz.” ▶ 35:49
Rudman was highly skeptical a deal addressing Iran's 900+ pounds of 60%-enriched uranium plus inspection regimes could be completed in 60 days; the 2015 Obama JCPOA took two years to negotiate.
causal🗣 Mara Rudmanconviction: highIranUSJCPOA
So what: A missed 60-day deadline or a weak partial deal prolongs uncertainty for oil markets and could trigger renewed conflict risk premium.
≈ “If we are lucky, if we are fortunate, the Trump team will come out of the 60 day or however long period of negotiations with something that we can only hope is somewhere close to what the Obama plan achieved.” paraphrase
Russia has offered to take custody of Iran's stockpile of 900+ pounds of highly enriched uranium — a pathway the US explicitly does not want to see.
data_point🗣 Mara Rudmanconviction: highRussiaIranUS
So what: A Russia-Iran nuclear materials transfer would deepen US-Russia antagonism and potentially trigger new sanctions waves with cross-asset ripple effects.
Major shipping company executives told Bloomberg within 24 hours of the ceasefire that they were unwilling to commit vessels to the Strait without insurer coverage — Trump's statements alone are insufficient for routing decisions.
causal🗣 Mara Rudmanconviction: highStrait of Hormuzshipping companiesIran
So what: The freight/insurance market is the real gating factor for Strait traffic normalization — watch war risk insurance premiums, not just political declarations, to gauge when oil supply will fully normalize.
“I think there is going to be a fair amount of due diligence before you see regular flows of ships through the Strait.” ▶ 39:39
In the first 24 hours post-ceasefire, 7 tankers transited the Strait, including at least 3 Saudi supertankers, with 8 million barrels passing through and a further 7 million expected — still far below pre-war levels.
data_point🗣 Yvonne / anchorconviction: highStrait of HormuzSaudi Arabiaoil
So what: The trickle of initial tanker traffic tells investors supply normalization will be gradual, not instantaneous, keeping near-term oil prices structurally lower than feared but not at pre-war levels yet.
Iran is pushing for a managerial/revenue-generating role over Strait traffic — effectively a toll or fee-for-service — which the US is keen to avoid but has not definitively ruled out.
thesis🗣 Derek (Bloomberg Senior Editor, San Francisco)conviction: mediumIranUSStrait of Hormuz
So what: A permanent Iranian transit toll would be structurally inflationary for global shipping and energy costs, representing a novel geopolitical rent extraction mechanism that markets have not priced.
“You could call that a toll, a fee-for-service, a tax, you could call it whatever you want to, but it would be a meaningful change. And it would be one that would have a disruptive and inflationary result for the world economy.” ▶ 6:27
Trump's primary motivation to settle was economic — he explicitly invoked fear of becoming 'another Herbert Hoover' in public remarks, signaling domestic recession/political risk was the key driver of the deal.
causal🗣 Mara Rudmanconviction: highTrumpUSIran
So what: With US midterms as a hard constraint, Trump has strong incentive to keep the ceasefire intact even on disadvantageous terms; markets should treat the ceasefire as durable in the near-term despite its weaknesses.
“His concerns about the economy and not wanting to be another Herbert Hoover, the President when the United States went into a deep depression. That is clearly front of mind for him.” ▶ 40:36
US gasoline prices remain $1/gallon above pre-conflict levels even after the ceasefire and are unlikely to drop to pre-war levels quickly due to logistical lags in tanker routing.
data_point🗣 Mara Rudmanconviction: highUSgasolineoil
So what: Persistent above-pre-war fuel prices give Iran ongoing leverage and limit Trump's political flexibility — watch US pump prices as a leading indicator of his negotiating tolerance.
“They are below four dollars a gallon in the United States, but still a dollar higher than before the conflict began.” ▶ 41:33
ASML / China Chip Export Controls 5
US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has directly accused ASML leadership of shipping one of its top-of-the-line EUV (extreme ultraviolet) lithography machines to China in violation of export controls, though US officials have not confirmed they have evidence of a complete machine in China — only evidence of components and shipping equipment.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low (Bloomberg reporter, Shanghai)conviction: highASMLHoward LutnickChinaUS Commerce Department
So what: The evidentiary gap — components vs. full machine — is critical: ASML faces a 'prove a negative' burden while the US threatens to restrict the 20% of ASML revenue from China legacy machine sales as leverage.
“ASML is now left having to prove a negative and the officials are saying ASML is acting in bad faith.” ▶ 16:53
ASML's EUV machines are the size of a school bus, require regular ASML staff maintenance, and the company says it can automatically detect abnormal interruption or loss of connectivity to its machines — making covert transfer operationally very difficult.
data_point🗣 Minmin Low / ASML spokespersonconviction: highASML
So what: ASML's technical arguments are credible; the more likely explanation for China's chip progress is engineering workarounds using legacy DUV tools, not a smuggled EUV machine — implications differ for the severity of the US response.
Bloomberg's in-house technology analyst suggested the US allegations may reflect Chinese chip engineering progress using legacy DUV tools rather than any compliance failure by ASML — China is increasingly able to make more advanced chips with legacy equipment via multi-patterning techniques.
contrarian🗣 Bloomberg analystconviction: mediumASMLChinasemiconductorSMIC
So what: If China is achieving advanced nodes through DUV multi-patterning rather than smuggled EUV, the policy response (restricting ASML's legacy sales to China) would harm ASML's revenue without actually slowing China's progress.
“China is increasingly able to make more advanced chips with legacy tools and U.S. concerns may reflect Chinese engineering progress rather than lapse in ASML's compliance with controls.” ▶ 1:26:57
The US-ASML dispute threatens US-Netherlands and broader US-EU diplomatic relations; the Netherlands government stated it enforces rules strictly but has not sided with the US accusation, and further escalation depends on whether the US provides actual evidence.
causal🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: mediumASMLNetherlandsEUUS
So what: A transatlantic semiconductor trade dispute adds another vector of geopolitical fracture — watch for Netherlands/EU pushback on US export control extraterritoriality as a signal of alliance stress.
“Depending on how far the U.S. administration pushes this, it can increase the tension between the U.S. and Netherlands potentially with broader EU in general.” ▶ 18:44
ASML derives approximately 20% of total revenue from China through sales of legacy (DUV) lithography machines, making punitive US action directly threatening to ASML's P&L.
data_point🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: highASMLChina
So what: Any US threat to restrict ASML's remaining China access is a quantifiable headwind: 20% revenue at risk is a material negative catalyst for ASML stock regardless of the legal merits.
≈ “Threatening the 20% of revenue ASML gets from revenue. It still sells older lines of their machines to China at this point.” paraphrase
Fed Policy Shift Under Kevin Warsh 4
Markets have re-priced from expecting two Fed rate cuts to now pricing a definite rate hike before year-end 2026 and two hikes by March 2027, reflecting the hawkish signal from new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's first press conference.
data_point🗣 Tony Sycamore (IG Australia)conviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshUS rates
So what: The full repricing from two cuts to two hikes by March 2027 represents a dramatic shift in the rate path — duration assets remain vulnerable and rate-sensitive EM currencies face sustained pressure.
≈ “We were pricing in two Fed rate cuts and now we're pricing in a definite rate hike before year end and we have two rate hikes priced by March 2027.” paraphrase
Kevin Warsh's early signal is that he will prioritize inflation credibility above all else and will not provide the forward guidance markets became accustomed to under Powell — instead putting more onus on markets to respond to incoming data.
thesis🗣 Tony Sycamoreconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshJerome Powell
So what: A data-dependent, no-forward-guidance Fed means higher realized volatility across rates, FX and equities — investors should structurally expect wider dispersion and reduce reliance on rate path visibility.
“Early indications are that he will be more hawkish and potentially we saw from Fed Chair Powell. And additionally we will just not get that forward guidance we become used to.” ▶ 12:09
The BOJ hiked rates this week but market consensus is that more tightening is needed; dollar-yen remains above 161, and Japanese finance officials are reiterating willingness to take 'bold action' against speculative FX moves.
data_point🗣 Anchor commentary / Avrilconviction: mediumBank of JapanBOJyendollar-yen
So what: With USD/JPY above 161 and Japan still seen as needing more hikes, the yen carry trade unwind risk is elevated — especially if Warsh's hawkishness further strengthens the dollar.
Bipartisan US senators are urging Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to pressure China on its 'undervalued' renminbi, with calls to label China a currency manipulator in the next FX report; offshore USD/CNH is near 6.77.
data_point🗣 Anchor commentaryconviction: mediumChinarenminbiScott BessentUS Treasury
So what: A currency manipulator designation would be a significant escalation in US-China trade tensions — watch the next US FX report as a potential catalyst for CNH volatility and broader EM risk-off.
Tech & Chip Sector Rally / Valuation 6
The Nasdaq rallied approximately 35% over nine weeks, but Amazon and Nvidia are now ~12% below recent highs while Microsoft and Meta are near their March lows — suggesting breadth has narrowed to Intel and AMD leading, not the full Mag-7.
data_point🗣 Tony Sycamoreconviction: highNasdaqAmazonNvidiaMicrosoftMetaIntelAMDMag-7
So what: The rally broadening to Intel/AMD but fading in mega-cap leaders is a classic late-cycle tech pattern — the index cannot sustain gains without Mag-7 participation, suggesting a near-term consolidation.
≈ “About 35% if we are just looking at the Nasdaq over nine weeks. Amazon and Nvidia about 12% below the recent lows. Microsoft and Meta are not that far away from their March lows.” paraphrase
South Korean equities (KOSPI, Samsung, SK Hynix) are considered attractively valued versus US tech peers and represent a more compelling way to express the AI capex/chip demand thesis, given stretched US valuations.
thesis🗣 Tony Sycamoreconviction: mediumSouth KoreaKOSPISamsungSK Hynix
So what: Samsung and SK Hynix offer a value-relative-to-AI-capex trade; SK Hynix shipping next-gen HBM chip samples (up ~8% on the day) is a concrete near-term catalyst.
“South Korea certainly doesn't look expensive and there is still demand for the key components of that index. Samsung, to me that still seems like a good thematic in the weeks and months ahead.” ▶ 14:29
Amazon is in active talks to sell custom AI chips to other companies' data centers to cut into Nvidia's dominance; Amazon's AI chief confirmed discussions have begun but declined to name customers.
data_point🗣 Anchor / Bloomberg reportingconviction: highAmazonNvidiaAI chips
So what: Amazon entering the third-party AI chip market is a direct competitive threat to Nvidia's data-center monopoly and validates that hyperscaler custom silicon is becoming a product, not just a cost save.
Trump announced Intel will work with Apple to design and produce semiconductors in the US; Intel had struck an unconventional deal with the US government last year making the government one of its biggest investors.
data_point🗣 Anchorconviction: mediumIntelAppleTrumpUS government
So what: Government-backed Intel-Apple partnership is a structural shift — it changes Intel's competitive dynamics with TSMC and ASML and positions Intel as a policy-embedded national champion.
AI disruption is beginning to cannibalize consulting services revenues; Indian IT sector stocks were falling on the day, with commentary that AI could reduce revenue for consulting firms in coming months.
causal🗣 Anchor commentaryconviction: mediumAIIT consultingAccentureIndian IT
So what: Sell-side IT services (Infosys, Wipro, TCS, Accenture) face a structural AI-displacement headwind to billable hours — the market is beginning to price this in but the transition has further to run.
≈ “AI disruptions, could it upend consulting services. They could have less revenue in the coming months.” paraphrase
SpaceX bankers are preparing investor calls for a bond offering of at least $20 billion, potentially beginning as soon as the following Monday, following SpaceX's record IPO.
data_point🗣 Anchor / Bloomberg reportingconviction: highSpaceXElon Musk
So what: $20B+ SpaceX bond deal is one of the largest private tech debt offerings ever — a successful deal at tight spreads would signal deep institutional appetite for private-market AI/space infrastructure debt.
“Bankers for SpaceX are preparing to hold calls with investors to discuss a potential bond offering of at least $20 billion.” ▶ 20:40
China Oil Demand Peak 7
China's oil imports fell to an 8-year low of under 8 million barrels per day in May 2026 — roughly one-third less than before the Iran war broke out — a 4.6 million b/d drop equal to a quarter to a third of China's entire oil demand.
data_point🗣 David (Bloomberg columnist/analyst)conviction: highChinaoilOPEC
So what: If China's structural oil demand decline is real (not a data anomaly), it fundamentally alters the global oil demand outlook and is more bearish for long-term oil prices than any geopolitical resolution.
“4.6 million barrel drop a day, that is a third to a quarter of China's entire oil demand.” ▶ 1:18:50
Bloomberg analyst David explains China's oil import collapse through three transparent, published factors: (1) elimination of 2.5 mb/d in above-refinery-capacity stockpiling done in prior years; (2) EV/PHEV penetration destroying 800,000 b/d of gasoline demand (April public EV charging fell 37% month-on-month); (3) plastics production collapse (down 62% in Asia) removing 700,000 b/d, partly substituted by ethane from the US (another 300-400k b/d).
framework🗣 David (Bloomberg)conviction: highChinaoilEVsplasticsnaphtha
So what: This three-factor decomposition means China's oil demand decline is structural (EVs, plastics substitution) plus cyclical (destocking) — the structural portion does not reverse when the Strait reopens, putting a ceiling on oil price recovery.
≈ “China's oil demand, as a lot of owners have indicated, has peaked and is in decline.” paraphrase
China produces the same tonnage of plastics as gasoline — under-appreciated outside China — and naphtha prices doubled in March 2026 during the Strait closure, causing plastic resin production to fall at the fastest pace on record, removing 700,000 b/d of oil demand.
data_point🗣 David (Bloomberg)conviction: highChinanaphthaplasticsoil
So what: The plastics-oil linkage is a non-obvious channel: as the Strait reopens, naphtha normalization will revive some plastics production and restore 700k b/d of demand, partially offsetting EV structural losses.
“China produces the same tonnage of plastics as gasoline. I think that is under appreciated outside the country.” ▶ 1:20:53
China's EV/PHEV fleet has made gasoline demand more price-elastic than pre-EV economic models assumed: PHEV owners can simply stop filling tanks when prices spike, instantly destroying demand, a dynamic not captured in traditional oil demand models.
framework🗣 David (Bloomberg)conviction: highChinaEVsoil demandgasoline
So what: China's PHEV fleet creates a new high-elasticity demand layer that traditional IEA/OPEC demand models underestimate — making oil demand forecasts structurally optimistic and oil price ceilings lower.
“If you have a plug-in hybrid car, you can just stop filling it up at the tank and you have destroyed your oil demand.” ▶ 1:20:05
Asian refiners are already well-supplied for June and July despite the Strait reopening — the incoming wave of Persian Gulf oil (millions of barrels stored since the war began) arrives into a market that is already sated in the short term.
causal🗣 Stephen Stapczynski (Bloomberg energy reporter)conviction: highAsian refineriesoilStrait of HormuzPersian Gulf
So what: Near-term oversupply risk is real: refined product inventories are full, incoming oil wave is large, and demand is weak — Brent could re-test $70 or lower even in the short term.
“Bloomberg sources say crude is coming at a time when refiners in Asia are already well supplied for this month and the next.” ▶ 1:13:46
Pre-war Brent oil prices were around $70 or even lower; with Iranian oil sanctions waivers plus steady US output plus the incoming Persian Gulf supply wave, the market could quickly return to those levels or undershoot them.
prediction🗣 Stephen Stapczynskiconviction: mediumBrent crudeIranOPECUS oil
So what: A Brent price at $70 or below would be significantly negative for energy sector equities and positive for consumer/industrial sectors in oil-importing Asian economies.
“If U.S. output remains consistently high, you will be hitting those prewar levels which would be $70 Brent or even lower if there is an emergence of an oversupply at least in the short term.” ▶ 1:16:42
Qatar's LNG allocation after the Strait reopening will be driven by relationships and geopolitical ties, not purely spot prices — Pakistan received most of Qatar's LNG despite not being the highest bidder because Pakistan helped mediate the ceasefire.
framework🗣 Stephen Stapczynskiconviction: highQatarLNGPakistan
So what: LNG allocation by political relationship rather than price means highest-bidder buyers (Japan, Korea) may not receive priority supply even after the Strait reopens — spot LNG prices may diverge from what market clearing implies.
≈ “It is about relationships. Most of the LNG supply out of Qatar has actually gone to Pakistan. Pakistan not the richest country. Definitely not the highest bidder. But there is this relationship here. They helped mediate this deal so they get something.” paraphrase
Indonesia / EM Asia Markets 4
MSCI's latest review ahead of its decision on Indonesia's market status flagged 'continued opacity in shareholding' and 'coordinated trading behavior' — the latter a strongly worded phrase implying price manipulation concerns — as new obstacles to retaining EM status.
data_point🗣 Lianting (Bloomberg managing editor, Asian equities)conviction: highIndonesiaMSCIJakarta CompositeIDX
So what: A coordinated-trading accusation from MSCI signals a possible downgrade from EM to Frontier status next week; such a downgrade would force passive EM fund outflows from Indonesian equities.
“The coordinated trading behavior, that could hurt the proper price formation. That is quite strongly worded and a loaded phrase.” ▶ 49:21
Bank Indonesia has already hiked rates 100 basis points between May and June 2026, with at least one more 25bp hike expected in H2 — an aggressive frontloaded response to currency pressure and inflation risk.
data_point🗣 Radhika Rao (Commerzbank, Executive Director/Economist)conviction: highBank IndonesiaIndonesiarupiah
So what: BI's frontloaded 100bp+ cycle is designed to anchor the rupiah, but if the Fed stays hawkish and the dollar strengthens, the rate differential may still prove insufficient to stem outflows.
≈ “They have taken successive rate increases already between May and June. They raise rates by about 100 basis points.” paraphrase
Radhika Rao argued that BI and BSP hiking despite the Iran ceasefire sends a strong signal that Asian central banks are not assuming the deal holds — they're hedging against a deal breakdown that could send oil higher again.
framework🗣 Radhika Raoconviction: mediumBank IndonesiaBSPPhilippinesAsian central banks
So what: Asian CB behavior is a real-time market signal — their refusal to ease post-ceasefire reflects genuine skepticism about deal durability; this is a bearish contrarian indicator for oil-price-peace-dividend trades.
“B.I. and BSP's hikes tell you there is a ceasefire and potentially a deal. But regional policymakers are not taking it easy.” ▶ 59:05
Indonesia's Jakarta Composite is the world's worst-performing equity market in this period, and the rupiah erased most of the gains from the previous day's 25bp hike within a single session.
data_point🗣 Anchor commentaryconviction: highIndonesiaJakarta Compositerupiah
So what: The rupiah's rapid reversal post-hike highlights that rate actions alone cannot offset structural investor concern about governance, MSCI status, and fiscal credibility — a sell-the-rate-hike dynamic.
Alibaba / AI Capex & European Tech Gap 3
Alibaba Group Chairman framed AI as a $50 trillion economy — not just IT budget displacement — targeting the ~$66 trillion labor segment of global $110 trillion GDP (60% of world GDP is labor), arguing AI's TAM is human productivity itself.
framework🗣 Alibaba Group Chairmanconviction: highAlibabaAI
So what: A $50T AI TAM framing justifies multi-year heavy capex regardless of near-term ROI — bullish for AI infrastructure suppliers but implies AI companies must sustain extremely high capex for much longer than consensus expects.
“It's a $50 trillion economy because it is all about human productivity and human intelligence and roughly in the world there's about 110 trillion dollars GDP and about 60% of that is labor. AI is going after that segment.” ▶ 24:35
Alibaba has transformed from a capital-light marketplace business into a 'heavy balance sheet company' due to AI capex demands, and the Chairman explicitly said the key differentiator is having a clear view of the future to justify ROI confidence.
thesis🗣 Alibaba Group Chairmanconviction: highAlibaba
So what: Alibaba's balance sheet transformation to AI-heavy capex is a sector-wide dynamic — every major platform company is undergoing the same shift, compressing free cash flow and increasing leverage risk industry-wide.
“We have become a heavy balance sheet company pretty quickly over the last few years.” ▶ 26:36
A European tech investor at VivaTech argued the EU-US GDP gap of roughly $2 trillion equals the accumulated market cap of the US big five tech firms — representing a structural wealth transfer from Europe to the US via AI dependency.
causal🗣 European investor (VivaTech panel)conviction: mediumEUUSBig TechAI
So what: Europe's failure to build sovereign AI application layers means ongoing GDP leakage to US tech — creating policy pressure for EU AI industrial policy and potential for EU-based AI infrastructure investment themes.
“We are seeing the largest voluntary transfer of wealth from Europe and other regions toward the U.S.” ▶ 25:45
China-Japan Defense Tensions 4
US Pentagon estimates China's actual defense spending is 30-60% higher than reported, as items like armed forces and coast guard are listed under 'public security' headers rather than defense, systematically understating the official budget figure.
data_point🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: highChinaJapanUS Pentagondefense spending
So what: If China's true defense spending is 30-60% above official figures, the effective military balance in Asia is more asymmetric than markets price, raising risk premiums for Taiwan-adjacent assets.
“The U.S. is repeatedly saying China has underreported defense data into the Pentagon estimating actual spending is about 30-60 percent higher than reported.” ▶ 1:06:36
Japan's defense budget has risen from below 1% of GDP to 2% since 2022, with pressure to raise it to 3.5%, while China officially frames its rare-earth export controls as measures to 'prevent Japan from acquiring nuclear weapons' — linking export policy to geopolitical deterrence.
causal🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: highJapanChinarare earthsdefense spending
So what: Japan's defense build-up to potentially 3.5% of GDP is a multi-year structural demand driver for defense electronics, drones, and AI-enabled military systems — a thematic investment for Japan defense contractors.
≈ “Japan has been gradually increasing its defense budget since 2022. It's been kept below 1% of GDP, they raised to 2% and pressured to raise it to 3.5%.” paraphrase
China framed its rare-earth export controls in part as a response to Japan's willingness to get involved militarily in the event of a Taiwan attack, linking semiconductor supply chain leverage to Taiwan deterrence explicitly.
causal🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: highChinaJapanrare earthsTaiwanG7
So what: China's explicit Taiwan-rare earth linkage signals that any Taiwan crisis triggers immediate supply chain weaponization — a risk that is grossly underpriced in Western chip manufacturer valuations.
G7 nations agreed at their recent summit to reduce reliance on Chinese supply chains; China characterized this as 'anti-China circles' and accused Japan's defense minister of hypocrisy for talking of dialogue while endorsing supply chain decoupling.
data_point🗣 Minmin Lowconviction: highG7ChinaJapansupply chain
So what: G7 supply chain decoupling commitment accelerates onshoring/ally-shoring investment globally — long-term positive for Japanese, Korean, and US semiconductor and defense supply chain buildout.
UK Political Instability 2
Andy Burnham, popular Mayor of Greater Manchester, won the UK's Makerfield by-election, opening a potential Labour leadership challenge against incumbent PM Keir Starmer, who has been underperforming in polls and recently lost his defense secretary in a spending dispute.
data_point🗣 Rosalind Mathieson (Bloomberg UK reporter)conviction: highAndy BurnhamKeir StarmerUK LabourUK politics
So what: UK political uncertainty is a headwind for gilts and sterling — especially as Burnham has been associated with 'spending should not be in hock with bond markets,' which markets read as fiscal looseness risk.
Burnham's comment that UK spending 'should not be in hock with bond markets' has already unnerved gilt investors who have PTSD from the Liz Truss era; the gilt market gets jittery at any suggestion of relaxed fiscal rules.
causal🗣 Rosalind Mathiesonconviction: mediumUKAndy Burnhamgiltssterling
So what: Watch the gilt market and sterling as leading indicators of market confidence in UK political succession — a Burnham-driven Labour leadership challenge could reprice UK sovereign risk.
“There's a concern that even if he does try to stick to the rules, his inclination will be to spend more and that's where the market could get unnerved.” ▶ 1:26:14
Fed Rate-Hike Bets Fuel Equities Rally | The Close 6/18/202689 min45 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bloomberg's 'The Close' for June 18, 2026 covers a broad equity rally led by chipmakers and the Russell 2000, driven by a 10%-plus oil slide (Strait of Hormuz relief), a hawkish debut from new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh that the market ultimately spun as inflation-credibility positive, and sustained AI-infrastructure enthusiasm around SpaceX's post-IPO bond plans, the Intel-Apple foundry tie-up, and the looming Anthropic/OpenAI IPO pipeline. Multiple guests warn of extreme valuation stretch (53 S&P 500 stocks at >10x revenues), a supply/demand shift as mega-tech pivots from buybacks to issuers, and a broadening rotation that remains fragile without tech participation.
Kevin Warsh Fed Debut: Hawkish-but-Equity-Friendly · 5Equity Valuation Stretch and Momentum Risk · 4Rotation Trade: Real or Fragile? · 4Oil, Geopolitics, and Real Assets · 3SpaceX IPO Aftermath and the Mega-IPO Supply Shock · 6Semiconductors and AI Infrastructure Buildout · 5Fixed Income, Private Markets, and Portfolio Rebalancing · 4Media/Streaming Consolidation and Content Economics · 4AI Workforce Impact and CIO Role Transformation · 4Gaming, GTA VI, and Take-Two Interactive · 3Sports, Athlete Wealth, and Private Equity in Sports · 3
Kevin Warsh Fed Debut: Hawkish-but-Equity-Friendly 5
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting delivered a hawkish tone, but markets shook off the initial sell-off and rallied ~1% on the interpretation that credible price stability is a prerequisite for sustainable equity gains.
causal🗣 Romaine Bostick / Katie Greifeld (hosts)conviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveFOMCS&P 500
So what: If the market has internalized hawkish Warsh as a 'put' on inflation rather than a ceiling on growth, near-term rate-hike fears may be less bearish for equities than history suggests — watch for sustained re-rating.
The market has priced only 1 to 1.5 Fed rate hikes between October and year-end — not a persistent hiking cycle — meaning the adjustment risk is bounded and manageable in the near term.
data_point🗣 Mimi Duff (Senior Client Advisor)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Front-end rates markets are not yet pricing a full hiking cycle; if Warsh guidance escalates, the short end is the most vulnerable and financial stocks would re-price sharply.
“LOOKING AT THE FRONT END RATE, WHICH IS HIS PREFERRED MECHANISM TO DEPLOY MONETARY POLICY, WE DO HAVE ONE TO 1.5 HIKES PRICED IN BETWEEN OCTOBER AT THE END OF THE YEAR.” ▶ 5:16
Warsh's historically expressed preference for balance-sheet reduction (beyond just rate hikes) adds an incremental tightening channel that the market has not yet fully priced — a task force has been commissioned to study it.
thesis🗣 Mimi Duff (Senior Client Advisor)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: Balance-sheet runoff is an underappreciated risk lever; if Warsh accelerates QT it compounds rate-hike impact on credit spreads and mortgage rates even if the Fed Funds rate moves modestly.
Warsh signaled that some Federal Reserve survey-based economic indicators — particularly labor market participation — never recovered post-COVID, giving him justification to challenge the existing data collection framework and potentially reweight his policy reaction function.
causal🗣 Mimi Duff (Senior Client Advisor)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: If the Fed adopts new or revised economic measures under Warsh, historically calibrated market models for Fed reaction functions could be wrong, creating risk of policy surprises.
Warsh's hawkishness disrupted short-term fixed income pricing the day before the rally — yet risk assets ignored the dislocation, illustrating the market is in 'buy the rumor, buy the news' momentum mode that has broken the normal risky-asset-pricing chain from safe assets.
contrarian🗣 Steve Sosnick (Chief Strategist, Interactive Brokers)conviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveInteractive Brokers
So what: When price action disconnects from the traditional safe-asset anchoring mechanism, momentum unwinds can be violent and fast — tail risk is building.
“YESTERDAY THEY UPENDED THE SHORT-TERM FIXED INCOME MARKET WITH WARSH'S COMMENTS. ONE OF THE NORMAL THINGS THAT HAPPENS IS IF YOU CAN'T PRICE SHORT-TERM DEBT OR SAFE ASSETS, HOW DO YOU PRICE RISKY ASSETS? THAT GOT THROWN OUT THE WINDOW, TOO.” ▶ 37:10
Equity Valuation Stretch and Momentum Risk 4
Approximately 53 S&P 500 stocks — more than 10% by count and 35–37% by index weight — now trade at over 10x revenues, pushing valuation analysis beyond traditional P/E metrics into territory last seen at the dot-com peak.
data_point🗣 Steve Sosnick (Chief Strategist, Interactive Brokers)conviction: highS&P 500Interactive Brokers
So what: At 10x+ revenue multiples, future earnings must essentially all flow to shareholders for a decade to justify price — extreme downside if any macro shock hits; short vol or long put strategies on high-multiple names have asymmetric payoff.
≈ “WE ARE IN A SITUATION WHERE...I THINK IT WAS 53 STOCKS, MORE THAN 10% OF THE S&P 500 I NUMBER AND I THINK IT WAS 35 OR 37% BY WEIGHT. AND THAT IS A PROBLEM.” paraphrase ▶ 38:16
Pure momentum-driven markets treat fundamentals as secondary — this is structurally fragile because when the catalyst shifts (e.g., a tech CEO signals flat AI capex), the correction could be severe given how much is priced into perpetually expanding AI investment.
thesis🗣 Steve Sosnick (Chief Strategist, Interactive Brokers)conviction: highS&P 500NasdaqInteractive Brokers
So what: The single biggest latent risk in this market is a major cloud/AI spender signaling capex stabilization rather than growth — watch Alphabet, Microsoft, Meta capex guidance as leading indicators.
“IF HE WAKES UP AND SAYS I'M NOT GOING TO INCREASE SPENDING BUT KEEP IT STABLE, IMAGINE THE REPERCUSSIONS OF THAT.” ▶ 43:39
Every dip is being bought ('no good dip gets left unbought'), making it difficult for shorts to press and sustaining momentum — but this dynamic also means broader investor positioning is crowded long.
framework🗣 Steve Sosnick (Chief Strategist, Interactive Brokers)conviction: highS&P 500Nasdaq
So what: Crowded long positioning is a contrarian short signal; a single sharp catalyst could trigger forced de-risking by a large cohort of momentum followers simultaneously.
“IT LOOKS LIKE NO GOOD DIP GETS LEFT ON BOUGHT, THAT'S REALLY THE WAY I THINK ABOUT IT.” ▶ 36:50
The S&P 500's top 10 names now account for 30% of total market cap, and U.S. stocks represent over 60% of global equity market cap — concentration risk is at historically unusual levels.
data_point🗣 George Gatch (CEO, J.P. Morgan Asset Management)conviction: highS&P 500J.P. Morgan Asset Management
So what: Global index investors are structurally overweight U.S. mega-cap tech; any mean-reversion event would be amplified by passive fund flows unwinding — ex-U.S. and equal-weight vehicles are tactical hedges.
≈ “YOU LOOK AT THE S&P 500. THE TOP 10 NAMES REPRESENT 30% OF THE TOTAL MARKET. THE U.S. STOCK MARKET NOW REPRESENTS OVER 60% OF THE GLOBAL MARKETS.” paraphrase
Rotation Trade: Real or Fragile? 4
The Russell 2000 finished up 2.1% and hit a record high on June 18, outperforming the S&P 500 (+1%) and Nasdaq (+1.9%) over a six-month period as the equal-weight S&P 500 continues to beat the cap-weighted index — indicating broadening is real and durable so far.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick / Katie Greifeld (hosts)conviction: highRussell 2000S&P 500NasdaqMagnificent 7
So what: Small-cap and equal-weight ETFs (IWM, RSP) are capturing alpha vs. cap-weighted exposure — consider trimming concentrated Mag-7 positions in favor of broader exposure while the rotation persists.
The rotation out of Mag-7 into small/mid-caps and international equities has more legs, particularly as non-tech companies absorb AI efficiency tools and expand margins — but tech still 'drives the bus' and any sustained tech downturn cannot be offset by rotation alone.
thesis🗣 Mimi Duff (Senior Client Advisor)conviction: mediumMagnificent 7Russell 2000S&P 500
So what: Rotation is a meaningful tactical trade but not a structural replacement for tech leadership; pairs trade (long equal-weight/small-cap vs. short Mag-7) works only in a narrow scenario without an outright tech selloff.
≈ “WE THINK THE ROTATION OUT OF THE MAG 7 HAS MORE LEGS. ESPECIALLY AS SOME OF THESE OTHER BENEFICIARIES ARE ABLE TO TAKE IN THE AI TRADE AND BECOME MORE EFFICIENT.” paraphrase
The broadening narrative is complicated by today's session: industrials outperformed on Gulf resolution optimism, but tech and semis dominated — suggesting the rotation remains 'underneath the surface' rather than a clean leadership handoff.
contrarian🗣 Steve Sosnick (Chief Strategist, Interactive Brokers)conviction: mediumS&P 500Philadelphia Semiconductor IndexIndustrials
So what: Do not over-read single-day rotation data — true broadening requires tech to step back, not merely for other sectors to move in parallel while tech leads.
“THE ROTATION IS SORT OF UNDERNEATH THE SURFACE BUT IF TECH DOESN'T KEEP PERFORMING, THEY ARE DRIVING THE BUS.” ▶ 40:36
One strategist is raising equity allocation to 55% with a tilt toward industrials, U.S. consumer cyclicals, and U.S. and European banks as 'resilient economic growth combined with higher inflation' is a positive environment for equities that central banks cannot easily kill.
thesis🗣 Bloomberg anchor (citing strategist)conviction: mediumIndustrialsConsumer CyclicalsEuropean Banks
So what: Higher-for-longer inflation + resilient growth is a classic stagflation-lite regime where real assets, banks with repriced loan books, and industrials tend to beat pure-growth tech on a risk-adjusted basis.
Oil, Geopolitics, and Real Assets 3
Oil futures fell more than 10% on the session on Strait of Hormuz relief signals tied to U.S.-Iran diplomacy — Brent falling back below $80/barrel removed a key inflation-fear overhang and was the proximate catalyst for the equity rally.
causal🗣 Romaine Bostick (host)conviction: highBrent CrudeStrait of HormuzIran
So what: A continued resolution in the Gulf is directly deflationary and removes the biggest near-term upside risk to CPI, potentially giving Warsh cover to pause hikes — oil price is the key macro variable to monitor.
Real asset buckets (uranium, gold, energy stocks) are up 15–20% this year and continue to serve as inflation hedges — even if oil falls further, the non-oil components provide diversification against geopolitical instability.
thesis🗣 Mimi Duff (Senior Client Advisor)conviction: mediumUraniumGoldEnergy
So what: Real asset allocations are providing genuine portfolio protection in 2026 — uranium specifically is a less-crowded inflation hedge that benefits from AI data center power demand, offering a double tailwind.
“THAT REAL ASSET BUCKET HELPS US HOLD ONTO SOME OF THESE OTHER ASSETS WHEN WE HAVE PERSISTENT INFLATION. THAT BUCKET IS UP 15% TO 20%.” ▶ 6:34
Energy was the worst-performing S&P 500 sector on June 18 (down ~1.7%) even as other sectors rallied — the oil price drop is a headwind for energy equities that partially offsets the broader market tailwind.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeld (host)conviction: highEnergy SectorS&P 500
So what: Long energy vs. short S&P 500 as a geopolitical premium trade has unwound sharply — reassess energy equity positions if Hormuz risk premium continues to compress.
SpaceX IPO Aftermath and the Mega-IPO Supply Shock 6
SpaceX reported current revenues of $16–18 billion, with the bulk coming from Starlink network provision rather than rocket launches or AI services — making it primarily an infrastructure/connectivity company, not a space company in the traditional sense.
data_point🗣 Principal Venture Partners founder (unnamed)conviction: highSpaceXStarlink
So what: Valuing SpaceX as a space company significantly understates its revenue quality — Starlink is a recurring-revenue broadband/network infrastructure business, which commands higher multiples than launch economics.
“THE BULK OF IT COMES FROM PROVISION OF STARLINK, THE NETWORK. IT IS THE KIND OF INFRASTRUCTURE COMPANY THAT PROVIDES A NETWORK TO OTHER COMPANIES AND CONSUMERS.” ▶ 11:52
SpaceX has a pipeline data-center deal with Google Gemini and Anthropic that could more than double revenues in coming years by providing compute and data-center access to AI model companies — transforming it into a critical AI infrastructure layer.
prediction🗣 Principal Venture Partners founder (unnamed)conviction: mediumSpaceXGoogleGeminiAnthropicStarlink
So what: If the Gemini/Anthropic data-center contracts materialize, SpaceX's AI infrastructure revenue could rival or exceed Starlink — this is the upside scenario that justifies the $2.3 trillion market cap and supports the buy case at current levels.
“WHAT IS IN THE PIPELINE IS THE DATA CENTER DEAL WITH GOOGLE GEMINI AND ANTHROPIC. IT IS GOING TO MORE THAN DOUBLE THE REVENUE IN THE COMING YEARS.” ▶ 12:11
SpaceX's stock was initiated at Buy by at least one sell-side firm with a $410 price target by end of 2027, citing the suburban broadband opportunity via Starlink — shares currently trading at ~$180, implying ~130% upside in the bull case.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick (host, citing analyst note)conviction: mediumSpaceXStarlink
So what: A $410 target from a fresh initiating analyst signals aggressive upside assumptions are already in the market — the target may embed the Starlink broadband opportunity but not the AI infrastructure optionality, creating room for further upside revisions.
SpaceX is preparing a bond sale of at least $20 billion immediately after its record IPO, with Moody's rating it BAA1 (investment grade, equivalent to BBB+) — confirming access to investment-grade debt markets for large-scale capex.
data_point🗣 Katie Greifeld (host)conviction: highSpaceXMoody's
So what: An investment-grade rating at scale means SpaceX can fund Starlink expansion and AI data-center buildout at low cost of debt; this is a competitive moat — watch for bond spread as a real-time credit sentiment indicator on SpaceX's business risk.
“SPACEX REPORTEDLY IS PREPARING FOR A BOND SALE OF AT LEAST $20 BILLION...WE JUST GOT THIS HEADLINE PRICING FROM MOODY'S THAT THEY'RE GOING TO RATE SPACEX AS BAA1.” ▶ 1:00:03
The pending mega-IPO wave (SpaceX already public, Anthropic and OpenAI rumored at close to $1 trillion each) will meaningfully shift equity supply/demand dynamics — some of the biggest historical buyback programs (Alphabet, potentially Meta) are transitioning to capital-raising mode as AI capex requirements grow.
causal🗣 Steve Sosnick (Interactive Brokers) / George Gatch (JPMorgan AM)conviction: highSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIAlphabetMetaS&P 500
So what: The shift from buyback support to new issuance is a material headwind for equity prices independent of earnings — factor in reduced buyback flow when modeling near-term index targets.
≈ “SOME OF THE BIGGEST BUYBACK USERS ARE NOW ISSUERS. TAKE A LOOK AT ALPHABET, NOW MAYBE META MIGHT JOIN THE PARTY. THEN THROW ON TOP SPACEX, ANTHROPIC, OPENAI.” paraphrase
SPACs have returned to market — six IPOs priced on the Nasdaq on June 18 alone — which historically signals late-cycle market euphoria and excess supply of speculative vehicles.
contrarian🗣 Steve Sosnick (Interactive Brokers)conviction: mediumNasdaqSPACs
So what: SPAC revival is a contrarian warning signal; historically, SPAC peaks correlate with frothy market tops — use as a sentiment indicator to reduce risk exposure at the margin.
“I BELIEVE SPACS ARE BACK. DRAW WHATEVER CONCLUSIONS YOU WANT ABOUT SPACS BEING BACK.” ▶ 42:22
Semiconductors and AI Infrastructure Buildout 5
The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index surged 6.5–6.7% on June 18 to a record high, with all 30 members in the green, led by Micron and Intel (+11%) — this was the primary driver of the Nasdaq's outperformance.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick / Katie Greifeld (hosts)conviction: highPhiladelphia Semiconductor IndexMicronIntelNasdaq
So what: Semis are the cleanest AI infrastructure play — SOX at record highs means markets are pricing continued massive AI capex; any guidance cut from a hyperscaler immediately cascades into semiconductor stocks.
Intel's turnaround is being led by its unique U.S.-based semiconductor manufacturing capability — Apple and other major players are seeking to diversify beyond TSMC, and Intel is the only credible American foundry alternative.
thesis🗣 Mandeep Singh (Bloomberg Intelligence)conviction: mediumIntelAppleTSMCBloomberg Intelligence
So what: Intel's foundry business is a geopolitical and supply-chain-driven option that the market may be underpricing relative to its AI infrastructure significance — watch for more Apple/hyperscaler foundry commitments as a catalyst.
“IT IS LED BY SOMETHING THAT NO ONE ELSE HAS IN AMERICA, THE ABILITY TO MANUFACTURE SEMICONDUCTOR CHIPS. WHETHER IT IS APPLE OR ONE OF THE OTHER PLAYERS, IT SEEMS LIKE EVERYONE WANTS TO DIVERSIFY BEYOND TSMC.” ▶ 32:53
Global AI infrastructure spend is projected at $4–5 trillion through 2030, creating semiconductor demand that will likely outpace even TSMC's capacity — underscoring why multiple domestic foundry investments are occurring simultaneously.
data_point🗣 Mandeep Singh (Bloomberg Intelligence)conviction: mediumTSMCIntelNVIDIABloomberg Intelligence
So what: At $4–5T in projected AI spend over 5 years, even a modest share of the semiconductor content creates multi-year revenue visibility for chip equipment, EDA software, and advanced packaging — these are less-crowded exposure points vs. fabless NVIDIA.
“WE ARE TALKING ABOUT $4 TRILLION TO $5 TRILLION IN SPEND THROUGH 2030.” ▶ 33:27
Amazon has quantified ~$20 billion in chip consumption and is developing proprietary GPU alternatives to NVIDIA, and Google's TPU now has a backlog — creating a long-term competitive risk to NVIDIA's near-monopoly, though the market is large enough for co-existence in the near term.
causal🗣 Mandeep Singh (Bloomberg Intelligence)conviction: mediumAmazonGoogleNVIDIABloomberg Intelligence
So what: Cloud hyperscaler custom silicon is a 3–5 year structural headwind to NVIDIA's share of AI training/inference — at current valuations, this risk is underpriced; watch AWS and Google capex disclosures for internal silicon deployment metrics.
≈ “AMAZON DID QUANTIFY IT TO BE AROUND $20 BILLION, WITH ALL THE CHIPS THAT ARE BEING CONSUMED. GOOGLE GPU NOW HAS A BACKLOG, SO IT IS A RISK.” paraphrase
Accenture fell 18% on June 18 amid existential fears that AI is disrupting the traditional IT consulting and software services model — a signal that AI's deflationary impact on services is accelerating faster than consensus expected.
causal🗣 Katie Greifeld (host)conviction: highAccentureAI
So what: Accenture's selloff is a leading indicator for the broader IT services and consulting sector (IBM, Infosys, Wipro, Cognizant) — underweight or short legacy IT services as AI automates their core labor arbitrage model.
Fixed Income, Private Markets, and Portfolio Rebalancing 4
The 10-year U.S. Treasury now yields 4.44%, investment-grade aggregate yields ~4.5%, with some strategies yielding ~5.5% and aggressive short-duration credit strategies at ~6% — making fixed income genuinely competitive with S&P 500's ~1% dividend yield for the first time in years.
data_point🗣 George Gatch (CEO, J.P. Morgan Asset Management)conviction: highU.S. TreasuryJ.P. Morgan Asset ManagementS&P 500
So what: With credit yielding 4.5–6% vs. equity dividend yield of 1%, fixed income offers compelling risk-adjusted income — a tactical shift from equities to bonds is mathematically justified for income-oriented investors, especially at elevated equity valuations.
“YIELDS HAVE REALLY RESET TO HISTORICALLY ATTRACTIVE LEVELS, 4.44 FOR THE 10 YEAR U.S. TREASURY...I THINK FIXED INCOME YIELDS NOW HAVE ENOUGH YIELD TO INSULATE YOURSELF FROM POTENTIAL UPTICKS IN INFLATION.” ▶ 51:28
J.P. Morgan Asset Management is raising ~$7 billion for a new private credit fund targeting institutional investors, seeing opportunity in liquidity dislocations from wealth-management funds that over-allocated to private credit and now face redemption pressure.
data_point🗣 George Gatch (CEO, J.P. Morgan Asset Management)conviction: highJ.P. Morgan Asset ManagementPrivate Credit
So what: Institutional capital is moving to pick up distressed private credit positions from wealth-channel funds facing redemptions — this creates a buying opportunity in private credit secondary markets with institutional-quality pricing.
JPMorgan Asset Management explicitly declined to pursue inorganic M&A (specifically rebutting Dimensional Fund Advisors speculation), preferring organic growth — it has scaled to $380B in ETFs with 170 funds including active ETFs as its competitive differentiation.
data_point🗣 George Gatch (CEO, J.P. Morgan Asset Management)conviction: highJ.P. Morgan Asset ManagementDimensional Fund Advisors
So what: JPM AM is signaling it will compete on organic active ETF innovation rather than buying scale — Dimensional's independence is therefore preserved but the active ETF arms race is intensifying, benefiting investors through product proliferation.
International and emerging-market equities are entering their second consecutive year of outperforming U.S. equities, driven by both valuation and an improving earnings cycle — fund flows into international categories are rising and represent a multi-year reallocation trend.
thesis🗣 George Gatch (CEO, J.P. Morgan Asset Management)conviction: mediumEmerging MarketsInternational EquitiesJ.P. Morgan Asset Management
So what: Two years of consistent EM/international outperformance with broadening fund flows is a durable trend, not just a mean-reversion blip — increase non-U.S. allocation, especially in AI-related international tech and European industrials.
“WE ARE INTO THE SECOND YEAR WHERE INTERNATIONAL IT'S NOT JUST THE VALUATION, IT'S ALSO AN EARNINGS OPPORTUNITY.” ▶ 57:00
Media/Streaming Consolidation and Content Economics 4
Fox's $22 billion acquisition of Roku is primarily an ad-supported streaming bet — Roku functions as an operating system for connected TVs and is positioned at the center of targeted ad delivery, which Fox believes will be the dominant business model in streaming.
thesis🗣 Kevin Mayer (Co-CEO, Candle Media; former Disney executive)conviction: highFoxRokuNetflixHuluCandle Media
So what: The Fox-Roku deal validates ad-supported streaming (AVOD) as the monetization winner over pure subscription — investors should overweight ad-tech and AVOD-exposed names (The Trade Desk, Magnite) and underweight pure-sub models lacking scale.
“ROKU IS A GREAT EXAMPLE OF IT. IT IS AN OPERATING SYSTEM FOR TELEVISION. IT IS RIGHT IN THE CENTER OF DELIVERING TARGETED COUR ADS TO VIEWERS.” ▶ 1:16:22
YouTube dominates time-spent on connected TVs among streaming content — surpassing Netflix in that metric — and its user-generated/creator content is colliding with longform Hollywood storytelling, as evidenced by a YouTube-originated property ('Back Rooms') beating a major studio film ('The Mandalorian') at the box office.
contrarian🗣 Kevin Mayer (Co-CEO, Candle Media)conviction: highYouTubeNetflixDisneyTikTokCandle Media
So what: YouTube/Alphabet is the most underappreciated streaming winner — its ad-supported model, creator ecosystem, and connected-TV penetration make it structurally dominant in a way that is underrepresented in media sector narratives; long Alphabet as a streaming play.
“IF YOU LOOK AT THE TIME SPENT ON CONNECTED TV'S WITH STREAMING CONTENT ON YOUR TELEVISION, YOUTUBE DOMINATES THAT SPACE.” ▶ 1:17:58
Netflix is feeling strategic pressure despite being the longform streaming leader — it bid for both Warner Bros. Discovery and Roku (per reports), and is also eyeing Lionsgate, suggesting it senses incompleteness in its asset mix and is searching for new growth avenues beyond pure sub growth.
thesis🗣 Kevin Mayer (Co-CEO, Candle Media)conviction: mediumNetflixWarner Bros. DiscoveryRokuLionsgate
So what: Netflix's M&A interest reveals U.S. subscriber saturation and a need for advertising, gaming, and content diversification — factor in acquisition risk premium and potential dilution when modeling Netflix; Lionsgate becomes a realistic takeout candidate.
≈ “THEY ARE CLEARLY FEELING INCOMPLETE IN SOME WAY AND EVERYONE IS. NO COMPANY HAS THE PERFECT ASSET MIX.” paraphrase
Disney's strongest strategic priority in response to the Fox-Roku deal and YouTube's ascendance is to bridge from longform content into short-form and vertical video — failing to do so leaves it exposed to cultural relevance loss even with its unmatched brand portfolio.
thesis🗣 Kevin Mayer (Co-CEO, Candle Media)conviction: mediumDisneyFoxYouTubeRoku
So what: Disney's moat is in brands and franchises, not distribution — short-form video is the distribution layer eroding its competitive position; watch for Disney acquisitions in creator platforms or short-form video as a strategic necessity.
“I HAVE TO BE MORE INVOLVED IN WHAT WE TALKED ABOUT, WE HAVE TO BEAT DISNEY ON SHORT FORM AND VERTICAL VIDEO.” ▶ 1:23:03
AI Workforce Impact and CIO Role Transformation 4
Tech workers who rarely use AI tools are three times more likely to lose their jobs than those who regularly use them — the AI divide is extending beyond Silicon Valley into all industries.
data_point🗣 Romaine Bostick (host, citing new data)conviction: highAITech Workers
So what: Human capital risk is bifurcating sharply by AI adoption — companies with high AI tool penetration in their workforce will see faster productivity gains and cost reduction, making AI adoption rate a key underwriting variable for equity analysis.
The CIO role has evolved from back-office IT manager to strategic glue of the enterprise — the primary challenge is no longer technology implementation (which is now easy) but uniting people, building culture, and influencing without authority across business functions.
framework🗣 Julie Averill (former CIO, Lululemon; author 'Chief Impact Officer')conviction: highLululemonAI
So what: Companies that appoint CIOs with strong organizational influence skills (not just technical skills) will execute AI transformation faster — this is a talent differentiator that investors can probe in earnings calls.
≈ “TECHNOLOGY IS NO LONGER THE HURDLE. TECHNOLOGY IS EASY TODAY. WHAT'S HARD IS UNITING PEOPLE, BUILDING CULTURE, INFLUENCING WITHOUT AUTHORITY.” paraphrase
Many companies are deploying AI-powered customer service chatbots under investor pressure without adequate quality — bad chatbot experiences drive immediate customer churn to competitors, meaning the AI FOMO rush is creating reputational risk.
causal🗣 Julie Averill (former CIO, Lululemon)conviction: highLululemonAIRetail
So what: Retail and consumer companies rushing low-quality AI deployments face customer attrition — short-term AI investment optics vs. long-term brand damage is a real trade-off; quality of AI execution (not just adoption) is the metric that matters.
“THERE IS STILL A DESIRE TO BE ABLE TO FILL YOUR BOARD AND YOUR INVESTORS THAT YOU ARE AN A.I. COMPANY BUT...YOU NEED TO DIG A LITTLE BIT.” ▶ 1:13:07
The real value of AI in retail is in supply chain and inventory optimization — getting the right product in the right place at the right time using AI-driven demand forecasting — and retailers with this capability are outperforming peers.
thesis🗣 Julie Averill (former CIO, Lululemon)conviction: highLululemonRetailAI
So what: Inventory AI is a tangible, measurable profit driver (reduced markdowns, higher sell-through) vs. customer-facing AI which is visible but often unprofitable — overweight retailers with advanced inventory AI; probe this in due diligence.
“THOSE RETAILERS THAT HAVE THE DATA AND ARE CREATING WAYS TO GET THE RIGHT PRODUCT WHERE THE CUSTOMER WANTS IT, THEY ARE EXCELLING.” ▶ 1:11:59
Gaming, GTA VI, and Take-Two Interactive 3
GTA VI launch probability is now above 90% per Wedbush analyst Alicia Reese, with pre-orders opening the following week — Take-Two carries an Outperform rating and $300 price target, with shares up 5% on the pre-order date announcement.
data_point🗣 Alicia Reese (SVP Equity Research, Wedbush Securities)conviction: highTake-Two InteractiveGTA VIWedbush Securities

GTA VI base price will likely launch at $70, with premium packages potentially at $100 — the key investor catalyst is price-point announcement, not just the launch date, as higher-than-expected pricing could re-rate Take-Two.
prediction🗣 Alicia Reese (SVP Equity Research, Wedbush Securities)conviction: mediumTake-Two InteractiveGTA VI

≈ “A $70 PRICE POINT WILL GET EVERYONE EXCITED. $100 PRICE POINT WOULD GET INVESTORS EXCITED.” paraphrase
GTA VI is a structural shift from prior GTA cycles — with over 80% recurring revenue from live services, any post-launch pullback in Take-Two shares would be an entry point rather than a red flag.
contrarian🗣 Alicia Reese (SVP Equity Research, Wedbush Securities)conviction: highTake-Two InteractiveGTA VI
So what: GTA's transformation to a live-service model (like GTA Online) means the monetization tail is multi-year, not a one-time launch — model it like a live-service subscription rather than a traditional boxed game; any post-launch dip is a buying opportunity.
“THIS IS NOT YOUR FATHER'S GTA CYCLE. ANY STOCK SELLERS AFTER THE LAUNCH WOULD BE LEAVING MONEY ON THE TABLE.” ▶ 20:07
Sports, Athlete Wealth, and Private Equity in Sports 3
Professional athletes have shifted from passive wealth preservation to active investors — seeking equity stakes in businesses, team ownership, media companies, and technology ventures, with wealth managers now connecting athletes to entrepreneurs and tech founders.
causal🗣 Nicole Pullen Ross (Head of Sports & Entertainment, Goldman Sachs)conviction: highGoldman SachsProfessional Athletes
So what: Athletes are becoming a meaningful LP/angel investor class — financial advisors and VC funds that cultivate athlete capital are accessing a high-profile, brand-amplifying pool of patient capital that also provides marketing value.
Private equity is now investing not only in sports teams but in the infrastructure around them — stadiums, arenas, and surrounding real estate — with institutional capital active across all major U.S. leagues.
thesis🗣 Nicole Pullen Ross (Head of Sports & Entertainment, Goldman Sachs)conviction: highGoldman SachsNFLPrivate EquitySports Teams
So what: Sports real estate adjacencies (stadium districts, mixed-use developments) are a less-crowded entry point vs. headline team valuations — target PE funds focused on sports infrastructure as a differentiated real asset with guaranteed anchor tenants.

Women's sports investment is characterized as 'startup capital' in terms of risk/return profile versus mature league investments like the NFL, which is driven by pure supply/demand scarcity.
framework🗣 Nicole Pullen Ross (Head of Sports & Entertainment, Goldman Sachs)conviction: mediumGoldman SachsNFLWomen's Sports

“WOMEN'S SPORTS...FEELS MUCH MORE LIKE A SHIFT TOWARDS START UP CAPITAL, OR MORE MATURE INVESTMENTS LIKE THE NFL.” ▶ 30:37
Anthropic's Co-Founder and Top Economist on Doing Research at the AI Frontier | Odd Lots71 min40 insights2026-06-19
▶ Watch on YouTube
