How adjustment costs are allocated between surplus and deficit countries is the central strategic question: powerful deficit countries (e.g., 1930s US) force costs onto surplus countries; weak deficit countries (Latin America 1970s, Asia 1990s, European periphery 2000s) absorb most costs themselves.
framework🗣 Michael Pettisconviction: highUSChinaEULatin America
So what: The outcome of the US-China imbalance adjustment depends crucially on relative bargaining power; US tariffs and capital-inflow taxes are an attempt to shift from 'weak deficit country' to 'powerful deficit country' mode and force adjustment costs onto China.
“the real game is to figure out how those adjustment costs are going to be allocated.” ▶ 1:15:49
The 1930s is the best historical model for the current global imbalance configuration: high debt levels in both deficit and surplus countries, powerful deficit country (US), breakdown of global trade architecture — and the 1930s resolved with most adjustment costs absorbed by surplus countries.
framework🗣 Michael Pettisconviction: mediumUSChina1930s
So what: If the 1930s template holds, China bears a disproportionate share of the adjustment pain; long-duration Chinese asset exposure (equities, credit, property) should be sized with this in mind.
“I think the 1930s is really the best model to look at this because they share a lot of characteristics.” ▶ 1:14:59
Investment-led growth creates necessary debt accumulation as a buffer to postpone painful rebalancing, but the longer the postponement, the higher the ultimate debt load and the worse the eventual adjustment — China's accelerating debt trajectory is exactly this dynamic.
causal🗣 Michael Pettisconviction: highChinaJapan
So what: Every year China avoids rebalancing means higher debt and a deeper eventual correction; the non-linear risk in Chinese credit is growing, not stable.
“China has been stuck, like Japan, with postponing the resolution in exchange for rapidly rising debt.” ▶ 1:19:26
Keynes's original Bretton Woods proposal — rejected by the US — called for automatic penalties on persistent surplus AND deficit countries, and for a maximum permissible imbalance with forced adjustment mechanisms; Pettis argues this is the only viable long-term solution to prevent recurring crises.
policy🗣 Michael Pettisconviction: highJohn Maynard KeynesBretton WoodsIMF
So what: The debate about a new global monetary architecture (Mar-a-Lago Accord, new Bretton Woods) has serious intellectual foundations; investors should take seriously the possibility of a multilateral trade-balance enforcement regime emerging from the current crisis.
“Keynes said, 'Look, let's have a system where you as an individual sovereign country, you can follow any economic policy you like. What you cannot do is externalize the costs of your domestic policies to your trade partners.'” ▶ 47:31

Forward Guidance · Blockworks 1 episode(s) · 42 insights

A New Era Is Beginning In Markets | Weekly Roundup64 min42 insights2026-06-19
▶ Watch on YouTube
On Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting, the hosts argue we have hit peak Fed hawkishness: the dot plot's two-hike-by-2027 pricing is contradicted by oil down 30% from the prior meeting, collapsing one-year inflation swaps, and a record short in SOFR futures that is primed to squeeze. Simultaneously, Warsh's abolition of forward guidance is expected to secularly raise rate volatility while flattening the yield curve—a move the panel reads as a coordinated Warsh/Bessent effort to lower long-end yields, enable the AI capex buildout bond wave, and reopen US housing. Bitcoin/MSTR get a bearish structural critique as AI capex competes for capital.
Peak Hawkishness & FOMC Positioning Squeeze · 8Warsh Fed Regime Change: End of Forward Guidance, Higher Rate Volatility · 6Macro Transmission: Dollar, Yen, Yield Curve Flattening & Carry Trade Risk · 6AI Capex Cycle: Hyperscalers vs. Bottleneck Stocks & Bond Issuance Phase · 5MicroStrategy / Bitcoin Structural Stress · 5Bitcoin vs. AI Capital Competition — Structural Regime Shift · 5AI Ownership Politics, K-Shaped Economy & National Ownership Risk · 3Market Structure: Summer Seasonality, Gold Contrarian Setup & Vol Dynamics · 4
Peak Hawkishness & FOMC Positioning Squeeze 8
The market is pricing two Fed rate hikes by mid-2027 and has built a record short position in SOFR futures, but the hosts believe this is the capitulatory moment of peak hawkishness and that no hikes will materialize this year.
contrarian🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highFOMCSOFRFed
So what: Record SOFR shorts are a classic mean-reversion setup; if data softens, a violent short squeeze in front-end rates is the trade.
“Man, this hurts, but like I know there's no way in hell that we're hiking at the end of this year.” ▶ 0:36
The dot plot's two-hike signal is internally inconsistent: multiple governors penciled in hikes in 2026 followed by cuts within 12 months—a pattern that historically signals reactive policy driven by transient oil-price moves, not a genuine tightening cycle.
framework🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highFOMCdot plotFed
So what: A dot plot with hike-then-immediately-cut pattern reveals the committee is reacting to a one-time tariff/oil shock, not entrenched inflation—raises conviction that the hikes won't happen.
Oil is down 30% from the Fed's last two dot-plot meetings where it pivoted hawkish; one-year inflation swaps have already collapsed back to pre-Operation Epic Fury levels, making the hawkish dots backward-looking.
data_point🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highoilinflation swapsFOMCOperation Epic Fury
So what: CPI headline will likely print sharply lower next month given spot oil; this undercuts the rationale for the hawkish dots and reinforces the peak-hawkishness trade in short rates.
The 2-year yield spiked to near 4.20% and then immediately backed off several basis points on the day after the FOMC, suggesting the market had already pre-priced the hawkish outcome and quickly reverted once traders asked whether hikes would actually occur.
data_point🗣 Felix (host)conviction: medium2-year TreasuryFOMC
So what: The failed breakout in the 2-year is a technical signal that the market accepted and then rejected peak hawkishness in real time—a potential entry for short-duration longs.
Non-voting Fed regional presidents likely submitted the most extreme hawkish dots as a show of independence or resentment toward Warsh—adding noise but little signal to the dot plot's forward rate path.
causal🗣 Felix (host)conviction: mediumFOMCdot plotKevin Warsh
So what: Strip the non-voting presidents' dots and the underlying Fed view is materially less hawkish than the headline median implies.
Blackstone COO noted shelter costs—the largest CPI component—are running in the 'low ones' in private data versus 'low threes' in government data, and wages have decelerated from ~5% to ~3%; both trends should significantly ease the post-war inflation picture.
data_point🗣 Tyler (host, quoting Blackstone COO)conviction: highBlackstoneCPIshelter costswages
So what: Private-market shelter data leads BLS by ~12 months; if Blackstone is right, official CPI will converge sharply lower and erase the inflation scare justifying hawkish dots.
“If you look at shelter costs which are the biggest components of CPI I would say they're running in the low ones versus government data in the low threes.” ▶ 11:05
A government-shutdown-related zero mark-up in shelter/OER back in October created a base-effect spike in subsequent shelter CPI readings; that effect has now fully reverted, so shelter inflation will mechanically pull lower.
causal🗣 Felix (host)conviction: mediumCPIOERshelter inflation
So what: The shelter CPI spike that justified hawkish dots was partly a statistical artifact—another reason to fade peak hawkishness via front-end rate longs.
With the US-Iran ceasefire resolved and tariffs effectively at net zero, all four major CPI components (food, energy, core goods, core services/housing) are turning disinflationary simultaneously—making the case for hikes exceptionally weak.
thesis🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highCPItariffsIranFOMC
So what: A multi-component CPI roll simultaneously has historically been sufficient to shift the Fed; the next July CPI print is the event to watch for confirming the pivot narrative.
Warsh Fed Regime Change: End of Forward Guidance, Higher Rate Volatility 6
Kevin Warsh cut ~80% of the FOMC statement and ended it with 'The committee will deliver price stability'—signaling a deliberate move away from verbose forward guidance toward outcome-based policy with no pre-commitment on rates.
thesis🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highKevin WarshFOMCFederal Reserve
So what: Less forward guidance mechanically removes a key volatility suppressor; rate vol and FX vol should trend structurally higher, disrupting vol-targeting systematic strategies.
“The committee will deliver price stability mic drop that's it.” ▶ 2:48
Forward guidance was a zero-lower-bound innovation by Bernanke to substitute for negative rates and suppress rate volatility; in a world of structurally higher secular inflation, it is no longer the right tool—its removal is a net positive long-term.
framework🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: highFedKevin WarshBen Bernankeforward guidance
So what: The structural shift away from forward guidance implies higher rate vol is a secular, not cyclical, phenomenon—favor rate vol strategies and underweight assets priced for perpetual low vol.
Historical Fed communication word count grew parabolically over 15 years and functioned as a 'volatility stifler'—suppressing market vol and enabling systematic, momentum-driven asset management to thrive; removing it could unwind the systematic asset management bubble.
causal🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: exploratoryFederal Reservesystematic strategies
So what: If vol structurally rises, risk-parity and vol-targeting quant funds face systematic de-grossing; this is a multi-quarter tail risk for the passive/systematic allocation complex.
Warsh's reform agenda includes five task-force items with the balance sheet being the most important; his stated desire is to shrink the balance sheet footprint and reduce duration, using rate policy as the transmission mechanism instead of QE/QT.
thesis🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reservebalance sheetQT
So what: If executed, a smaller Fed balance sheet and less duration suppression would be structurally bearish for mega-cap tech and the AI trade that benefited from suppressed long-end yields.
Warsh's first FOMC is politically motivated in part: the hawkish tone was designed to establish Fed independence from Trump's rate-cut pressure, not primarily to fight inflation—making the hawkishness performative and likely to ease.
thesis🗣 Felix (host)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFOMCTrumpFederal Reserve
So what: Performative hawkishness that is meant to signal independence rather than reflect macro reality is inherently temporary; the fade trade in hawkish pricing is supported by this political-optics framework.
Warsh's first serious test will only come when equities drop 10-15% and inflation is still above target simultaneously—until then, his job is easy because the administration is managing market volatility through other channels.
framework🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserveequities
So what: The real Warsh put level is unknown; investors should not assume the backstop is at current levels—credit widening would be the first stress signal to test actual Fed reaction.
“It's easy to be hawkish when stocks are at all-time highs and everything's rosy.” ▶ 0:30
Macro Transmission: Dollar, Yen, Yield Curve Flattening & Carry Trade Risk 6
The transmission chain from the Warsh hawkish pivot is: dollar rally → FX vol spike → yield vol → credit spread widening → PE multiple compression; the panel is at the early FX-vol stage with the dollar breaking out and credit spreads still historically tight.
framework🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: mediumUSDcredit spreadsPE multiplesyield curve
So what: Credit spread widening is the key early-warning signal—until HY spreads move, the AI capex cycle stays intact; when they blow out, risk assets compress.
The yen is breaking out of a 20-year base going back to near Plaza Accord (1985) levels against the dollar; a sustained yen weakening while dollar strengthens could trigger a reverse carry trade, though falling oil prices (Japan is a major commodity importer) provides a partial offset.
causal🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: mediumJPYUSDcarry tradePlaza Accord
So what: The yen's break from a multi-decade base combined with dollar strength is the setup for a violent carry unwind if a catalyst emerges—a 2024-August-style event risk to watch.
Real rates are tightening global liquidity meaningfully: the 2-year nominal yield now significantly exceeds the 2-year breakeven inflation rate—the opposite of 2021 when bonds paid negative real rates—recalibrating the 60/40 risk-reward decisively in favor of bonds.
framework🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: high2-year Treasurybreakeven inflation60/40 portfolio
So what: Positive real yields fundamentally alter the relative attractiveness of equities vs. bonds; boomers with cash now have a genuine alternative, which could slow the buyback-driven equity flywheel.
The US Treasury issues approximately 85% of debt on the front end of the curve; Bessant's strategy is to flatten the yield curve so he can term out issuance further out without rate spikes—directly analogous to the Soros-vs.-Bank-of-England dynamic, but with Bessant as the manipulator, not the victim.
causal🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: mediumScott BessantUS Treasuryyield curveGeorge Soros
So what: If Bessant succeeds in flattening the curve and extending duration, Treasury issuance risk is re-profiled, reducing rollover risk; any bond shorts are fighting the person who 'broke the Bank of England.'
≈ “You're literally shorting bonds against the guy who broke the Bank of England in the pound.” paraphrase
Warsh and Bessant appear to be coordinating: flattening the long end of the yield curve would simultaneously reduce mortgage rates (housing affordability), lower corporate bond issuance costs for the AI buildout, and reduce the US deficit's interest burden—a tri-fecta policy objective.
thesis🗣 Tyler/Felix (hosts)conviction: exploratoryKevin WarshScott BessantUS Treasurymortgage ratesAI
So what: If the Warsh/Bessant long-end suppression thesis plays out, long-duration bonds and rate-sensitive housing names could be structurally re-rated; the 30-year yield is the primary instrument to watch.
Household cash as a percentage of total financial assets remains unusually elevated despite all-time high equities; this suggests the continued recycling of cash into bonds is keeping long-end vol suppressed even without Fed intervention.
data_point🗣 Tyler (host, citing Scott Rubner/Citadel)conviction: mediumhousehold cashbondsequitiesCitadel
So what: Elevated cash on the sidelines acts as a structural buyer of bonds, providing a headwind to any yield spike; also means equity upside is supported by potential re-allocation if sentiment shifts.
AI Capex Cycle: Hyperscalers vs. Bottleneck Stocks & Bond Issuance Phase 5
JP Morgan data shows data center capex growth decelerating—year-over-year spending growth is dropping from ~80% to ~45% as the absolute spend goes from ~$56-75B to ~$86B—suggesting the initial rate-of-change boom is moderating even as absolute spend remains massive through 2030.
data_point🗣 Tyler (host, citing JP Morgan)conviction: highJP Morgandata centersAIhyperscalers
So what: Hyperscaler stocks are priced for the rate-of-change, not the level; decelerating YoY growth is a headwind to multiple expansion even as the absolute capex number grows to 2030.
Money is rotating out of hyperscalers (MSFT, META, GOOGL, AMZN) and into AI bottleneck/infrastructure stocks; Goldman Sachs hyperscaler index is underperforming its AI beneficiary index.
thesis🗣 Tyler (host, citing Goldman Sachs)conviction: highGoldman SachsMicrosoftMetaGoogleAmazonAI infrastructure
So what: The hyperscaler-to-bottleneck rotation trade has further legs as long as high-yield credit spreads stay tight; the specific entry point—HY spread widening—would signal the end of this rotation.
The AI buildout has entered a bond issuance phase: hyperscalers have exhausted their internal free cash flows and are now turning to equity issuance and corporate bonds—SpaceX was looking at a $20 million bond issuance as an example—with corporate bond pricing driven by long-end yields, not short-term rates.
causal🗣 Felix (host)conviction: highSpaceXAIhyperscalerscorporate bondslong-end yields
So what: The marginal funding cost for the AI buildout is the long-end Treasury yield, not the Fed funds rate—this is why the Warsh/Bessant yield curve flattening agenda aligns with the AI-dominance geopolitical objective.
High yield credit spreads have barely moved despite the Warsh hawkish pivot, meaning the AI capex cycle remains fully funded and intact; the threshold for the bull case to break is a meaningful HY spread widening.
thesis🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highhigh yieldcredit spreadsAI capex
So what: HY spread widening is the single most important signal to monitor; until it cracks, AI infrastructure and bottleneck stocks remain in a 'free money machine' environment for corporate issuers.
“I just don't unless high yield credit spreads blow out I just don't see that stopping.” ▶ 27:55
The administration's implicit policy agenda—ensuring the US wins the AI race against China—means the Warsh/Bessant axis views keeping long-end yields contained (to fund corporate bond issuance for AI) as a quasi-national security objective, not just monetary policy.
thesis🗣 Felix/Tyler (hosts)conviction: exploratoryAIChinaKevin WarshScott Bessantcorporate bonds
So what: If AI-buildout funding is a geopolitical objective, the government has an unusually strong incentive to suppress long-end yields via policy coordination—making 'sell the long end' a trade that fights the implicit sovereign sponsor.
MicroStrategy / Bitcoin Structural Stress 5
MicroStrategy (MSTR) is in a liquidity stress loop: it continues to issue equity to buy Bitcoin even as Bitcoin falls, forcing progressively more dilutive share sales; every STRK/preferred issuance strips value from common equity holders.
thesis🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRBitcoinSTRK
So what: MSTR common is being systematically diluted by preferred/convertible issuances; the stock's chart peaked the day STRK launched—a textbook preferred-equity value trap for common holders.
“The day that stretch launched and started going live was the top in MSTR.” ▶ 50:31
MSTR still trades above its liquidation value (Bitcoin holdings minus all debt and preferred), so Saylor will keep using equity issuance as a release valve; the real stress begins if MSTR trades into the 90s of NAV—closer to liquidation value.
framework🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRBitcoin
So what: The asymmetric distress trade is not today—it is when MSTR trades at or below liquidation value; monitor the premium/discount to NAV as the key trigger level.
Saylor faces a growing preferred dividend burden: required yields are rising toward 12-13%, creating a massive ongoing cash drain for a company with no operating revenue, amplifying the liquidation risk over time.
data_point🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRMichael Saylor
So what: A 12-13% preferred yield on a no-revenue company is structurally unstable; the clock is ticking on when MSTR must either issue massively dilutive equity or sell Bitcoin.
The solution for MSTR is straightforward—raise enough cash to demonstrate coverage of 2-3 years of preferred dividends, interest payments, and upcoming debt maturities—but management refuses to do so, continuing to lever up instead.
thesis🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRMichael Saylor
So what: The resolution is known and priced in as a tail risk; if Saylor capitulates and raises the liquidity buffer, the entire MSTR capital structure—bonds, preferred, common—would bounce violently.
No large Bitcoin whale is willing to buy $5B of Bitcoin while the largest single holder of ~5% of the asset class is visibly distressed—creating an overhang that suppresses Bitcoin's near-term price regardless of fundamentals.
causal🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: mediumMicroStrategyBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: MSTR's distress is a Bitcoin price suppressor because rational whales will not bid up an asset at risk of a forced seller with 5% market share; resolution of the MSTR situation is a prerequisite for the next Bitcoin leg up.
Bitcoin vs. AI Capital Competition — Structural Regime Shift 5
Bitcoin's original investment thesis—a hedge against currency debasement and unproductive centralized economies—is losing relevance in a cycle where genuine productivity growth (AI) and real capital investment have emerged, reducing the opportunity cost argument for a non-yielding store of value.
thesis🗣 Tyler/Felix (hosts)conviction: highBitcoinAIFederal Reserve
So what: Bitcoin is likely in a prolonged underperformance period relative to AI infrastructure assets; re-entry case rebuilds only when AI capex cycle turns or fiat debasement re-accelerates.
For ~15 years post-2008 there was 'TINA' (no alternative to equities/Bitcoin) because the only business models generating returns were ad-tech monopolies doing stock buybacks rather than real investment—the AI buildout has broken TINA by creating genuine capital investment opportunities.
framework🗣 Felix (host)conviction: highBitcoinAIFacebookMetaFed
So what: The end of TINA means Bitcoin, gold, and non-yielding stores of value face secular headwinds as capital finds productive deployment; private markets in biotech, AI infra, and industrial become more attractive.
Bitcoin miners that own significant electricity/data center capacity are flipping to AI compute hosting (centralized economy) because it is more economically rational than mining—potentially creating a margin call catalyst for Saylor before Bitcoin's decentralization thesis reasserts itself.
causal🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: mediumBitcoin minersAI computeMicroStrategy
So what: Bitcoin miner capacity rotation into AI hosting reduces hashrate growth and fundamentally shifts the energy-computation value chain away from Bitcoin in the near term.
Bitcoin's role in portfolios likely reasserts once the AI capex cycle peaks, fiat debasement re-accelerates, or the 'state capitalism AI buildout' creates a backlash—at that point gold and Bitcoin become the logical hedge on the other side of the AI bubble.
prediction🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: exploratoryBitcoingoldAI
So what: Position sizing Bitcoin as a long-dated option on AI-cycle exhaustion or policy error, not as a current-cycle alpha generator.
“Maybe it's on the other side of that as insurance to like the state capitalism AI buildout thing. On the other side, that's when it's time to own it again.” ▶ 53:15
Crypto DeFi created genuine innovations in settlement and payment rails—trade breaks that take days in TradFi are solved in crypto—but the industry lost the battle to the banking cartel and could not convert technical superiority into mainstream adoption.
anecdote🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: mediumDeFicryptobanking
So what: The settlement-rail disruption thesis in crypto remains unresolved; Clarity Act passage and regulatory cleanup are prerequisites for the next adoption wave.
AI Ownership Politics, K-Shaped Economy & National Ownership Risk 3
Both political parties are converging on the idea of government or public ownership stakes in frontier AI companies: Bernie Sanders has proposed legislation for public AI company ownership, while the Trump team's actions (e.g., the 'Fable banning' controversy) are interpreted as maneuvering for ownership stakes in Anthropic and other frontier models.
prediction🗣 Tyler/Felix (hosts)conviction: mediumAnthropicAIBernie SandersTrumpFable
So what: Bipartisan momentum toward government ownership or revenue sharing in AI is a material tail risk for private AI company valuations and IPO optionality; Anthropic, OpenAI, and other frontier labs should be monitored for regulatory/ownership developments.
≈ “This whole song and dance with the fable banning thing is them trying to get to strongarm some sort of ownership of anthropic and the frontier models too.” paraphrase
If AI generates 20-30% of GDP, the only politically viable path to avoiding social unrest is distributing ownership to the public ('Anthropic dividends to the plebs')—in effect offloading AI company equity to taxpayers at high valuations, analogous to Elon dumping bags on the public.
thesis🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: exploratoryAIAnthropicElon Musk
So what: Forced or negotiated public ownership distributions could be a liquidity event for early AI investors and a dilution/valuation-cap event for new investors entering post-IPO.
The K-shaped economy problem is intensifying: asset owners have benefited enormously while non-asset-holders are left behind; the panel sees no market mechanism to solve it and views the political/legislative solutions (Sanders legislation, AI ownership) as early-stage attempts to address it.
thesis🗣 Tyler (host)conviction: mediumK-shaped economyAIequities
So what: Political risk premium on AI assets and wealth taxes is underpriced; the legislative risk to AI company ownership structures is a multi-year overhang that increases as AI GDP contribution grows.
Market Structure: Summer Seasonality, Gold Contrarian Setup & Vol Dynamics 4
Record $8.3 trillion notional in quadruple witching is expiring this week; combined with summer seasonality tailwinds, systematic vol-targeting funds re-leveraging, and the VIX getting smashed after the FOMC—the setup points to a low-vol grind higher into August.
prediction🗣 Tyler (host, citing Scott Rubner/Citadel)conviction: mediumVIXCitadelquadruple witchingsystematic strategies
So what: The summer low-vol grind is the base case until fall; tactically, selling vol into the summer window is the path of least resistance trade.
Gold 6-month put/call skew is near 10-year highs while CTA positioning in gold has collapsed to the first percentile on a one-year lookback—an extreme sentiment reversal from the physical gold line-ups just a few months ago.
contrarian🗣 Tyler (host, citing Lee Coppersmith)conviction: highgoldCTAoptions skew
So what: Extreme bearish skew and near-zero CTA length in gold is a textbook contrarian long setup; the crowded short/put position means any catalyst (dollar weakness, credit stress) could produce a violent squeeze.
≈ “Gold six month put call skews near 10-year highs. CTA position in gold has collapsed toward the first percentile on a one-year look back.” paraphrase
Centralized asset management at massive scale (e.g., $100B funds at Franklin Templeton) structurally produces extreme boom/bust cycles because large managers can only buy assets when they are on sale and sell when every retail investor holds them—perpetuating volatility extremes across all asset classes.
framework🗣 Tyler (host, anecdote from Franklin Templeton)conviction: mediumFranklin Templetonasset management
So what: The increasing concentration of AUM in mega-managers means asset price swings will continue to overshoot in both directions—the gold contrarian setup is a case study of this dynamic.
Volatility extremes are now characteristic of virtually every asset class—equities, crypto, metals, oil—driven by centralized, passive-flow-driven asset management and heavy government intervention; this bubble-like vol structure is likely here to stay.
thesis🗣 Quinn (host)conviction: mediumVIXoilgoldcryptopassive investing
So what: Owning convexity (vol) in multiple asset classes is structurally attractive when government intervention and passive concentration repeatedly create extreme dislocations.

Wealthion 1 episode(s) · 40 insights

Henrik Zeberg: The Final Melt-Up Before Everything Breaks44 min40 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Henrik Zeberg (macro strategist, Swiss Block) argues markets are in a terminal melt-up targeting NASDAQ 33,000–34,000 and a Q3 2026 top, after which a crash combining dot-com-scale equity deflation with GFC-scale credit unwinding will unfold — while the real U.S. economy has already been deteriorating badly, masked by headline data the market is ignoring.
Terminal Melt-Up & Market Top Timing · 6The Combined-Crisis Thesis: Dot-Com + GFC Simultaneously · 5U.S. Labour Market Deterioration Hidden by Headline Data · 5Consumer Balance Sheet & Affordability Stress · 4Fed Policy, Inflation Narrative, and Regime Shift · 5Crypto, Gold & Silver in the Melt-Up and Crash · 6Safe Haven Positioning and Debt Dynamics · 5AI's Labour Impact and Demand Destruction · 4
Terminal Melt-Up & Market Top Timing 6
Zeberg expects at least an additional 15% gain in the NASDAQ from current levels before the cycle top, with a NASDAQ price target of 33,000–34,000 based on Fibonacci master levels and fractal analysis.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highNASDAQ
So what: Implies remaining upside in mega-cap tech and Nasdaq-linked products (QQQ) before positioning defensively; the 33–34k target is a specific level to watch for topping signals.
“We may see another at least 15% in the NASDAQ from from right here. And I'm not even certain that the next pullback we will see will be the one that will actually be the the big big move.” ▶ 0:08
Zeberg calls a market top in Q3 2026, the most specific time-bounded call in the interview, based on negative momentum divergences emerging across major indices on the technical charts.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highS&P 500NASDAQ
So what: Q3 2026 is a hard deadline; investors with long equity exposure should have a risk-reduction plan by late summer 2026.
“I'll probably get chased for this but I'll say that the top will be in Q3 of this year. And that that's being as bold as I can.” ▶ 37:32
The NASDAQ's near-vertical rally since late March 2026 fits a 1997–2000 fractal pattern, suggesting the final parabolic phase is now underway rather than having already peaked.
framework🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highNASDAQS&P 500
So what: The fractal lens argues against being short today; the vertical-move phase historically accelerates before collapsing, making premature shorts expensive.
≈ “The vertical move is what you see into the final phase and you saw that back in 2000... we are from 97 and until 2000 that kind of development we saw there in the NASDAQ and the S&P is very much what we see right now.” paraphrase
Two confirming indicators that a top is NOT yet in: (1) liquidity conditions have not deteriorated to 2000-peak levels; (2) the S&P-to-10-year-bond ratio is still trending up rather than declining 2–3 months ahead of the peak.
framework🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highS&P 50010-Year Treasury
So what: Investors can use these two gauges — liquidity trends and the equity/bond ratio — as leading confirmation signals before rotating defensively.
The final melt-up will be characterized by accelerating capital rotation: first into U.S. large-caps and financials, then broadening into laggards like software, then crypto — each rotation signaling the cycle is aging rather than healthy.
framework🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highS&P 500NASDAQsoftware sectorBitcoinEthereum
So what: Rotation into software and crypto should be read as a late-cycle euphoria signal, not a fundamental re-rating; it marks the window to reduce risk, not add it.
Zeberg was explicitly long ('bulled up') at interview time despite forecasting a recession, illustrating his core framework: a bearish macro view and a bullish market view can coexist until liquidity and technical topping signals align.
framework🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: high
So what: Macro bears who exited equities early have left money on the table; the framework argues for riding the melt-up until 20 specific indicators flip, not on macro fundamental data alone.
≈ “People always point to me and say you say that there is a recession coming... and then it comes to the market and people say but does that mean you also can bearish the market? No, it does not.” paraphrase
The Combined-Crisis Thesis: Dot-Com + GFC Simultaneously 5
Zeberg's central thesis is that the coming crash will be a hybrid: a dot-com-style equity bubble collapse (the 'appetizer') followed by a GFC-style credit/balance-sheet unwind (the 'main course'), making it worse than either crisis alone.
thesis🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highS&P 500NASDAQprivate credit
So what: A two-phase crisis means no safe equity rotation; value stocks that survived 2000 were destroyed in 2008. Investors need to plan for both equity and credit dislocation.
≈ “I think the .com will be the appetizer and then you'll see the 2008-9 as the main course.” paraphrase
Current equity valuations are roughly 2.5x the bubble level seen in 2007 by Zeberg's measures, making today's stock market a much larger bubble than the one that preceded the GFC.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highS&P 500
So what: A larger starting-point overvaluation implies a larger deleveraging; historical mean-reversion math suggests peak-to-trough drawdowns could significantly exceed 2008's ~57%.
≈ “The stock market... it's a bigger bubble much much bigger like like two and a half times the size of what we saw in '07.” paraphrase ▶ 0:31
Private credit markets are flagged as the structural fault line that turns a 2000-style equity crash into a 2008-style credit crisis, analogous to the role subprime/real estate played in the GFC.
causal🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highprivate credit
So what: Monitor private credit stress indicators (spreads, redemption gates, NAV marks) as early warning signals of the second phase of the crisis.
“We could see a huge deflationary factor from the fact that the stock market could implode, which I think it will, which will then cause a private credit situation.” ▶ 35:23
The post-crash credit crunch will develop because inflated corporate valuations serve as collateral; once equities collapse in a recession, the credit underpinning those valuations is pulled and a lending freeze begins.
causal🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highprivate creditS&P 500
So what: Corporate bond holders and leveraged-loan investors face a double whammy: equity collateral devalues while recession hits cash flows simultaneously.
The initial crash will last roughly 6–9 months, followed by a Fed-driven bounce of 4–5 months (repeating the Jan–May 2008 pattern), before the second and more destructive leg down as credit dominoes fall.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumFederal ReserveS&P 500
So what: The Fed-induced bounce will be a trap; selling into that relief rally rather than buying it is the key tactical call for the crisis period.
“There will be a reaction from the Fed... and the market bounced for four or five months in 2008 as well even though we were in a recession and the markets were you know doomed.” ▶ 38:21
U.S. Labour Market Deterioration Hidden by Headline Data 5
The August 2025 non-farm payroll revision erased approximately 1 million previously reported jobs, revealing that full-year 2025 job creation was only 184,000 — compared to ~1.5 million per year considered acceptable.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. Bureau of Labor Statisticsnon-farm payrolls
So what: Headline NFP readings are structurally overstated; investors relying on them to gauge consumer health are making decisions on fictitious data.
“There came you know August the revision in the non-farm payrolls and we saw that there was a 1 million jobs all of a sudden disappearing and the job creation throughout 25 was 184,000.” ▶ 17:36
The last five months of 2025 (after revision) showed an average of minus 9,000 jobs per month; the current underlying pace Zeberg tracks is approximately 75,000–80,000 net new jobs per month — a recessionary rate historically.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highnon-farm payrolls
So what: At 75–80k/month, the jobs market is already printing recession-level numbers that the Fed and markets are ignoring — a catalyst for a sharp policy U-turn.
May 2026 saw a loss of 79,000 full-time jobs; since January 2025, 1.7 million full-time jobs have been lost in the U.S. — masked because the headline NFP counts part-time and full-time jobs together.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. labour market
So what: Full-time job losses at this scale historically precede consumer spending contractions; watch personal consumption and retail sales for confirming weakness.
“What we actually saw in May was that there was a loss of 79,000 full-time jobs in that same month. And since January of 25, we have seen a loss of 1.7 million jobs, full-time jobs in the US.” ▶ 18:59
Average unemployment duration has risen to 25 weeks, versus 16 weeks going into the 2008 financial crisis — more than 50% higher, indicating the labour market is more structurally impaired than it was at the GFC onset.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. labour market
So what: Extended unemployment duration suppresses consumer spending power and confidence even before a headline recession is declared; the consumer is already weakening.
“The duration of people's unemployment is now up to 25 weeks where before going into the financial crisis it was at 16 weeks. So we are you know more than 50% above that.” ▶ 19:29
The job-creation-to-labour-force ratio is currently at a level only seen during recessions over the past 60 years, even using the optimistic headline NFP figures.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. labour market
So what: A recession is already likely underway in the real economy; markets trading at peak valuations on the assumption of economic strength are mispriced.
Consumer Balance Sheet & Affordability Stress 4
Zeberg argues the bottom 80–90% of U.S. consumers may be in the worst financial shape ever recorded — worse than during the GFC or even 1929 — driven by housing unaffordability, prolonged real income erosion, and fear of job loss.
thesis🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. consumer
So what: Consumer discretionary and housing-exposed sectors face a structural demand cliff, not a cyclical soft patch; CAPE-style mean reversion in consumer stocks could be severe.
“The consumer on an aggregated level... the bottom 90%... you will see that they probably haven't been in a worse situation ever. I mean, even going in and being in the financial crisis and even also in the 1929.” ▶ 16:42
Housing unaffordability is flagged as today's structural analogue to the 2008 real estate bubble — people can't afford to stay in their homes — but today's problem is on the demand/income side rather than levered speculation, making it harder to fix with rate cuts.
causal🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. housing market
So what: Rate cuts alone won't reflate housing if consumer incomes are falling; homebuilders and mortgage REITs face a scenario where the policy antidote fails to work.
U.S. national savings rate is currently 2–3%, versus 10–20% in the 1970s stagflation era — this structural difference means consumers cannot sustain spending through an inflationary period the way they did in the 70s.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. consumerU.S. savings rate
So what: A 70s-style stagflation repeat is not the base case; instead expect deflationary demand collapse once consumers exhaust their ability to spend.
“In the 70s people's savings rate were like 10 to 20%. Even when it was bad now it's down to 2 to 3%.” ▶ 22:36
Zeberg's implied framework: when the post-crash fiscal/monetary stimulus arrives, consumers will SAVE rather than spend it due to scarring from prolonged financial stress — this risks a depression-length recovery, not a V-shape.
causal🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumFederal ReserveU.S. consumer
So what: Velocity-of-money dynamics will be crucial; if stimulus does not reach the real economy, liquidity injections will inflate financial assets again without restoring GDP growth — stagflationary asset bubble redux.
“This time if the money doesn't really start to rotate in the real economy but rotates in the financial world... then you can start to see inflation picking up while the economy is still sluggish.” ▶ 36:29
Fed Policy, Inflation Narrative, and Regime Shift 5
Zeberg argues Powell is dramatically behind the curve; the labour market data alone justifies aggressive cuts, and Trump's pressure for a leadership change to Kevin Warsh could itself be a market catalyst that sends equities sharply higher in the near term.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumFederal ReserveJerome PowellKevin Warsh
So what: A Fed chair transition to a more dovish/accommodative leader is a plausible near-term upside catalyst; watch Fed Chair succession news as a potential melt-up accelerant.
≈ “One of the things that can be sending the things much higher very sharply here could be that there will actually be a regime shift from Kevin Wars.” paraphrase
High bond yields today are NOT primarily driven by inflation — they reflect late-cycle capital demand from corporates deploying heavy capex — a distinction the market is misreading as a structural inflation problem.
contrarian🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: high10-Year Treasury30-Year TreasuryFederal Reserve
So what: If yields are a capex/demand phenomenon rather than an inflation signal, they should fall sharply when corporate investment turns down — creating a bond rally opportunity the bond market is not pricing.
≈ “What we see right now is that there is a big demand for capital coming from a lot of large firms putting a lot of capital in expenditures out there... this is the demand for capital late cycle phenomenon.” paraphrase ▶ 24:17
Inflation dynamics today are the opposite of the 1970s: the 70s had strong job creation (5–6x better ratio than today) and high savings to sustain price increases; today's economy lacks both, meaning persistent high inflation is not the base case.
contrarian🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highCPIFederal Reserve
So what: Stagflation bears are wrong on inflation persistence; the disinflationary/deflationary consumer demand collapse is the more likely outcome, making long-duration Treasuries attractive post-crash.
≈ “In the 70s the non-farm payrolls the job creation was like five six end times at sometimes better ratio wise than what we are right now. That's creates that's the vehicle for inflation.” paraphrase
Zeberg argues the world has entered a new structurally higher inflation regime since 2020 (visible in three distinct inflation waves over the last decade on his charts), meaning long-term investors face a secular inflation risk even if near-term deflation hits first.
thesis🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumCPIFederal Reserve
So what: The sequence matters: (1) buy long bonds during the deflationary crash, (2) rotate to inflation-protecting real assets as the structural inflation regime reasserts — timing the pivot is the key portfolio challenge.
“We have now an higher inflation level it may come down it may drop but we'll not see new lows and from here on we probably see it going up.” ▶ 42:49
During the GFC, inflation was running at 5–7% as late as mid-2008 and the Fed was cutting aggressively — the current Fed paralysis over inflation while the labour market deteriorates repeats this same historical error.
causal🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highFederal ReserveJerome Powell
So what: The Fed cutting despite elevated headline inflation is historically normal in late-cycle stress; when cuts come, interpret them as recession confirmation rather than reflation.
≈ “Going into 2008, inflation was shooting up to 5 6 or 7% in mid 2008 and the Fed was cutting like crazy. Nobody knew we had a recession back then.” paraphrase
Crypto, Gold & Silver in the Melt-Up and Crash 6
Bitcoin and crypto will join the melt-up in a late rotation wave — after equities have already run — and will serve as the 'fat lady singing' signal that the cycle top is imminent. Zeberg has positions in Digital Asset Treasuries (DATs).
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highBitcoinEthereumcryptoDigital Asset Treasuries
So what: Crypto surging to new highs is not an independent bullish signal — it marks the final speculative mania phase and is a timing indicator to begin reducing ALL risk exposure.
≈ “When we start to see that these extreme risk assets which they cry including Bitcoin starting to take off then you'll see that that's the fat lady singing into this late phase of the cycle.” paraphrase
Zeberg sees Bitcoin reaching $100,000–$120,000 as the cycle top range, after possibly dipping a further $3,000–$4,000 first; he does NOT expect a new all-time high above the previous peak, suggesting the $120k area would be historically bounded.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: $100k–$120k is the take-profit zone for Bitcoin longs; above that, risk/reward flips sharply negative heading into a combined equity+crypto top.
≈ “100,000, yeah, 100,000 I see definitely and I think it could go to either 100 or up to 120,000. But that I think was going to be the top in that range.” paraphrase
Ethereum and altcoins will outperform Bitcoin in the final crypto leg as capital seeks higher beta within crypto — the ETH/BTC ratio historically turns up in this phase — making Ethereum a better late-cycle trade than Bitcoin.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumEthereumBitcoin
So what: Favour ETH over BTC for the late melt-up trade; when BTC tops, ETH and alts may continue briefly but then collapse faster.
≈ “You'll probably going to take off first and then you see Ethereum taking over which we normally see in that phase.” paraphrase
Gold and silver have already had their run — silver hit an 'Eiffel Tower' parabola (referenced around $130) and has already reversed — and will NOT make new all-time highs in this cycle.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highGoldSilver
So what: Gold and silver longs should be cautious; the catalyst narrative (inflation hedge) has played out. A modest dollar-weakness-driven bounce is possible (DXY to ~94) but not a new high.
≈ “Silver is mainly a industrial product and it went to 130 or something and it was so clear that that was the same kind of Eiffel Tower move... they had their time in the sun.” paraphrase
During the crash phase, gold, silver, Bitcoin and all risk assets will sell off simultaneously as the dollar surges — forced selling to meet margin calls and loan repayments will overwhelm any 'safe haven' narrative for gold.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highGoldSilverBitcoinU.S. DollarDXY
So what: Do not use gold or crypto as crash hedges; the only true safe haven in the initial crisis phase will be USD cash and short-term U.S. Treasuries.
“You actually saw that both gold and silver also went down because there was a need for cash... You need to sell it to get dollars to do it with.” ▶ 40:54
The DXY could rally to 120 during the crash — a level not seen since ~2001 — as global dollar-short positions unwind; Zeberg compares it to 2022's rally to 114 but expects this move to be even more violent.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumDXYU.S. Dollar
So what: A DXY-120 scenario is catastrophic for EM currencies, commodity prices, and foreign-currency-denominated U.S. corporate debt; it amplifies the deflationary shock globally.
≈ “I see the dollar actually heading up north of to 120 on the Dixie. And I know this sounds insane. We were at 114 I think in 22.” paraphrase
Safe Haven Positioning and Debt Dynamics 5
The only safe haven in the initial crash phase is short-duration U.S. Treasuries and USD cash; after the Fed moves to full accommodation, the safe haven trade rotates to inflation-protected real assets as the structural inflation regime reasserts.
framework🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. TreasuriesUSDFederal Reserve
So what: A barbell strategy — short Treasuries now, then rotate to real assets after the crash bottom — is the implied playbook, with the transition point triggered by aggressive Fed easing.
≈ “In that environment, the safe haven will be bonds in that particular period. But the moment the Fed comes out and start to say okay now we push on for real I'll be cautious because we have entered a new inflationary period.” paraphrase
U.S. federal debt-to-GDP is approximately 120% versus ~60% at the time of the GFC, meaning the government's capacity to absorb the next recession via fiscal stimulus is substantially constrained compared to 2008.
data_point🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highU.S. TreasuryU.S. debt
So what: Fiscal stimulus will be limited in size relative to the problem; the recovery will be slower and more reliant on monetary policy, which has diminishing real-economy effectiveness.
≈ “The debt level in the US was 60% back in in 2008. Now it's 120 something on the debt to GDP.” paraphrase
Buffett's current behaviour of building cash and short-duration bond exposure is cited as corroborating evidence for the defensive crash playbook; Zeberg implies Buffett is 'staging up' for the crisis window.
anecdote🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumWarren BuffettBerkshire HathawayU.S. Treasuries
So what: Buffett's cash pile is a real-time signal aligned with Zeberg's framework; monitor Berkshire's cash deployment as an independent topping indicator.
≈ “I think that's what Mr. Buffett is also trying to starting to do now is, you know, staging up on on that side.” paraphrase
Capital borrowed from future growth via repeated interest rate suppression has created a structural debt overhang that guarantees the coming recession will be unusually deep; there is no painless exit.
thesis🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highFederal ReserveU.S. debt
So what: There is no soft-landing exit from this cycle; the debt overhang means that a growth slowdown mechanically triggers a solvency/liquidity crisis faster than in prior cycles.
≈ “We have been pushing and been craving tomorrow's growth by pushing the inflation in sorry the interest rates lower. We are borrowing consumption from tomorrow.” paraphrase
The 'stagnation but not stagflation' distinction is important: true stagflation requires 6–8% inflation AND 7–8% unemployment simultaneously; Zeberg believes current conditions do not qualify and the immediate risk is deflationary collapse, not stagflation.
contrarian🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highCPIFederal Reserve
So what: Investors positioned for stagflation (commodity longs, TIPS) may be in the wrong trade for the near term; the deflationary crash scenario argues for bonds and USD over commodities.
≈ “Stagflation is when you have six, seven, eight% inflation and unemployment is staying at 7 8% as well. Then you got stagflation... Don't even use that word here. That's wrong.” paraphrase
AI's Labour Impact and Demand Destruction 4
AI's near-term net employment effect will be negative: productivity gains allow companies to produce the same output with fewer workers, and the quarter-to-quarter cost-cutting incentive ensures headcount reductions precede any new job creation by years.
causal🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highAIU.S. labour market
So what: AI-driven productivity gains are a demand-destruction force in the near term; labour-intensive sectors and consumer spending will face more pressure than AI bulls are pricing in.
≈ “The net effect of using AI in your business would be probably that you're going to make yourself more productive and more productive people means that you lead less people.” paraphrase
AI may create jobs over a 5–10 year horizon, but the transition gap (today through ~2030) is a period of net job destruction, coinciding with the peak-bubble period Zeberg is calling for — compounding economic weakness.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumAIU.S. labour market
So what: The timing overlap of AI displacement and a credit/equity crisis means the social and political response will be unusually intense, likely driving large fiscal interventions that ultimately fuel the structural inflation Zeberg warns about long-term.
The Hang Seng and Kospi (Samsung) are flagged as already at or near cycle tops; capital is rotating out of Asian equities into U.S. assets, which is one force fuelling the final U.S. melt-up.
prediction🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: mediumHang SengKospiSamsung
So what: Asian equity outperformance may be exhausted; the trade is to short Asian indices on bounces while the U.S. captures the final wave of global capital rotation.
≈ “I think there is a top already in the in the Hang Seng for instance... and that can potentially then unfuel as I said the market in the US.” paraphrase
The SpaceX IPO — valued at over $1 trillion despite cumulative losses of $41 billion over 5–6 years and a $5 billion loss last year alone — is cited by Zeberg as the archetypal 'crazy phase' signal equivalent to the most egregious dot-com-era valuations.
contrarian🗣 Henrik Zebergconviction: highSpaceXIPO market
So what: The SpaceX IPO is a sentiment indicator, not an investment opportunity at current pricing; the willingness of capital to defend trillion-dollar loss-making ventures marks the psychological peak of the cycle.
≈ “When people try to defend an IPO that where we see a company coming out with you know having made a loss of five you know billion last year and accumulated a loss of 41 billion over the last five six years and they try to defend that the worth of that is more than trillion... that's absurd.” paraphrase

The Prof G Show · Scott Galloway 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

Heather Cox Richardson: What History Predicts Happens Next65 min37 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Historian Heather Cox Richardson and Scott Galloway dissect the Iran 'deal' (a memo of understanding, not a binding agreement), the gutting of U.S. diplomatic expertise, and draw extended parallels between 2026 and the Gilded Age / late-1920s concentration of wealth — arguing a populist political reckoning is imminent and that Democrats have a deep bench while Republicans are stifled by Trump's dominance.
Iran Deal: Memo of Understanding, Not a Deal · 8Erosion of Institutional Expertise and Government Competence · 5Historical Parallels: Gilded Age, 1929, and the Coming Reckoning · 6Political Landscape: 2026 Midterms, 2028 Presidential Bench · 8Macroeconomic Stress: Tariffs, Energy, Consumer Pain · 4Geopolitical Order and U.S. Brand Equity Erosion · 5AI Adoption and Workforce Upskilling (Sponsor Segment — Signal) · 1
Iran Deal: Memo of Understanding, Not a Deal 8
The document signed with Iran in Geneva is a memorandum of understanding — an agreement to pursue an agreement — not a binding deal; Galloway estimates 30–50% of MOUs result in actual deals, making celebration premature.
framework🗣 Scott Gallowayconviction: highIranIRGCStrait of HormuzJCPOAGeneva
So what: Markets pricing relief on oil supply disruption risk may be pricing a resolution that has a coin-flip or worse probability of materializing; oil and energy volatility risk remains elevated.
“I would bet somewhere between 30 and 50% of memos of understanding actually result in a deal and it strikes me that to position this as a deal is disingenuous.” ▶ 12:11
Richardson counted approximately 39 'announcements of understanding' from the Trump administration by the time of this episode, and argues the timing of these announcements appears designed at least in part to affect financial markets.
causal🗣 Heather Cox Richardsonconviction: mediumTrumpIranoil markets
So what: Repeated market-moving announcements that don't culminate in deals suggest a pattern of deliberate market manipulation or at minimum media-cycle management; traders should discount headline deal announcements from this administration.
“if you look at the timing of the announcements of understandings which have been 39 of them I think at this point it certainly seems to be designed at least in part to affect the market” ▶ 13:28
Every day the Iran conflict extends without a definitive deal, leverage shifts from the U.S. to Iran; the American public has no appetite for military re-engagement, making Iranian delay the dominant strategy.
causal🗣 Scott Gallowayconviction: highIranIRGCStrait of HormuzU.S. military
So what: Iran is playing a time-arbitrage game — the longer the standoff, the better their negotiating position; a bullish oil thesis (supply constraint via Hormuz) has a longer tail than market consensus implies.
Galloway argues the Iran conflict outcome could leave the U.S. with a worse deal than the JCPOA after spending enormous money and credibility — 'the most expensive boomerang in history.'
thesis🗣 Scott Gallowayconviction: highIranJCPOAU.S.IRGC
So what: U.S. geopolitical credibility erosion is a persistent structural negative for dollar hegemony, defense contractor earnings dependent on diplomatic deterrence, and allies' willingness to coordinate on future sanctions regimes.
“we have essentially paid a ton of money and uh credibility in terms of US brand equity such that we could get a worse deal than we had with the JCPOA” ▶ 13:11
Richardson notes the U.S. 'is not looking real good against a mediocre power' and that Iran has figured out it can use the Strait of Hormuz as real leverage — a position difficult to counter militarily.
thesis🗣 Heather Cox Richardsonconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzU.S. military
So what: U.S. military deterrence credibility has degraded in a visible, publicized way; this could embolden other regional actors and raise the risk premium on energy assets transiting contested chokepoints.
“The United States has not turned out uh looking real good against a mediocre power. It certainly looks as if the United States is a lot weaker than Iran.” ▶ 10:52
Leaders of major U.S. oil companies went to the press (rather than quietly briefing the White House) to warn that U.S. oil reserves are running low and 'something has to happen,' a highly unusual public pressure move.
data_point🗣 Heather Cox Richardsonconviction: highU.S. oil companiesWhite Houseoil reserves
So what: Critically low U.S. strategic and commercial oil stocks create a hard deadline for resolving the Strait of Hormuz disruption; a spike in diesel/gasoline prices is a near-term macro risk that will hit consumer sentiment and transport-dependent sectors.

Historical Parallels: Gilded Age, 1929, and the Coming Reckoning 6
Richardson draws explicit parallels between 2026 and the Gilded Age (1870s–1900s): concentration of wealth, industrialists buying senators, and carving up global resources — but notes the key difference is today's power holders actively want to destroy democracy, whereas Gilded Age robber barons still believed in separation of powers.

Galloway and Richardson both invoke the 1929 crash as a template: policy errors (tightening when stimulus was needed) compounded the disaster, but the resulting fury at financial elites drove a landslide swing from Hoover (1928) to FDR (1932) in just four years.

Geopolitical Order and U.S. Brand Equity Erosion 5
Galloway argues the entire post-WWII prosperity was built on a shared operating system — common language of evidence, norms, economics, science — and that when the world's most powerful nation shifts to 'I've got an invisible friend behind me,' it threatens the entire global social order.
