The Lawfare Podcast 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

Rational Security: The "Predestination" Edition75 min37 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Lawfare panel dissects three June 2026 national-security crises: a fragile US-Iran ceasefire deal that analysts call a strategic defeat, the Commerce Department's export-control shutdown of Anthropic's Fable model which signals a de facto AI licensing regime, and outgoing DNI Tulsi Gabbard's politically motivated rescission of Havana syndrome intelligence assessments. Across all three topics the dominant theme is how politicized decision-making and hollowed-out institutions are producing foreseeable, costly failures.
US-Iran War Outcome: Strategic Defeat · 13Anthropic Fable Export Controls: De Facto AI Licensing Regime · 11Politicized Intelligence: Gabbard's DNI Legacy · 7Politicized Decision-Making and Institutional Hollowing · 4Strait of Hormuz as Geopolitical Asset · 2
US-Iran War Outcome: Strategic Defeat 13
The US-Iran ceasefire deal is a 14-point framework so thin in legal language that experienced nuclear negotiators say no lawyer would have permitted the US side to leave the room with it; virtually every major substantive issue — nuclear program, proxies, missiles, human rights — is deferred to a 60-day follow-on window.
thesis🗣 Ariane Tabatabaiconviction: highIranUnited StatesIAEAOmanSwitzerland
So what: A 60-day deadline for resolving the nuclear file creates a hard catalyst: expect renewed market volatility in oil and shipping around mid-August 2026 if talks stall.
“Did a lawyer approve of this? ... when we're negotiating a nuclear agreement a few years ago, I do not think that our lawyers would have left us ... leave the room alive if we had walked away with this kind of text that leaves so much unsaid.” ▶ 7:35
Iran appears to have secured the right to impose a fee or toll structure on Strait of Hormuz transit, potentially creating a new sovereign revenue stream and a passive on/off lever over global oil shipping that did not exist before the conflict.
causal🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: mediumIranStrait of HormuzOmanLloyd's List
So what: A permanent Iranian fee regime on Hormuz transit would structurally raise baseline shipping costs for Gulf crude exports and give Iran low-cost coercive leverage without triggering overt military response.
The outcome is worse than the pre-war status quo: the US depleted critical munitions stockpiles, lost 14 service members, spent billions degrading cheap Iranian capabilities Iran can rebuild quickly, and received no meaningful concession on the nuclear file, proxies, or missile programs.
thesis🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highUnited StatesIranIRGC
So what: Defense contractors may see near-term munitions restocking demand (a positive), but the strategic loss undermines US deterrence premiums that have historically supported Gulf-region risk assets.
“The United States won every battle of this conflict. The US military accorded itself as the finest fighting force in the world, but the United States lost this war.” ▶ 16:50
Russia and China are drawing the lesson that the US will back down under serious political costs and lacks endurance for protracted conflict — a direct adversarial inference from the Iran outcome.
causal🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highUnited StatesRussiaChinaIran
So what: Reduced US deterrence credibility increases tail risk across Taiwan Strait, Eastern Europe, and South China Sea scenarios — a macro risk premium expansion for EM assets in those regions.
“If you don't think people in Russia and China have observed this and drawn the conclusion that President Trump will back down in the face of serious political costs or that the US lacks the endurance for a protracted conflict, this has been a strategic disaster.” ▶ 17:11
Post-war Iran is governed by a more radical, less experienced leadership cohort than pre-war Iran; Amnesty International and other rights monitors report executions at a pace unmatched in recent decades, with the conflict used as cover for mass arbitrary arrests.
data_point🗣 Ariane Tabatabaiconviction: highIranIRGCAmnesty International
So what: A more extreme, repressive regime with new revenue streams and validated military doctrine is a structurally higher geopolitical risk factor for the region than the pre-war equilibrium.
“We are not dealing with a different, reformed, enlightened regime. We're dealing with the same regime, but worse.” ▶ 14:03
Iran has validated and refined its Strait of Hormuz closure doctrine through this conflict, learning real implementation lessons that make future use of that lever more credible and more precisely calibrated.
thesis🗣 Ariane Tabatabaiconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzIRGC
So what: Oil market participants should reprice the probability and cost of a future Hormuz disruption upward; the tail risk is no longer theoretical.
“Iran had obviously been planning for this for a long time. But this conflict really allowed for it to kind of refine that whole scenario planning and think through the costs and benefits.” ▶ 26:50
The US Navy has been running a covert shadow-fleet operation along the Omani side of the Strait — ships running without lights or tracking devices — to move cargo, a sign that publicly claimed freedom-of-navigation operations have quietly failed.
data_point🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: mediumUS NavyOmanStrait of HormuzLloyd's List
So what: Shipping insurance rates for Gulf transit should price in the fragility of this arrangement; any resumption of Iranian pressure could rapidly collapse the workaround.
Iran's negotiating posture has been strengthened by observing the US repeatedly shift goalposts — from demanding unconditional surrender to accepting a ceasefire that restores the status quo ante — and Iran is in no hurry knowing midterms constrain Trump from restarting conflict.
framework🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highIranUnited StatesTrump
So what: Expect Iran to drag the 60-day nuclear negotiation, extracting further concessions, particularly as the November 2026 US midterms approach and Trump's political risk appetite shrinks.
“Negotiating with the United States, I don't think the Iranians are going to be in a hurry ... they'll likely end up with a better deal if they just wait a little longer.” ▶ 22:47
The ceasefire has no durable guarantee because Israel is a co-belligerent but not a party to the agreement and Netanyahu remains under domestic pressure to strike targets in Lebanon, making Israeli action a persistent spoiler risk.
thesis🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highIsraelNetanyahuLebanonHezbollahIranUnited States
So what: The ceasefire should be priced as fragile; a single Israeli strike on Lebanon could unravel it, spiking oil and triggering safe-haven flows.
“Whatever influence the president has over our Israeli partners, it's not perfect. He will respond to his domestic political requirements and this will be a persistent problem.” ▶ 35:43
No cabinet official actually backed Trump's decision to go to war with Iran — they simply were not willing to say no — reflecting a decision-making environment where dissent has been structurally eliminated from the national security apparatus.
causal🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: highTrumpJD VanceUnited States
So what: The absence of internal brakes means future presidential impulses toward military action face even less institutional friction, making tail-risk scenarios in other theaters more probable.
The US-Iran deal appears to include sanctions relief giving Iran access to billions of previously frozen dollars even in this first 60-day phase, with JD Vance framing this as 'no US dollars going to Iran' — a rhetorical distinction that obscures substantial economic relief.
data_point🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: mediumIranUnited StatesJD VanceTrump
So what: Sanctions relief without nuclear concessions increases Iranian hard-currency reserves and reduces the economic pressure that was the primary non-military lever over Iranian behavior.
Netanyahu has moved from having Trump as his closest ally to having Trump as a potential source of leverage against him — with the US-Israel security assistance agreement up for renegotiation and Trump openly rebuking Netanyahu publicly.
causal🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: mediumNetanyahuIsraelTrumpJD Vance
So what: The structural shift in the US-Israel relationship under a future JD Vance administration (closer to the isolationist-right wing of the GOP) represents a longer-term risk to Israel's security posture and the US defense assistance model.
“Bibi's gone from having Trump as his closest ally to somebody who could actually use that leverage against him.” ▶ 34:50
Air-only military campaigns historically tend to reinforce target regimes by delegitimizing domestic opposition who cannot safely protest while their country is being bombed — the pattern seen in Serbia applies here to Iran.
framework🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highIranSerbiaUnited StatesIRGCBasij
So what: Regime change through air power alone is not a credible scenario; investors should not price in political instability in Iran as a near-term variable.
≈ “An air-only campaign, if you look at Serbia and elsewhere, often tends to reinforce the regime and delegitimize the opposition.” paraphrase
Anthropic Fable Export Controls: De Facto AI Licensing Regime 11
The Commerce Department's export control letter on Anthropic's Fable model marks the beginning of a de facto licensing regime for frontier AI models: companies must now obtain government approval before releasing powerful models or risk having them shut down post-launch.
thesis🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: highAnthropicCommerce DepartmentBISFableMythos
So what: Any frontier AI company — OpenAI, Google DeepMind, xAI, Meta — now faces regulatory launch risk that did not previously exist; this is a material new compliance cost and timeline uncertainty for AI product roadmaps.
“This is the beginning of essentially a de facto licensing regime for frontier models where if you're Anthropic or presumably OpenAI or Google ... you need to go to the government and ask permission before you can release one of these powerful models.” ▶ 38:02
The Commerce Department's export control letter only controls the model weights, not API or web interface access to the model — meaning Anthropic may not have been legally obligated to shut down Fable for all users, and may have made a strategic business decision rather than a legal one.
contrarian🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: mediumAnthropicBISCommerce DepartmentFable
So what: The government may be bluffing on the scope of its authority; future AI companies facing similar orders could legally challenge the breadth of the shutdown rather than comply, potentially narrowing the regime's reach.
“The letter does not on its face apply to model access ... it confirms something that ... export control dorks who have been following this have thought, which is that it's not actually clear that the export control laws apply to accessing US software when it's cloud hosted.” ▶ 40:40
BIS has a long-standing policy determination that cloud-hosted software-as-a-service is not subject to export controls, and the House of Representatives earlier in 2026 passed legislation to explicitly expand export controls to cover remote access — implying current law does not reach it.
data_point🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: highBISCommerce DepartmentUS House of RepresentativesAnthropic
So what: The Senate is now considering the same expansion; passage would dramatically broaden the government's ability to shut down AI APIs globally and represents a key legislative risk to watch for all cloud AI providers.
Fable represents Anthropic's next full pre-training run — roughly 'adding a zero' to compute scale — and the resulting capability jump confirms that AI scaling laws are still producing step-change improvements without requiring novel architectural breakthroughs.
thesis🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: highAnthropicFableMythosOpenAIGoogleMetaxAI
So what: If scale alone drove Fable's capabilities, then OpenAI GPT-6, Google Gemini 4, xAI, and open-source Chinese models will reach equivalent capability in 6–9 months — meaning the national security rationale for Fable-specific controls will be obsolete within a year.
≈ “What that tells us is that the scaling laws of capabilities are still alive and well ... the Chinese open-source models are going to get there in 6 to 9 months.” paraphrase ▶ 50:11
If every model at Fable's capability level triggers national-security emergency controls, then every frontier model from every lab going forward will be treated as equally dangerous — making Anthropic-specific controls incoherent as a policy.
causal🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIGooglexAIMetaCommerce Department
So what: A coherent US AI regulatory regime cannot be company-specific at this capability tier; the policy debate will be forced toward sector-wide licensing, creating both compliance costs and potential competitive moats for incumbents who can navigate it.
The Trump administration's AI Cybersecurity Executive Order explicitly stated it was 'not a licensing regime,' yet within weeks Commerce imposed export controls that create precisely such a regime — revealing internal policy incoherence.
contrarian🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: highTrumpCommerce DepartmentAnthropicBIS
So what: Policy whiplash on AI regulation increases uncertainty for AI company valuations and investment timelines; the gap between stated and operative policy makes planning extremely difficult.
≈ “The EO was explicit, it literally said this is not a licensing regime. And while it is true that it is not a licensing regime, then a week later the United States government acts in a way that creates a de facto licensing regime.” paraphrase
Applying export controls to foreign nationals inside the US as AI researchers is a potentially severe constraint on frontier AI development talent pipelines, given that leading labs rely heavily on non-citizen researchers on temporary employment visas.
causal🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIGoogleCommerce Department
So what: If this precedent holds and foreign-national access to model weights becomes a controlled activity, US AI labs face a structural talent disadvantage against overseas competitors who face no such restriction.
Anthropic chose not to legally challenge the export control letter — likely as a strategic business decision to preserve its relationship with the government during ongoing DCR negotiations — even though it may have legal grounds for an ultra vires challenge arguing Commerce exceeded its statutory authority.
anecdote🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: mediumAnthropicCommerce DepartmentBIS
So what: Anthropic's non-confrontational posture reveals that even well-resourced AI labs are calculating that regulatory relationships matter more than legal victories in the current political environment — a signal that the sector is moving toward accommodation rather than resistance.
The Trump administration has a separate, broader campaign against Anthropic including supply chain risk designations currently being challenged in court, indicating the export control action may be part of a systematic adversarial relationship rather than a one-off safety concern.
thesis🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: mediumAnthropicTrumpCommerce Department
So what: Investors in Anthropic or AI infrastructure exposed to Anthropic's revenues face not just regulatory risk but targeted political risk from a hostile administration — a discount factor not fully priced in pre-IPO valuations.
Dario Amodei has publicly supported export controls on AI chips and semiconductors as a tool to give democratic nations an edge over China in AI development, making it harder for Anthropic to credibly oppose the principle of government intervention in AI even as it disputes this specific application.
contrarian🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: mediumAnthropicDario AmodeiChina
So what: Anthropic's ideological alignment with AI safety and export controls creates a rhetorical trap that limits its ability to build a broad coalition against government overreach in AI regulation.
Congress's current NDAA drafts include provisions to limit the supply chain risk authority being used against Anthropic — suggesting bipartisan concern about executive overreach into AI that could constrain the administration's tools.
data_point🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: lowUS CongressAnthropicNDAACommerce Department
So what: Track the NDAA markup for limitations on BIS/Commerce supply chain designation authority; passage would materially reduce the government's coercive toolkit against AI companies.
Politicized Intelligence: Gabbard's DNI Legacy 7
Outgoing DNI Tulsi Gabbard rescinded Biden-era intelligence community assessments on Havana syndrome without presenting any specific classified evidence for why those assessments were flawed, and without naming a replacement culprit — effectively invalidating prior findings while producing no new analytical conclusion.
thesis🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highTulsi GabbardCIANICRussia
So what: The rescission without replacement leaves the US with no operative IC consensus on a decade-long mystery, degrading the analytical baseline at exactly the moment a new DNI (Jay Clayton) takes over.
“This was a recall, not a replacement. The DNI didn't show her work. She judged the prior assessments by a standard that no one can actually look at.” ▶ 1:03:27
Approximately 1,500 US personnel reported Havana syndrome cases; many were Russia-focused officers; and the agonizing analytical problem is that a subset of genuine foreign attacks may be hiding within a larger population of naturally occurring or environmentally caused incidents.
data_point🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highCIAUS intelligence communityRussia
So what: The victim population is large and politically active; the unresolved question will continue to drive legislative and institutional pressure regardless of which administration is in power.
Gabbard's office cited five specific analytic tradecraft failings in the prior Havana syndrome assessments — selective exclusion of contrary information, mischaracterization of raw reporting, suppression of alternative conclusions, flawed medical studies, and limited collection — but provided no unclassified evidence supporting any of them.
causal🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highTulsi GabbardNICBiden administration
So what: The pattern of serious-sounding procedural charges without evidentiary backing is a template for future politically motivated intelligence rescissions — the same approach used by Ratcliffe recalling 16 CIA products in 2020.
Gabbard's office issued a new Intelligence Community Directive (ICD 203) against which it is judging prior assessments — but that directive is unclassified yet has not been made public, making independent evaluation of the rescission's validity impossible.
data_point🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highTulsi GabbardODNINIC
So what: Secret analytic standards are antithetical to oversight; this is a structural degradation of IC accountability that will outlast Gabbard's tenure.
Gabbard's final week also included release of a report implying US involvement in biological weapons labs in Ukraine — claims that closely track Russian information operations from the start of the Ukraine war and have been publicly debunked, including by Laura Loomer.
contrarian🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: highTulsi GabbardRussiaUkraineLaura LoomerODNI
So what: A US intelligence leader producing analysis that mirrors adversary propaganda in her final days sets a damaging precedent for the IC's credibility with allies, particularly those in Eastern Europe.
Jay Clayton is being considered the likely DNI successor over Bill Pulte (who had used mortgage fraud investigations to target political rivals); Clayton is described as a serious person with a professional background who does not show signs of weaponizing the office.
prediction🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: mediumJay ClaytonBill PulteODNI
So what: A Clayton confirmation would be a modest positive signal for IC institutional stability relative to the Gabbard tenure, though the bar is extremely low.
Julia Curley characterizes Gabbard as 'probably the worst DNI in the office's 20-year history' and notes it has led many to question whether the DNI position should be abolished or fundamentally reformed given the danger it poses to IC non-partisanship.
thesis🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highTulsi GabbardODNIUS intelligence community
So what: Serious institutional reform of the DNI structure is now a live policy debate; any such reform would reshape oversight of 18 IC agencies and affect defense/intelligence contractor relationships.
“Probably the worst DNI in the office's 20-year history and has led many people to question whether the DNI position should be abolished or fundamentally reformed.” ▶ 1:07:22
Politicized Decision-Making and Institutional Hollowing 4
The DIA director was publicly fired after his agency's battle damage assessments contradicted the president's claims during the Iran conflict — demonstrating that analytic dissent in this administration is punished with career termination and clearance revocation.
causal🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highDIATrumpUS intelligence community
So what: When intelligence analysts self-censor to avoid career risk, the US government is systematically denied accurate information before major policy decisions — a compounding structural risk across all national security domains.
“Analysis at every phase of this was shut out, punished, or ignored. So, this is the price of politicized intelligence. The U.S. is blundering into foreseeable crises that no one is willing to forecast.” ▶ 30:34
The problems in the Iran conflict — the regime's ability to survive leadership decapitation, Iran's ability to close the Strait of Hormuz — were foreseeable and had been foreseen in numerous public war games and assessments; they were not intelligence surprises but politically inconvenient truths that were ignored.
thesis🗣 Julia Curleyconviction: highIranUnited StatesDIAStrait of Hormuz
So what: Investors should not assume the US will accurately assess the costs of future military action in advance; the gap between public bravado and classified reality can be used against the US by adversaries who understand the dynamic.
The Trump administration's relationship with AI regulation is, by the panel's assessment, 'not super coherent and rapidly changing' — exemplified by an AI Cybersecurity EO that explicitly renounced licensing, followed within a week by export controls that create one.
thesis🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: highTrumpCommerce DepartmentAnthropic
So what: Policy incoherence is itself a risk factor for any company that has built strategic plans around regulatory predictability; AI companies should stress-test for rapid policy reversals.
The Anthropic export control letter may reflect basic incompetence rather than a coherent legal strategy — it controls 'the model' without clearly defining whether that means weights only or API access, and may not have cited the correct statutory authority.
contrarian🗣 Alan Rosensteinconviction: mediumCommerce DepartmentBISAnthropic
So what: Regulatory actions built on shaky legal foundations are vulnerable to court challenge; companies with deep legal resources and high stakes should not assume compliance is the only option.
Strait of Hormuz as Geopolitical Asset 2
Iran's key strategic insight from the conflict is that the Strait of Hormuz functions as a pressure valve: completely closing it eventually forces global actors to act militarily, but releasing just enough pressure to allow partial traffic flow converts it from a military target into a permanent political tool and revenue source.
framework🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: highIranStrait of HormuzOmanUnited States
So what: The optimal Iranian strategy going forward is calibrated partial restriction — keeping enough traffic flowing to forestall military response while extracting fees and political concessions; this is a permanently more complex shipping risk environment.
The conflict has demonstrated that the US and international community are not willing or able to force the Strait of Hormuz open through military means if Iran pushes back hard enough — a test case that has now been run in live conditions.
thesis🗣 Scott Andersonconviction: highUnited StatesIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: Oil market participants and insurers should permanently revise upward the probability and duration of Hormuz disruption in their risk models — this is no longer a hypothetical.
“The real damage of this conflict, I think, is that the United States had ... shown that it's not able or willing to force the straits open like I think it was always assumed they could or would if Iran pushed too hard.” ▶ 25:14

Macro Voices · Erik Townsend 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

MacroVoices #537 Brent Johnson: There's No Turning Back88 min37 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Santiago Capital's Brent Johnson argues the US-Iran MOU is a fragile pause rather than a real peace, that the Strait of Hormuz closure has set up delayed agricultural and energy shocks in Q4 2026/Q1 2027, and that dollar de-dollarization is a structural myth — with dollar-denominated stablecoins now becoming a new, underappreciated arm of US monetary dominance.
Iran MOU: Tenuous Pause, Not Resolution · 6Oil Price Outlook: Forced Selloff Sets Up Reversal · 4Agricultural & MRO Second-Order Shocks · 4Dollar Milkshake & De-dollarization as Myth · 7Stablecoins as a Dollar Dominance Amplifier · 3Geopolitical Framework: US Hegemony, Deglobalization & Fourth Turning · 6Market Technicals & Investment Opportunities · 7
Iran MOU: Tenuous Pause, Not Resolution 6
The US-Iran MOU is best described as a managed pause rather than a genuine resolution: neither the US, Iran, nor Israel is likely to fully honor their obligations, and Brent Johnson explicitly expects a re-escalation within weeks to a few months.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highIranUnited StatesIsraelIRGC
So what: Investors pricing the Iran conflict as 'over' are likely wrong; oil-market and geopolitical risk premiums that unwound sharply may need to be rebuilt.
“I think the kinetic conflict is over for now and we're winding down for now, but I do not expect it to last.” ▶ 5:15
The MOU contains deliberately ambiguous language: each side is interpreting its obligations differently, particularly on weapons-grade uranium removal (Iran/US say 'down-blend on site'; Israel insists on physical removal) and Lebanon ceasefire terms that Israel explicitly rejects.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highIranUnited StatesIsraelIAEA
So what: The deal has structural fault lines that could cause it to collapse quickly; watch Israel's actions in Lebanon as the tripwire.
The US version of the MOU acknowledges a $300 billion (not $300 million) payment framework for Iran to be funded by third parties not party to the agreement — a mechanism Townsend considers implausible and likely to unravel.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highIranUnited States
So what: The financial architecture of the deal is a potential deal-breaker; if the $300B mechanism fails, expect re-escalation and oil price spike.
Iran's parliament speaker announced immediately after the episode was recorded that the Strait of Hormuz will never return to its pre-war configuration and that Iran retains the right to charge transit fees — consistent with Johnson's prediction that a re-escalation or permanent Iranian toll is the likely outcome.
prediction🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: If Iran permanently controls Hormuz and charges fees, global tanker economics and energy pricing structures change fundamentally; long Iran toll-beneficiary scenarios.
Both the US and Iranian versions of the MOU explicitly state that the Strait of Hormuz will be toll-free for only 60 days, after which Iran charges a $1/barrel user fee (approximately $2 million per VLCC), meaning both sides effectively codified permanent Iranian toll authority over the strait.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highIranUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: A $1/barrel permanent levy on roughly 20% of global oil trade is a lasting structural cost; could be bullish for non-Hormuz routing infrastructure (pipelines, alternate transit routes).
“Both versions of theou say that's what it goes back to after 60 days... Iran has basically been authorized to construct a permanent lifetime toll booth over the Straight of Hormuz.” ▶ 16:54
Johnson believes the US pushed for a deal now — despite incomplete terms — because global petroleum reserves were being drawn down to dangerous levels, commercial US inventories hit operational minimums, and the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to its lowest level since 1983, meaning inaction risked a catastrophic supply crisis.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesSPROPEC
So what: The US negotiated from a position of energy weakness; the SPR needs to be rebuilt — a structural buyer of physical crude regardless of the diplomatic outcome.
Oil Price Outlook: Forced Selloff Sets Up Reversal 4
The ~20% oil price collapse from ~$90 to ~$73 on the Iran peace announcement was driven by forced liquidation (margin calls, CTA flips, stop losses) rather than a real assessment of supply recovery; both Townsend and Anas Al-Hajj believe oil has already overshot to the downside.
thesis🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highWTI crudeCTAs
So what: Once forced selling exhausts, oil likely rebounds; large gap on the chart at ~$84.50 is a near-term technical target.
≈ “There is a a huge structural repositioning in oil that has driven these flows to the downside. Obviously once those forced flows are unwound there is going to be a reaction higher.” paraphrase
Townsend revised his oil target: he no longer expects $150-$200/barrel by year-end because China can buffer the crisis by releasing its own SPR (which is substantially larger than the US SPR) and resuming finished-product exports, but he still targets a rebound to the $85-$100 range.
prediction🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumWTI crudeChinaSPR
So what: The ceiling on the bullish oil trade is lower than feared; China's SPR is an effective global shock absorber, limiting extreme price spikes.
“I don't think we're headed to 200 anymore, but I think getting back to the 85 to 100 level is quite likely.” ▶ 1:17:48
Even if the Strait reopened immediately, it would take at minimum 30 days to normalize traffic flow, then an additional 6 weeks for ships departing today to reach their destinations — meaning physical supply relief is at least 10 weeks out from the episode date.
framework🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highStrait of HormuzVLCC
So what: Markets celebrating an immediate supply resolution are structurally wrong about the timeline; physical tightness will persist into late summer/early fall regardless of the diplomatic outcome.
Dr. Anas Al-Hajj, a well-known oil bear, said in the Townsend-moderated Curry vs. Al-Hajj debate that oil had already overshot to the downside — even before the additional $5 decline since that statement, suggesting even the bears see a near-term technical bounce.
contrarian🗣 Anas Al-Hajjconviction: mediumWTI crudeAnas Al-HajjJeff Curry
So what: When the most vocal oil bear turns tactically bullish, it is a meaningful signal that the downside move is exhausted in the short run.
Agricultural & MRO Second-Order Shocks 4
Ships didn't transit the Strait during the planting season, disrupting not just fuel but fertilizers, agricultural chemicals, and crop support inputs — Johnson expects a delayed 'food cascade' in Q4 2026 and Q1 2027 that the market is not pricing today.
prediction🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumagricultural commoditiesfertilizersStrait of Hormuz
So what: Go long agricultural commodities with a Q4 2026/Q1 2027 time horizon; DBA Jan 2027 $27/$30 bull call spread (90-cent debit, max $2.10 payoff) is one expressed trade from the episode.
Patrick Szna's Trade of the Week: buy DBA (Invesco DB Agriculture ETF, trading at $26.84) January 15, 2027 $27/$30 bull call spread for a 90-cent net debit, with max payoff of $2.10 if DBA is at or above $30 by expiration — a better-than-2:1 risk/reward play on delayed agricultural tightening.
prediction🗣 Patrick Sznaconviction: mediumDBAcornsoybeanswheatsugar
So what: Specific actionable structure with defined risk and convexity to express the thesis that food supply shocks materialize 6-9 months after the shipping disruption.
≈ “The trade is to buy the $27 strike call option for $1.70 and sell the $30 strike call for 80, which creates a 27 by 30 bull call spread for a 90 net debit.” paraphrase
The Gulf region is a critical hub for global aviation MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) operations; the Strait closure has disrupted this supply chain in a way that creates cascading disruptions into adjacent industries — a second-order effect Johnson says nobody else is discussing.
contrarian🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumaviationGulf regionMRO industry
So what: Watch for aviation supply-chain stress and delays in maintenance cycles; could benefit alternative MRO hubs or create cost pressure for airlines already stretched by fuel costs.
Supply chain nationalism has permanently broken the 'law of one price' for commodities: a given commodity now trades at materially different prices depending on where it is consumed, driven by tariffs, national security designations, and supply chain fragmentation — a regime change from the prior 25 years.
framework🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highcommoditiesChinaUnited States
So what: Commodity investors can no longer rely solely on a single global price; the spread between geographically-segmented markets is itself a tradeable variable.
“Commodity prices over the last year have started to diverge based on where they're traded or where they're used. You can now have a different price for a commodity in the United States than you do in China, you do in Africa.” ▶ 28:00
Dollar Milkshake & De-dollarization as Myth 7
De-dollarization is largely a myth when measured by actual usage metrics: FX turnover, cross-border lending, and trade invoicing are all at all-time highs — the dollar's share of official reserves has fallen mainly relative to gold, not versus other fiat currencies.
contrarian🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSDgoldFX markets
So what: The consensus 'dollar decline' narrative is unsupported by the data that actually measures dollar usage; dollar bear trades based on reserve share statistics are using the wrong metric.
“If you look at things like FX turnover, if you look at crossborder lending, if you look at trade invoicing, they're all as high now as they have ever been and they're higher than they were 30 years ago.” ▶ 50:31
Foreign holdings of US financial assets have nearly doubled since 2018 (when dollar-death predictions peaked), directly contradicting the de-dollarization narrative and validating the 'milkshake' thesis that capital would continue flowing into the US.
data_point🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSDUS financial assetsforeign investors
So what: The milkshake thesis has a strong 8-year track record; continued dollar demand driven by foreign capital inflows supports US equity and credit markets structurally.
The 'dual carry trade' structural constraint means non-US sovereigns always have at least one carry trade working against them (local vs. dollar), while the US only faces its own currency carry dynamics — this is why dollar crises in EM are repetitive and dollar dominance is self-reinforcing.
framework🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSDemerging markets
So what: The structural disadvantage of EM borrowers is permanent, not cyclical; dollar strength episodes will always cause disproportionate pain in EM — a recurring short opportunity in EM FX/credit.
“The rest of the world because they have both a local carry trade in their local currency and they have a global carry trade in the dollar, they always have one of these carry trades is going against them.” ▶ 52:54
Johnson's historical back-test: across 40 years and 16 EM case studies, 75% of the time when the dollar index moved outside its optimal 'band,' EM sovereigns went into distress and a dollar-funding crisis followed within a short time.
data_point🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSDemerging marketsDXY
So what: Watch the DXY band as a leading indicator for EM sovereign stress; a break above the band is a systematic signal to reduce EM exposure.
The US deliberately engineered a dollar shortage in Iran using Treasury tools, causing Iran's currency to collapse, driving domestic inflation, sparking street protests, and crushing the regime's revenue — demonstrating that 'weaponized dollar' operations are already in active use.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSDIranScott BessentUS Treasury
So what: Countries subject to US financial pressure face weaponized dollar dynamics beyond just sanctions; the dollar system gives the US an asymmetric tool that can destabilize adversary economies without military action.
“Bessant, Secretary Bessant just talked about this a few months ago where he said very specifically they used their tools to create a dollar shortage in Iran which caused their currency to fall very dramatically.” ▶ 54:42
Gold fell ~20% from the Iran war outbreak to the peace deal announcement because nations (Russia, Turkey, Gulf states) that had been buying gold as an alternative reserve asset were forced to sell it to get dollars needed for oil purchases on global markets, illustrating that gold functions as a dollar-liquidity substitute that gets liquidated in crises.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highgoldRussiaTurkeyGulf statesUSD
So what: Central bank gold reserves are not a true de-dollarization tool — they are liquidated precisely when dollars are scarce; the gold-as-reserve thesis has a critical Achilles heel.
“From the moment the war broke out in Iran to the moment that it looked like we were getting this deal signed last week, gold fell 20%. The reason it fell 20% is those countries who had gold... started selling it... because they needed more dollars to buy oil on the global stage.” ▶ 56:17
Rising US interest rates effectively imposed an 'early withdrawal penalty' on foreign dollar reserves held in Treasuries: when rates rose in 2022, bond prices fell and nations that needed to sell reserves to access dollars realized large losses — a covert wealth transfer to the US via the dollar system.
framework🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUS TreasuriesFederal Reserveemerging marketsUSD
So what: The US can simultaneously fight domestic inflation AND penalize foreign reserve holders; this dynamic will recur if/when the Fed tightens again.
“That's essentially the same thing as an early withdrawal penalty being put on their dollar reserves.” ▶ 57:34
Stablecoins as a Dollar Dominance Amplifier 3
99% of all outstanding stablecoins globally are US dollar-denominated, not because it was mandated but because market demand is overwhelmingly for dollar-based digital money — a revealed preference that strongly argues against any BRICS or global-south stablecoin displacing the dollar.
data_point🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSD stablecoinsBRICScrypto
So what: Dollar stablecoins are a structural extension of dollar hegemony into digital rails; bet on continued dollar stablecoin dominance and be skeptical of BRICS coin narratives.
“99% of all outstanding stable coins in the world today are US dollar stable coins... That was the issuers responding to demand in the market.” ▶ 43:57
As citizens in non-US countries choose to hold dollar stablecoins rather than their local currency, monetary sovereignty de facto transfers from local governments to Washington DC — Johnson describes this as 'an extremely powerful tool' that Treasury Secretary Bessant (a former currency speculator) is well-positioned to exploit.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUSD stablecoinsScott BessantUS TreasuryEM currencies
So what: Dollar stablecoin adoption in EM is a mechanism for gradual dollarization that bypasses capital controls; the US gains monetary leverage without firing a shot.
“The transfer of sovereignty, monetary sovereignty starts to leave that local jurisdiction and that local government and get transferred to Washington DC.” ▶ 45:03
Iran's oil — previously sold to China at a 20-30% discount in yuan on black markets — will now be sold at full price in dollars on open markets if the MOU holds, cutting off China's discounted yuan-denominated oil supply and forcing China to compete on dollar-priced markets.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumIranChinaCNYUSDcrude oil
So what: If the MOU holds, China loses a large supply of discounted yuan-settled oil, which increases its dollar demand and weakens a key pillar of the yuan internationalization project.
≈ “Iran is now going to be able to sell that oil, at least for the time being, for full price and in dollars... China was paying anywhere from a 20 to 30% discount and using guess what not dollars but yuan.” paraphrase ▶ 23:49
Geopolitical Framework: US Hegemony, Deglobalization & Fourth Turning 6
Johnson's core macro framework: the world is transitioning from globalization to deglobalization and from a US-led rules-based multilateral order to American bilateral 'America First' deal-making — he expects this arc to persist regardless of which US party wins future elections.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesglobalizationWTOUNWorld Bank
So what: Deglobalization is a durable regime, not a Trump-specific aberration; multi-year implications for supply chains, commodity regionalization, and EM vs. DM relative performance.
“I have a hard time seeing even if the Democrats come back to power, I have a hard time seeing us going back to the rules-based order.” ▶ 37:47
Johnson's most contrarian macro call: the US is more likely to transition from a republic to an empire than to yield hegemonic leadership to another power — he uses the historical analogy of Rome's transition from republic to empire (130-60 BC) as the most applicable historical parallel to today.
contrarian🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesChinaRome
So what: If the US-as-empire thesis is correct, institutions that constrain executive power will weaken, military spending will remain structurally high, and 'USD dies' scenarios are extremely premature.
“I think it's more likely we see the fall of the republic and the rise of the empire than the fall of the empire and some new power takeover.” ▶ 34:46
The Iran conflict revealed that Russia and China will not come to the direct military rescue of a key ally (Iran) despite geopolitical alignment — a significant intelligence finding with implications for the credibility of adversary security guarantees in future conflicts.
data_point🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highRussiaChinaIranBRICS
So what: The BRICS security umbrella is weaker than advertised; countries betting on Russian or Chinese protection in a crisis with the US should not trust it will materialize.
“We also found out that Russia and China will not come into the rescue of one of their biggest allies despite the fact that they need Iran.” ▶ 18:52
The Iran conflict achieved several US strategic intelligence objectives beyond the stated nuclear goal: confirmed Iran's military capabilities (including a missile that can reach Europe), verified Gulf states deepened ties with the US rather than pivoting away, and exposed OPEC's diminishing relevance.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumIranOPECGulf statesUnited StatesEurope
So what: Even an 'incomplete' military campaign can yield strategic information that reshapes alliance calculus and future deterrence; OPEC's declining relevance is now an empirical fact, not just a forecast.
Closing the Strait benefited both the US and Iran, but the US used the closure as economic leverage over third-party nations to extract better terms in bilateral trade and energy deals: since Feb 26, multiple large long-term energy contracts have been signed, including a new deal with Japan signed ~4-5 days before the episode.
causal🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: mediumUnited StatesJapanStrait of Hormuzenergy contracts
So what: Countries that signed favorable energy agreements with the US during the crisis are now locked into long-term supply relationships that reinforce dollar-denominated energy trade.
“The US is not only negotiating with Iran, they are negotiating with the rest of the world. And when the rest of the world is under pressure, the US can exact better terms on whatever deals that they're negotiating.” ▶ 20:16
Johnson distinguishes two distinct US global strategies: the 'greater good / multilateral' approach (Keynesian, post-WWII, UN/WTO/World Bank) versus the 'America First / bilateral' approach — and argues both have the same end goal (US on top) but completely different execution and side-effect profiles for the rest of the world.
framework🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highUnited StatesUNWTOWorld Bank
So what: Understanding which strategic mode the US is in determines how to think about dollar flows, alliance formation, and EM vulnerability; the current mode favors dollar strength and bilateral energy deals.
Market Technicals & Investment Opportunities 7
National defense is Johnson's highest-conviction investment theme: every country will need to rearm, and money will be spent on defense regardless of fiscal constraints — he has launched a private fund specifically targeting the US Office of Strategic Capital to invest in this sector.
thesis🗣 Brent Johnsonconviction: highdefense contractorsOffice of Strategic CapitalSantiago Capital
So what: Global defense spending is a structural multi-year theme; defense contractors in both the US and allied nations should see sustained demand regardless of the economic cycle.
“National defense is I think probably going to be the biggest investment opportunity any of us have ever seen or one of the biggest opportunities any of us have ever seen both in the United States and abroad.” ▶ 41:58
SpaceX IPO lock-up structure: if SpaceX trades above the IPO price for 5 of 10 days during a window starting in early July, 10% of insider lock-up shares unlock immediately — Townsend flags this as a potential near-term overhang risk alongside OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs absorbing massive capital.
causal🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumSpaceXOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Three mega-IPOs (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic) plus early lock-up expirations could create significant secondary supply pressure on risk assets as early as mid-July.
Townsend expects a sector rotation out of the last 6 months' winners (energy stocks and semiconductors) into laggards (financials, defense contractors, industrials) as the oil crisis narrative recedes and market breadth widens.
prediction🗣 Patrick Sznaconviction: mediumS&P 500energy stockssemiconductorsfinancialsdefense
So what: A broadening of market breadth would reward equal-weight or value tilts over growth/energy concentration; specific beneficiaries include financials and defense.
Kevin Worsh's first FOMC dot plot as Fed Chair already signals a hike is more likely than a cut as the first policy action — market pricing for rapid cuts under Worsh was wrong; Townsend says gold could face sharp pressure at the September FOMC if the Fed hikes.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WorshgoldFOMC
So what: The 'new Fed chair will cut immediately' trade is invalidated; repricing of rate-cut expectations is a headwind for gold in the near term and supportive of the dollar.
Uranium remains strategically bullish long-term (driven by AI data center power demand) but is in a seasonal soft patch; Townsend expects the market to re-engage around the World Nuclear Association conference in London in early September.
prediction🗣 Erik Townsendconviction: mediumuraniumWorld Nuclear AssociationAI data centers
So what: Late August/early September is the seasonal entry point for uranium equities; the sector's current chart distribution could convert to accumulation near the WNA conference catalyst.
The USD/JPY rate is quietly breaking above 160 — a level that previously triggered multiple Bank of Japan interventions — despite the BOJ raising interest rates with almost no market reaction; this implies a whole new leg down for the yen may be beginning.
prediction🗣 Patrick Sznaconviction: mediumUSD/JPYBank of JapanJPY
So what: USD/JPY breaking decisively above 160 with no BOJ intervention effectiveness is a structural yen weakness signal; long USD/JPY or short JPY-funded carry-trade reversals.
≈ “The 160 level on the USNY has been a rock solid overhead resistance where we have seen numerous interventions happen in the past and yet here we are quietly crawling above that level.” paraphrase
Gold's near-term outlook: the 4.5% bounce to ~$4,321 on the Iran deal has not produced a bullish trend reversal; Townsend is watching for gold to stop making lower lows and for structural accumulation signals, but won't rule out a retest of prior lows; a genuine bull market in gold likely awaits a pivot in rates and inflation expectations.
prediction🗣 Patrick Sznaconviction: mediumgoldFederal Reserve
So what: Do not chase the gold bounce; wait for structural accumulation evidence and a Fed pivot signal before sizing into a new gold bull market position.

Lightspeed 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

From Memecoins to Markets: Solana's Next Trade58 min37 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Lightspeed analysts discuss Solana's pivot from meme coins to tokenized equities, with Backpack's one-for-one fungible SpaceX shares as the headline catalyst; they assess market structure fragmentation risks, the Jito/JTX front-end opportunity, and the long-run convergence thesis where tokens simply become the superior issuance rail for equities.
Tokenized Equities on Solana: SpaceX as Catalyst · 8DEX Market Structure: AMMs vs. Prop Desks for Tokenized Assets · 5Jito / JTX: MEV Decline and the Pivot to Front-End Trading · 5Token vs. Equity Convergence Thesis · 6Token Bootstrapping Value vs. Governance Failure Modes · 5Prediction Markets: Geographic User Base and Sports Volume · 2Macro Context and Equity Issuance Surge · 4Backpack Token (BP) / Dual Token-Equity Risk · 2
Tokenized Equities on Solana: SpaceX as Catalyst 8
Backpack's tokenized SpaceX shares are one-for-one fungible with brokerage equities — KYC'd users can transfer them to and from traditional brokerages — making this structurally different from prior wrapped-asset products with uncertainty about the underlying.
thesis🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: highBackpackSpaceXSolanaDaylight
So what: Fungibility removes the central counterparty uncertainty discount; if this model scales, it positions Backpack as the compliance layer between on-chain DeFi and regulated equity markets.
“this is essentially the start of something very important. And yeah, then we can look into, I guess, like DEX dynamics and stuff like that as well, but the fact that this is now fungible” ▶ 10:17
Backpack's tokenized SpaceX did ~$15M in spot volume on day 1, ~$7M on day 2, and ~$13M on day 3 — meaningful but dwarfed by Hyperliquid's perp market for SpaceX, which reportedly did over $1B in volume on IPO day.
data_point🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: highBackpackSpaceXHyperliquidSolana
So what: The perp market is ~70x larger than tokenized spot on day 1, signaling that derivatives infrastructure is far ahead of regulated spot tokenization; spot upside is large if regulatory clarity arrives.
Solana accounts for approximately 98% of tokenized-equity spot trading activity across all of crypto, making it the de-facto chain for real-world asset equity trading.
data_point🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: highSolanatokenized equities
So what: Near-total market share in a nascent category is a strong moat signal; watch whether Ethereum-based competitors (e.g., Ondo on Base) can erode this.
“as for actual spot activity for tokenized equities, they've been about like 98% of activity for all of crypto” ▶ 9:42
Backpack's strategy mirrors the Circle/Tether playbook: push into a regulatory gray zone with a compliant product, accumulate regulatory hires (Can Sun, Mark Wetjen), and wait for the framework to catch up rather than waiting for permission to launch.
framework🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: highBackpackCircleTetherCan SunMark Wetjen
So what: If the regulatory analogy holds, first-mover compliant tokenized-equity platforms could dominate the way Tether dominates stablecoin market share; Backpack's regulatory hires are a leading indicator.
“what Backpack is doing now with one-for-one fungible assets, um it's a similar dynamic. And they have a range of key regulation hires like Can Sun and Mark Wetjen” ▶ 12:05
Superstate is partnering with Backpack to bring on-chain IPOs to market — a step toward disintermediating traditional investment banks as IPO underwriters, though the panelists note this is still unproven and hard to access.
thesis🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: mediumBackpackSuperstateSolana
So what: On-chain primary issuance would be a structural threat to bulge-bracket banks' IPO revenue; watch Robinhood (newly approved as IPO underwriter) and on-chain platforms converging on this opportunity.
Pre-IPO tokenized SpaceX (via pre-stocks SPV wrappers) is now trading at a discount to post-IPO spot price because of a ~6-month lockup on underlying share delivery and SPV fees, illustrating that pre-IPO spot tokens carry risks absent in perps or post-IPO spot.
causal🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highSpaceXpre-stocksSolana
So what: The discount creates an arb opportunity but also signals structural risk; investors in any pre-IPO tokenized asset should price in lockup duration, SPV fees, and share-delivery uncertainty before buying at par.
There are at least five distinct SpaceX token variants on Solana (Backpack Securities, X Stocks, Ondo tokenized SpaceX, pre-stocks, TSBPX) creating harmful liquidity fragmentation and user-experience friction that could deter institutional adoption.
causal🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: highSpaceXSolanaBackpackOndoX Stockstokens.xyz
So what: Fragmentation is the principal near-term risk to tokenized equity volumes; a standardization layer (e.g., Solana Foundation's tokens.xyz) or a dominant winner like Backpack's fungible model could be the key de-risking catalyst.
The Clarity Act is expected to enable institutional players to legally touch crypto-native tokenized equities; the panelists view its passage as the single largest unlock for on-chain equity volumes on Solana.
thesis🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: mediumClarity ActSolanaBackpackJTX
So what: Clarity Act passage is a binary catalyst for the entire Solana tokenized-equity stack (Backpack spot, JTX front-end, Radium/Meteora pools); monitor legislative progress as a lead indicator.
DEX Market Structure: AMMs vs. Prop Desks for Tokenized Assets 5
Traditional passive AMMs (Raydium, Meteora, Orca) currently dominate tokenized-equity spot liquidity on Solana, not prop AMMs, even though prop desks would theoretically have an edge quoting assets with live Nasdaq price feeds.
contrarian🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: mediumRaydiumMeteoraOrcaSolanaAlpha Q
So what: If prop AMMs eventually enter once volume thresholds are met, passive AMMs will likely lose market share in the largest-cap tokenized equities — a negative for Raydium/Meteora revenue from those specific pairs.
“these are great examples of something that a prop AMM would want to quote, right? It's like you have a very live, fresh, like up-to-date price from, you know, the Nasdaq” ▶ 23:37
Prop AMMs currently quote mainly SOL-stablecoin and stable-to-stable pairs; their majority volume does NOT come from equity or long-tail assets, but recent high-bridge volume growth on Solana is prompting them to quote hi-bridge/USD pairs profitably.
data_point🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: highSolanaprop AMMshi-bridge
So what: The hi-bridge / USD pair is a real-time test case: if prop desks become profitable quoting newly bridged assets, they will apply the same playbook to tokenized equities once those hit critical volume, compressing spreads and squeezing passive AMM fee revenue.
Long-tail tokenized equities (the 500th stock by market cap, or obscure names) will likely stay in passive AMM pools (Raydium, Meteora) permanently, because prop desks cannot justify infrastructure costs for low-volume assets — mirroring the meme-coin dynamic.
thesis🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: highRaydiumMeteoraOrcaSolanaBackpack
So what: Raydium and Meteora are the structural beneficiaries of the long tail of tokenized equities regardless of who wins the blue-chip pairs — a durable revenue stream if Backpack lists all US equities.
“I guess like obviously they did very well during the meme coin activity. And if we do see like FTX Backpack essentially list all the stocks on the United States Stock Exchange... pools like Radium and Meteor could do quite well” ▶ 20:25
Jump Trading, Securitize, and Jupiter are reportedly working together on tokenized equity market-making on Solana, potentially creating an institutional-grade liquidity arrangement similar to project-token deals at launch.

So what: If Jump provides institutional liquidity for tokenized equities via Jupiter, it would accelerate the shift of DeFi volume from meme coins to regulated assets and entrench Jupiter as the blue-chip routing layer.
Prop AMMs may require KYC infrastructure to quote tokenized equities (because brokerage KYC is a prerequisite for holding the underlying), making the cost of entry for quoting these assets structurally higher than for native crypto assets.
causal🗣 Joe (advisor team)conviction: mediumprop AMMsBackpackSolanatokenized equities
So what: KYC friction is a moat for Backpack (already has the infrastructure) and a barrier for permissionless prop AMMs; compliance-native participants gain structural advantages in quoting these markets.
“they have to KYC maybe on a on a brokerage or something like that and it's not worth the hassle until it's provably profitable”
Jito / JTX: MEV Decline and the Pivot to Front-End Trading 5
Jito's core MEV/tips business is structurally declining as Solana's microstructure has shifted toward priority fees; Solana core developers and the Jito team itself envision eventually sunsetting Jito tips in favor of a single fee mechanism.
causal🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: highJitoSolanaJTO
So what: Jito's block-building revenue is a melting ice cube; the JTO token's long-term value is conditional on JTX front-end success — investors should treat the token as a bet on a product-stage startup, not a stable MEV income stream.
≈ “Jito tips have been trending down and that's not necessarily Jito's fault, but is simply Solana's microstructure has shifted toward priority fees” paraphrase
JTX (Jito's front-end trading platform) targets a gap identified as 'no institutional-grade front-end on Solana today,' offering resting limits, bracket orders, OCO, TWAPs, and a maker-priority plugin — differentiated from existing retail-focused interfaces.
thesis🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: highJitoJTXSolanaJTO
So what: If tokenized equity volumes scale, JTX is positioned as the order-routing and execution layer for institutional flows; this is a high-optionality bet contingent on Clarity Act and Backpack success.
Bull-case moon math for JTX: if Solana DEX volumes triple from ~$50B to ~$150B monthly and JTX captures 20% at ~2bps with a 20% router take, annual revenue would be ~$50-60M vs. a current annualized run rate of ~$5M — implying the current JTO multiple (~150x) could compress to under 10x.
framework🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: mediumJitoJTXJTOSolana
So what: At 150x annualized revenue, JTO is pricing in a successful DEX volume tripling AND 20% market share for JTX — both aggressive assumptions; position sizing should reflect the binary nature of this outcome.
“if we see Solana DEX volumes roughly triple from around 50 billion to 150 billion and say JTX gets 20% of that and say they are able to clip around two basis points... that roughly equates to uh around 50 60 million dollars per year versus the current annualized run rate of around 5 million” ▶ 51:42
JTO token appreciated roughly 3x in the month prior to this episode, driven by market recognition of the JTX front-end pivot; panelists see a pullback risk from this compressed timeframe move.
prediction🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: mediumJTOJitoJTX
So what: The rapid re-rating has pulled forward much of the easy gain; risk/reward for new entries is skewed unless Clarity Act passage or Backpack volume milestones provide a fresh catalyst.
“wouldn't be surprised if we see a pullback from here given such a large move over the last couple weeks from around I think it's like roughly 3x in the last month or so” ▶ 50:49
Jito's infrastructure edge — expertise in transaction inclusion and the maker-priority plugin — gives JTX a structural advantage over generic front-ends in providing best execution for institutional tokenized-equity traders.
thesis🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: highJitoJTXSolana
So what: Jito's infrastructure stack is a defensible moat for JTX that generic UI competitors cannot easily replicate; watch for institutional flow migration to JTX as a confirmation signal.
Token vs. Equity Convergence Thesis 6
The long-run end state is that tokens and equities converge into a single instrument: the token is simply a superior issuance rail (programmable, 24/7, global, transparent) for what is economically an equity, not a separate asset class.
thesis🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: high
So what: If this convergence plays out, the 'token vs. equity' debate is a category error; the relevant question becomes which issuance platforms and compliance rails win, not whether tokens survive.
“I think it's not a matter of like we're going to see less tokens and more equity, but rather I think what we're all getting at is that they will both converge at some point and maybe the token is just a technology” ▶ 0:00
In a fully tokenized equity world, the only remaining distinction for native crypto tokens (vs. tokenized equities) would be for true digital commodities like Bitcoin or L1 tokens that represent network participation rights rather than corporate ownership.
framework🗣 Sam (research team)conviction: mediumBitcoinSolana
So what: This framework implies a bifurcation: digital commodities (BTC, SOL) retain a distinct identity while the vast majority of app-layer tokens will either become tokenized equities or become worthless — a bearish signal for governance tokens without clear equity rights.
“the ideal end state in 10 20 years is equities and tokens are essentially the same thing as one completely synergistic system. Uh with maybe a distinction for like Bitcoin or an L1 in regards to being like a digital commodity” ▶ 46:16
The Trump administration may ease US IPO regulations, potentially allowing companies to go public earlier and making tokens as pre-IPO access vehicles less necessary — a structural headwind for the 'token-as-early-access' investment thesis.
causal🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: lowTrump administrationSEC
So what: If IPOs become easier and earlier, the liquidity premium that tokens command as pre-IPO vehicles compresses; investors using tokens as pre-IPO proxies should monitor regulatory easing in public equity markets.
Historical pattern: when a crypto company has both a token and equity, the token has been zeroed in virtually every observed case — via acquisition of the equity while the token becomes worthless — making the dual token-equity structure a strong bearish signal for token holders.
causal🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: high
So what: Any project that issues both a token and retains equity (without explicit conversion rights) is a structural token short; only explicit conversion mechanisms (like Pump.fun's ACE round) have broken this pattern.
“the largely the history that we've seen so far with these dual structures is that um the token effectively gets zeroed in most scenarios, right?” ▶ 31:20
Pump.fun's ACE round pioneered a token-to-equity conversion mechanism for accredited US investors, becoming one of the first examples where token holders received actual equity rather than being zeroed out; Backpack appears to be following a similar path.
anecdote🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highPump.funBackpackBP token
So what: The Pump.fun ACE round is the closest comparable for Backpack's BP token conversion promise; investors in BP should study Pump.fun's accredited-investor restrictions and one-time conversion structure as a risk template.
Second-order DeFi opportunity from tokenized equities: using on-chain equities as collateral in lending/borrowing protocols (money markets) — an already common TradFi use case (borrowing against equities) that has barely materialized on-chain despite Kamino and Jupiter listing tokenized assets.
thesis🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: mediumKaminoJupiterSolanatokenized equities
So what: Lending protocols that accept tokenized equities as collateral are a second-derivative beneficiary of the RWA expansion; Kamino is the natural watch candidate on Solana for this use case.
“I'm really curious to see if eventually like for instance people use equities as collateral on money markets and borrow against that. I think that's a very like low hanging fruit use case” ▶ 46:57
Token Bootstrapping Value vs. Governance Failure Modes 5
Tokens remain valuable bootstrapping tools for products that have strong fundamentals but insufficient user acquisition: offering a token (or future revenue share) is a way to sacrifice future cash flows to acquire sticky early users, which can be positive EV if those users compound growth.
framework🗣 Carlos (research team), Sam (research team)conviction: highMeta DAOPhoenix DEXBee Chi
So what: Phoenix DEX (no token, promising perps product) is cited as a live case study in leaving growth on the table; a token launch there could be a meaningful volume catalyst and is a scenario to watch.
“if you have a very good product, but you're struggling to get users, um offering a token or essentially future cash flows of the business... can be a very good way to bring in users” ▶ 35:26
A Meta DAO project (Bee Chi neobank) reportedly saw ~10x user growth after its ICO on Meta DAO, illustrating the quantifiable bootstrapping power of token-driven community alignment.
anecdote🗣 Carlos (research team)conviction: mediumMeta DAOBee Chi
So what: The 10x user figure, if accurate, implies token-launch user CAC can be dramatically lower than traditional growth spend; worth tracking Bee Chi's retention metrics to validate whether the users were sticky.
Crypto governance moved in the wrong direction historically by having token holders vote on granular operational decisions (inflation rates, validator onboarding); the correct structure mirrors corporate governance — executives make most decisions with token holders voting only on major items like executive selection.
framework🗣 Sam (research team), Host (Danny)conviction: high
So what: Protocols moving toward a CEO-led decision structure (rather than broad token governance) should be re-rated positively for execution ability; watch for governance minimization as a quality signal.
“I feel like what um equities already have in place is like the correct structure. Like you need the CEO, a few executives making the key decisions” ▶ 43:06
The prior era's push for rapid token distribution and community governance was largely a regulatory defense mechanism against SEC action — now that the regulatory environment has shifted, protocols are visibly and unapologetically re-centralizing decision-making while retaining revenue-share tokens.
causal🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highSEC
So what: This structural re-centralization is a net positive for protocol execution quality but implies governance tokens (without revenue rights) are more exposed than before — another filter for token quality screening.
“a lot of that uh movement was a bit um of an a negative for tokens in general because it just the the incentives were in place such that you were pushing the project to quickly like give up ownership”
Decentralized infrastructure protocols (e.g., DePIN networks) are one of the few categories where a truly decentralized token model makes sense on first principles, because operator/user economics can be self-sustaining without centralized decision-making.
framework🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: medium
So what: DePIN tokens are the sub-category most defensible against the 'tokens should be equities' critique; they may command a structural premium over governance-only tokens as the convergence narrative plays out.
Prediction Markets: Geographic User Base and Sports Volume 2
Polymarket is seeing a significant uptick in World Cup trading volumes while Kalshi historically dominates US sports betting; the divergence tracks each platform's user base geography — Polymarket is more international, Kalshi more US-centric.
causal🗣 Host (Danny), panelistconviction: highPolymarketKalshi
So what: Geographic user base is the primary driver of prediction market category dominance; Polymarket's international base is a structural advantage for global sporting events, while Kalshi owns the US sports calendar — both can grow without zero-summing each other.
Prediction market trading volumes have continued to grind upward across platforms and are increasingly dominated by sports gambling rather than the political or geopolitical categories that DeFi-native users initially expected to lead.
contrarian🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highPolymarketKalshi
So what: Sports gambling as the dominant prediction market use case re-frames competitive analysis: Polymarket and Kalshi compete less with each other and more with regulated sportsbooks (FanDuel, DraftKings); monitor whether they gain regulated status.
Macro Context and Equity Issuance Surge 4
2026 is shaping up as a record year for net equity issuance, driven by expected IPOs from SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, and AI/semiconductor names; the surge is projected to blow past even 2021 IPO volumes and is being described as 'ending the era of stock scarcity.'
data_point🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highSpaceXOpenAIAnthropicEric Balchunas
So what: A record IPO wave is a direct volume catalyst for tokenized equity platforms on Solana; each high-profile IPO (especially AI names) is a potential demand spike for on-chain trading analogous to the SpaceX event.
The crypto market rally at the time of recording was partly attributed to Trump securing a deal with Iran, easing oil markets, which lifted equities and pulled crypto higher — illustrating the increasing macro correlation of crypto to risk assets.
causal🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: mediumTrumpIrancrypto
So what: As crypto becomes more correlated to macro risk-on/risk-off via equity market linkages (especially as tokenized equities grow on-chain), crypto hedges using oil/geopolitical positioning become more relevant.
Robinhood has been approved as an IPO underwriter, signaling that fintech and crypto-adjacent platforms are directly encroaching on the investment banking IPO business — a structural threat to bulge-bracket underwriting fees.
thesis🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: mediumRobinhood
So what: Robinhood's underwriter status plus on-chain IPO platforms (Backpack/Superstate) represent a two-front attack on traditional IPO intermediation; monitor for first on-chain primary issuance as a watershed event.
Solana Foundation is actively pushing asset trading on Solana via Frontier Traders, a campaign platform with trading leaderboards (e.g., SpaceX leaderboard with a $25K prize pool), marking a shift from 'build fast tech' to actively directing trading flow to the chain.
anecdote🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highSolana FoundationFrontier TradersSpaceXSunrise dashboard
So what: Foundation-sponsored trading campaigns are a near-term volume catalyst; watch the Sunrise dashboard data to quantify how much of Backpack tokenized volume is incentive-driven vs. organic.
Backpack Token (BP) / Dual Token-Equity Risk 2
Backpack has a live token (BP) and has also signaled plans to IPO, creating a dual token-equity structure historically associated with tokens being zeroed out; the key mitigant is reported indications that BP stakers would receive equity allocation at IPO — but terms are vague and not legally enforceable.
contrarian🗣 Host (Danny)conviction: highBackpackBP token
So what: BP token is a high-conviction short if Backpack IPOs without a formal, legally binding token-to-equity conversion; it is a high-conviction long only if a Pump.fun-style ACE conversion with clear terms is announced.
“having two assets, equity plus token, um, typically is like not a good sign in the sense that if you're a token holder, you're probably a bit worried” ▶ 26:05
The Backpack team would need equity holders to willingly dilute themselves for BP token holders with no legal enforcement mechanism — making the conversion entirely dependent on team integrity and goodwill, not contractual obligation.
causal🗣 Toma / Joe (advisor team)conviction: highBackpackBP token
So what: BP token carries team-integrity risk as its primary risk factor above market or execution risk; due diligence should focus on whether conversion terms are codified in any legal agreement.
“the equity has to be willing to take on this token that, you know, has a future state where it's worthless, and you have to be willing to be diluted for it. So, I think in the case of Backpack or like a Pump Cayd, you had equity that was willing to essentially dilute themselves in in good faith cuz there really is no legal enforcement to the token” ▶ 30:50

Johnny Harris 1 episode(s) · 36 insights

Oligarchy is worse than you think49 min36 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Drawing on Jeffrey Winters's new book 'The Blind Spot,' the video argues that oligarchy is not a foreign or conspiratorial phenomenon but an institutionalized feature of US democracy: a vast, mundane 'wealth defense industry' of lawyers, accountants, and lobbyists costs governments ~$500B/year in lost global tax revenues, forces average Americans to pay ~15% more in taxes, and is structurally protected by constitutional design choices made in 1787 that have since been exported worldwide.
Wealth Defense Industry: Scale, Structure & Mechanism · 6Quantified Costs of Wealth Defense: Tax Gap & Redistribution · 5Regulatory Capture & IRS Enforcement Failure · 4Political Science of Oligarchic Influence: The Gilens-Page Framework · 5Constitutional Design as Oligarchic Infrastructure · 4Historical Precedent: Gilded Age to Progressive Era Cycle · 5Current Oligarchic Actors & Specific Policy Levers · 7
Wealth Defense Industry: Scale, Structure & Mechanism 6
A loose network of millions of lawyers, accountants, lobbyists, tax experts, estate planners, and money managers — collectively called the 'wealth defense industry' — exists solely to prevent governments from taxing ultra-high-net-worth individuals, and it is far larger and more routine than any cabal of named oligarchs.
framework🗣 Jeffrey Winters (scholar, Northwestern) via Adam (producer)conviction: highwealth defense industryJeffrey WintersMossack Fonseca
So what: The industry is a persistent, structurally entrenched demand-driver for financial secrecy services, offshore jurisdictions, and tax-law arbitrage — essentially a recession-resistant business model worth monitoring for investment in private-wealth-services platforms, offshore financial centers, and regulatory-arbitrage plays.
“A loose network of millions of lawyers, accountants, lobbyists, tax experts, political operatives, money managers, think tanks, estate planners, all working for one simple goal: to keep the government from taking rich people's money.” ▶ 9:53
The core mechanism of the wealth defense industry is 'muddying the link between an asset and its owner': governments can only tax an asset if they know who owns it, so the industry creates maze-like corporate structures — shell companies, Cayman funds, Hong Kong subsidiaries, offshore trusts — that break that link.
framework🗣 Adam (producer) / Kimberly Hoang (academic)conviction: highCayman IslandsHong Kongshell companiesMossack FonsecaPanama Papers
So what: Opacity of beneficial ownership is the structural moat for this industry; any regulatory regime requiring transparent beneficial ownership (e.g., Corporate Transparency Act) is an existential threat to it and a potential catalyst for large-scale capital repatriation or re-pricing of offshore assets.
“You can see the asset but you don't know who owns it. And you can see the rich person, but you don't really know what they own.” ▶ 15:59
Offshore wealth defense structures are so compartmentalized that each individual professional only has visibility into their own slice of the structure; no single actor sees — or is liable for — the whole architecture.
causal🗣 Kimberly Hoang (academic)conviction: highKimberly HoangCayman IslandsHong Kong
So what: Enforcement is virtually impossible without inter-jurisdictional data sharing; regulatory efforts targeting single nodes (one advisor, one jurisdiction) are easily routed around — raising the bar for any meaningful tax enforcement reform.
“Every single person only has a view of the structure that they worked on.” ▶ 17:05
The wealth defense industry recruits the most academically talented graduates — not the richest — because elite intellectual horsepower is the core input for structuring complex tax-avoidance arrangements; this creates a brain drain away from public-interest work.
causal🗣 Kimberly Hoangconviction: highKimberly Hoang
So what: The public sector cannot out-compete private wealth management on compensation, structurally limiting regulatory capacity — an asymmetry that is unlikely to close without major legislative intervention and suggests continued underenforcement even with nominally higher IRS budgets.
“The state in the United States now doesn't have the capacity to pay regulators the salaries that they can make in the private sector.” ▶ 23:13
Tax avoidance through offshore structures is only economically viable above a threshold of accumulated capital; it is explicitly an echelon of services unavailable to ordinary wealthy people, let alone the middle class.
data_point🗣 Adam (producer)conviction: highPanama PapersMossack Fonseca
So what: This creates a nonlinear tax-incidence curve: below a capital threshold you face standard enforcement; above it you effectively operate in a parallel tax system. Investors in wealth-management platforms targeting ultra-HNW clients are buying into a structurally privileged regulatory regime.
“The key thing about tax avoidance is you can only afford to do that once you've amassed a certain amount of capital.” ▶ 15:22
Panama Papers (leaked from Mossack Fonseca) revealed that the client base of offshore wealth-defense firms consisted primarily of legitimate ultra-wealthy individuals, not just criminals — the headline criminal angle obscured the more important story of systemic elite tax avoidance.
contrarian🗣 Adam (producer)conviction: highPanama PapersMossack Fonseca
So what: The 'criminal offshore' narrative is a misdirection: the vast majority of offshore assets belong to technically legal (if morally contested) wealth defense by the ultra-rich; policy targeting only illegal flows misses the bulk of the problem.
Quantified Costs of Wealth Defense: Tax Gap & Redistribution 5
