So what: If compute fungibility becomes a reality (through standards or a new capability layer), it would commoditize cloud-specific lock-in and fundamentally reprice hyperscaler AI infrastructure margins.
≈ “The goal is to try to make megaflops flow like megawatts.” paraphrase
Regulatory & Community Risk for Data Centers 4
Up to 20% of US data centers under development in 2026 are reportedly at risk of not receiving the community support needed to come online, due to permitting, power grid, and environmental concerns — though Midha acknowledged the number may be somewhat overstated.
data_point🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: mediumAmp
So what: Community permitting risk is an underpriced tail risk for hyperscaler and neo-cloud capex plans; data center REITs and infrastructure operators with unresolved community opposition face project delays.
≈ “Up to 20% of all data centers this year in the US, my understanding is are at risk… of not getting the community support they need to get brought up.” paraphrase
Scott Nolan (founder of General Matter) proposed that data centers charge an incremental $0.50/hour on top of ~$4/hour compute costs and distribute that directly to host communities as cash — Midha says he would pay it because it reduces reliability risk.
framework🗣 Anjney Midha (attributing Scott Nolan)conviction: mediumGeneral MatterAmp
So what: This community-revenue-sharing model is a potential policy template that, if adopted, would add ~12.5% to compute costs but could unlock stranded capacity and reduce regulatory delays — a net positive for labs and a cost investors should model.
≈ “If you're having to bring up a new data center in a new community, why not just say we're going to charge $4.50 an hour and that marginal increase, we just literally take that and give it to the local community as cash.” paraphrase
Midha explicitly warns that regulators will eventually audit AI infrastructure operators who 'move fast and break things,' and that companies without long track records or proper community engagement will be exposed.
prediction🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAmp
So what: AI infrastructure players relying on speed over compliance face meaningful regulatory tail risk; preferring data center partners with 20+ year track records is an emerging risk management differentiator.
≈ “There will be audits. There will be investigations. And when the regulators come, I don't know when it's going to be. The folks who are moving fast and breaking things in the name of AI progress better be prepared.” paraphrase
Midha argues that traditional, established US data center operators with 20+ year track records (not neo-clouds) are the most reliable compute partners, explicitly contrasting them with neo-cloud providers who 'sponsor happy hours at NeurIPS' but lack infrastructure maturity.
contrarian🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highCoreWeaveLambda LabsAmp
So what: This signals durable demand for traditional colocation/data center operators (e.g. Equinix, Digital Realty, CyrusOne) over neo-cloud names in an environment of rising regulatory and reliability scrutiny.
≈ “There are really good, reliable, trusted data center providers in America who've been around 20-plus years. I love those folks. They know how to run land. They can run power. They have credit histories.” paraphrase
AI Lab Culture, Hardship & the Failure Mode of Over-Capitalized Labs 6
Many AI labs today have sufficient capital and compute but are failing to ship because of cultural rot — Midha's diagnosis is that culture is not a moat but a fragile garden requiring daily tending through consistent mission-aligned actions.
thesis🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAmp
So what: Investors evaluating AI lab bets should weight cultural cohesion and founder mission-alignment more heavily than compute or capital position; labs that raised too much too fast without defining a P0 are the highest-risk category.
“Culture is not a set of beliefs. It's a set of actions.” ▶ 51:07
Labs that raised too much money too early without being forced to define a singular P0 (primary objective) develop the most brittle cultures and often fail to reach capability takeoff, even when well-resourced.
causal🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAmpAnthropicOpenAI
So what: The current wave of billion-dollar AI lab fundraises may be creating a cohort of well-funded but culturally fragile organizations — watch for attrition signals as leading indicators of approaching failure.
≈ “There's so much money going to these labs, there's no hardship. There's no 21 knows. And that in hindsight was a feature not a bug for Anthropic.” paraphrase
Anthropic's P0 from day one was coding — predicated on the thesis that cracking coding is necessary and sufficient for cracking AGI, because coding is a generally powerful capability that can accelerate all computer work.
causal🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Anthropic's coding focus was a deliberate architectural choice, not a market-fit discovery — this gives it a durable head start that competitors pursuing broader capability targets may struggle to overcome.
“Their P0 from day one was coding. The reason mechanism system there was if we crack coding, then we will crack AGI.” ▶ 54:23
Anthropic achieved what Midha calls 'capability takeoff' in October 2025 with the training checkpoint that became Claude 3.5/3.7 (released publicly in December 2025), and this was recognized by insiders in the community as a phase transition, not an incremental improvement.
anecdote🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAnthropicCognition
So what: This suggests Anthropic's coding dominance is rooted in a specific training run that represents a qualitative threshold — rivals need to identify and replicate the conditions of that run, not merely scale compute.
“Anthropic basically achieved takeoff in October of last year. That training run… to those of us in the community, especially once post training was done and it was released in December.” ▶ 46:47
Anthropic's capital efficiency has been dramatically superior to OpenAI's — Midha says the gap is 'not even close' — because early fundraising difficulty forced extreme resource discipline that compounded into a performance and culture advantage.
contrarian🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Anthropic's burn rate efficiency relative to OpenAI is a publicly trackable signal; if true, Anthropic's path to sustainable unit economics is materially shorter than consensus estimates.
“It's not even… yeah. But it's so clear, right? Like how to output max for the world. They have been prepared.” ▶ 49:27
Midha frames Anthropic's safety culture as a compounding competitive moat — not because of any one decision, but because the daily reiteration of safety as a value created organizational memory, external accountability, and legal protection that competitors cannot easily replicate.
framework🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAnthropic
So what: Anthropic's safety brand may translate into enterprise procurement preference as AI liability becomes a legal concern — a durable revenue moat that pure-capability labs lack.
“How do you prevent against that? Well, day in day out, you say safety, safety, safety, safety. And when you start deviating from that, you have the team hold you accountable.”
DeepMind / Google Research Hoarding & Market Failure 3
DeepMind operates a 6-month publication embargo where any paper that a business team flags as commercially interesting is suppressed indefinitely — resulting in adverse selection in published research (only work deemed commercially worthless is published).
contrarian🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highDeepMindAlphabetGoogle
So what: DeepMind papers at NeurIPS/ICLR are not representative of its best work; investors relying on academic output to gauge DeepMind's capabilities are systematically underestimating its frontier position — and simultaneously, the commercially valuable research is locked away from the broader ecosystem.
≈ “The stuff that gets published is the stuff that's not good enough. So there's an adverse selection problem, basically.” paraphrase
Midha views DeepMind's research hoarding as a negative externality and market failure — extraordinary researchers produce work that 'never sees the light of day,' and Amp's Foundry arm exists partly to unlock this trapped research by spinning out misaligned researchers.
thesis🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highDeepMindAlphabetAmpFoundry
So what: There is a structural pipeline of research talent exiting DeepMind and similar labs (Gnomics example) that represents investable deal flow for specialized AI spinout investors; Amp/Foundry is systematically targeting this flow.
“How much extraordinary research has happened inside of DeepMind over the last 10 years… so much of that has never seen the light of day.” ▶ 14:57
Google's internal credit-based compute scheduling (Borg/Brain Marketplace) led to Google missing GPT-3/ChatGPT — because centralized command systems are sometimes required for breakthrough bets that a credit market would underfund.
causal🗣 Anjney Midha (contextualizing with interviewer)conviction: mediumGoogleOpenAIDeepMindBorg
So what: Distributed compute scheduling markets are powerful for utilization but can fail to fund long-shot bets that require concentrated resource commitment — a fundamental tension in any ISO-style compute grid model, including Amp's.
Alternative Silicon & Compute Fungibility 4
Matrox (chip startup, CEO Reiner Pope, former Google Palm co-founder) chose to build its chips to Nvidia's reference architecture and rack form factor, deliberately piggybacking on Jensen's published standard rather than innovating on data center design.
anecdote🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highMatroxNvidiaReiner Pope
So what: Nvidia's reference architecture is becoming an industry standard — analogous to x86 for servers — and companies building to that spec reduce deployment friction; investors in alternative silicon should value standards-compatibility as a key diligence criterion.
“They picked the Nvidia reference architecture. So, the Matrox chips just plug in to any site that has an Nvidia bring-up plan.” ▶ 31:40
Matrox's primary innovation is systems co-design (logic die optimization), not data center or interconnect design — and its core risk is the trust boundary problem: outside Google, they no longer have early access to next-generation model architectures, which are needed 2 years ahead of tape-out.
framework🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highMatroxGoogleNvidiaAmp
So what: For alternative chip startups, access to frontier model architecture roadmaps is as valuable as engineering talent; Amp's role brokering this trust relationship (between labs and chip teams) is a potential durable competitive advantage.
“To do co-design well you need visibility into the next model generation as soon as possible cuz it takes two years to tape out.” ▶ 34:13
Midha sees no competitive conflict between Nvidia and alternative chip makers like Matrox because compute demand is so far ahead of Nvidia's ability to supply production capacity — the market needs more chips, not displacement.
thesis🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highNvidiaMatroxAMD
So what: This is a demand-abundance framing that justifies premium valuations across the silicon supply chain for the near term; however, it is precisely the framing that collapses in a demand correction.
“I think Nvidia's not able to meet the demand of production. So we just need more chips.” ▶ 33:39
SF Compute's effort to create standardized futures contracts for compute is something Midha wants to accelerate — he envisions SF Compute plugging into Amp's grid as a two-way demand/supply protocol.
prediction🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: mediumSF ComputeAmp
So what: Compute futures contracts (if bootstrapped) would create price discovery and hedging instruments for AI infrastructure — a new financial product category relevant to infrastructure investors and AI labs managing cost volatility.
Scientists as CEOs & Talent Philosophy 4
Midha argues that top academic researchers (e.g. Anastasios Angelopoulos of LM Arena/lmsys, Robin Rombach of Black Forest Labs, Guillaume Lample of Mistral) make better AI company CEOs than professional operators, because the discipline of competitive academic publishing requires the same performance intensity as great leadership.
contrarian🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAnastasios AngelopoulosLM ArenaBlack Forest LabsMistralGuillaume LampleRobin Rombach
So what: Investors systematically discount researcher-CEOs — Midha explicitly calls this out — suggesting a valuation arbitrage in AI spinouts led by credentialed scientists who VCs mislabel as 'just researchers.'
≈ “I would just fund researchers all day… If they've put Soda out there, they're star athletes already.” paraphrase ▶ 37:52
Dario Amodei's trajectory from scientist to CEO of a company approaching a trillion-dollar valuation in 4 years is Midha's primary counter-example to the 'researchers can't be CEOs' heuristic.
anecdote🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highDario AmodeiAnthropic
So what: Dario's arc is becoming the reference benchmark for evaluating scientist-founders in AI; comps based on operator-CEO archetypes will systematically undervalue this cohort.
“Have you met Dario? Dario is a scientist. He's gone from zero to like what will soon be a trillion-dollar company in 4 years.” ▶ 36:43
VCs systematically fail to evaluate founders as 'dynamic agents' — they put researchers in a fixed box and miss compounding, multidimensional individuals who are already both publishing and building products.
contrarian🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highLM ArenaAnastasios Angelopoulos
So what: This suggests the best AI investment opportunities are being systematically passed by by consensus VCs — contrarian early access to researcher-led labs is likely underpriced.
≈ “What I've realized is venture capitalists suck at seeing human beings as dynamic agents. Where they want to put you in a box.” paraphrase
The critical failure mode of researcher-CEOs who don't want to be CEOs is that they primarily want to publish — those who want to keep publishing but are nominally running a company are a poor bet.
framework🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAmpFoundry
So what: The key diligence question for researcher-CEO bets is: are they willing to be confrontational outside scientific discourse (with team, hiring, customers)? Publication appetite is a leading negative indicator.
“If you want to be a CEO, what you need to be willing to do is be super confrontational… to your own team, hiring, recruiting, customers.” ▶ 38:31
Periodic Labs & Scientific AI Applications 4
Periodic Labs (Amp's portfolio, hosted at interview site) is focused on AI-driven superconductor discovery — specifically room-temperature superconductors — which Midha believes would enable 'flying cars within a few years' and unlock lossless energy transmission.
thesis🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highPeriodic LabsAmpFoundry
So what: Periodic is a high-optionality deep science bet; room-temperature superconductor discovery would be a civilization-level infrastructure unlock — worth monitoring for experimental results.
Periodic Labs recently had a technical breakthrough creating a 'soda system' approximately 12 months after founding, despite not being popular with potential recruits early on — some of whom left for higher-paying jobs and then tried to return after the breakthrough, only to be rejected.
anecdote🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highPeriodic Labs
So what: A technical inflection at Periodic warrants attention; if superconductor-adjacent results are reproducible, this is a potentially investable milestone.
Midha is actively developing an end-of-life prediction AI company as his next incubation — targeting the 30%+ of Medicare/Medicaid spend on end-of-life care, using RL-based models trained on longitudinal patient data (e.g. Stanford's STRIDE dataset of 12M+ patient lives).
thesis🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highAmpFoundryStanford
So what: End-of-life prediction AI targeting Medicare/Medicaid spend is a potentially massive healthcare AI opportunity; the main bottleneck is regulatory (liability assignment for AI clinical decisions), not technical.
“Over 30% of all Medicare Medicaid spend, at least at that time, was spent on end-of-life care.” ▶ 18:04
The core technical barrier to deploying end-of-life prediction AI is not model accuracy (even simple regression models work well on large longitudinal datasets) but regulatory: US law cannot currently shift medical malpractice liability from physician to AI system.
causal🗣 Anjney Midhaconviction: highStanfordAmp
So what: Healthcare AI investors should track regulatory reform on liability assignment as the unlock event for clinical decision support AI — model performance is not the gating factor.
“You actually can't shift the burden of the wrong clinical diagnosis from the physician to the AI system.” ▶ 23:12

Bankless 1 episode(s) · 36 insights

Stablecoins Finally Found Real Yield59 min36 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Re (on-chain reinsurer) has built a protocol that channels stablecoin capital into the $1T global reinsurance market, offering depositors 12%+ yields backed by uncorrelated insurance premiums from 51 US carriers — not crypto-native feedback loops. Electric Capital's Avichal Garg argues Re represents the template for all future fintech: regulated front-end, smart-contract operations, on-chain capital markets as the back-end, giving startups a structural cost-of-capital advantage incumbents cannot replicate.
Re Protocol: Business Model & Capital Mechanics · 9Stablecoin Capital Markets: The $5T Yield-Hunting Wave · 6Fintech Disruption: On-Chain Operational Efficiency Moat · 5Lloyd's of London as the DeFi Blueprint · 4RE Token: Value Accrual & Governance Design · 3Market Size & Macro Insurance Landscape · 5Bear Market Timing & Contrarian Infrastructure Observation · 4
Re Protocol: Business Model & Capital Mechanics 9
Re is an on-chain reinsurer that backs 35 US insurance carriers with $500M in business today, targeting $1B within 7 months, with fewer than 12 employees — a radically higher operating leverage than legacy reinsurers with tens of thousands of staff.
data_point🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRereinsurance
So what: Sub-12-employee company at $500M business run rate signals extreme unit economics; if the capital formation thesis holds, revenue per employee dwarfs incumbents and justifies a premium valuation multiple.
“We back 35 insurance carriers with a half a billion in business. Um and should uh should grow to about a billion over the course of the next uh seven-ish months.” ▶ 0:39
Re's capital structure has three tranches: a first-loss layer of ~$77M of the team's own capital, a higher-yielding illiquid mezzanine (reUSD junior), and a senior liquid layer (reUSD) that is minimum 2x over-collateralized and very remote from insurance risk.
framework🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRereUSDMorphoFluidPendle
So what: First-loss capital from the founders is a credibility signal. The senior tranche's double over-collateralization makes it a potential institutional-grade instrument, not retail speculation.
Stablecoin depositors in Re's senior tranche earn risk-free + 250 bps; the mezzanine tranche earns risk-free + 800–850 bps, translating to approximately 12% total yield at current rates.
data_point🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRereUSDUSDC
So what: At 12% vs. 4% T-bill yields, the spread is ~800 bps — wide enough that even risk-adjusted it competes with many alternative credit instruments; important comparator when sizing DeFi yield strategies.
≈ “the senior the senior tranche is fixed. It's 250 basis points above risk-free, and then for the mez tranche, I think it's between 800 and 850” paraphrase
Re's yield is generated through implicit leverage: $1 of on-chain collateral posted in a regulated trust account collateralizes $5–$7 of written premium, effectively allowing the capital pool to earn 5x the risk-free rate before fees and loss ratios.
framework🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highRereinsurance
So what: This 5–7x premium leverage is the core math behind why stablecoin holders can earn teens returns; the key risk is loss-ratio spike — investors should monitor Re's combined ratio disclosures.
“that dollar is levered right five to seven times. And so in effect that pool of capital can earn 5x the risk-free rate.” ▶ 36:30
Re focuses exclusively on low-volatility, law-of-large-numbers lines — auto, home, workers' comp, commercial property — deliberately avoiding binary-outcome catastrophe risk (hurricane, wildfire) to make the business highly predictable and modellable.
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRe
So what: The deliberate avoidance of cat risk is an underwriting discipline signal; it limits upside but dramatically narrows tail risk for depositors, making the protocol safer to stress-test.
Re's reUSD receipt token is already accepted as collateral on Morpho, Fluid, and Pendle, enabling looping (borrowing against the receipt to re-enter the pool), pushing effective yields to high teens–low 20s% on a fully looped basis.
data_point🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRereUSDMorphoFluidPendleSteakhouseChaos Labs
So what: DeFi composability is a TVL flywheel for Re; looping demand increases the capital Re can deploy, but introduces liquidation risk for the looper — not the protocol itself.
Bermuda reinsurers are already approaching Re to tap its on-chain capital pool, suggesting the protocol is transitioning from a standalone carrier to a common-pool capital marketplace analogous to Lloyd's of London.
anecdote🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highReLloyd's of LondonMunich ReSwiss Re
So what: Incumbent reinsurers using Re rather than competing with it is the key inflection signal; watch for formal partnerships or white-label announcements as a buy signal for the RE token.
“Bermuda reinsurers came in reinsurers are approaching us to be able to tap that pool” ▶ 18:45
Re's primary growth metric is TVL, not revenue; Karan spends most of his time on TVL formation and recruiting underwriters with multi-billion-dollar book relationships from traditional reinsurance.
framework🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRe
So what: For token/protocol investors, TVL growth is the leading indicator; declining TVL is a red flag before it shows in yields or partner count.
Re is targeting $1B+ in premium by January 1, 2027 — the annual insurance market renewal date — treating that as the pivotal commercial milestone to establish itself as a serious market player.
prediction🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRe
So what: Jan 1, 2027 is a hard check-in date; underperformance vs. $1B target would signal market-share traction problems ahead of the next renewal cycle.
“we're gearing up for Jan 1st, 2027, which is when the insurance market kind of turns over and renews, to crack a billion um in premium.” ▶ 51:53
Stablecoin Capital Markets: The $5T Yield-Hunting Wave 6
Avichal Garg (Electric Capital) predicts stablecoin supply will grow from ~$200B today to $5 trillion, and argues 20–80% of those holders will seek yield above T-bill rates, creating a massive new demand pool for structured on-chain yield products.
prediction🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric CapitalUSDCstablecoinsEthereum
So what: A $5T stablecoin market with 20% yield-seeking = $1T hunting for instruments like Re's; protocols that lock in supply-side relationships early (insurance cos, mortgage originators) have structural first-mover advantage.
“you're going to have today you have a couple hundred billion, you're going to end up with 5 trillion dollars of USD stablecoins on chain” ▶ 5:14
Garg frames on-chain stablecoin markets as a direct replacement for the entire traditional capital-markets function of fintech — collapsing Blackstone-style debt tranching, pension fund distribution, and securitization into a single smart contract.
thesis🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric CapitalBlackstonestablecoinsEthereum
So what: If correct, every fintech with a large capital-markets team (lending, insurance, HELOC, credit cards) faces margin compression from on-chain competitors with no equivalent overhead.
“you've collapsed that whole thing now into a smart contract. And the stablecoin markets are just the capital markets now.” ▶ 6:11
Garg predicts that within roughly a decade, the distinction between 'on-chain' and 'off-chain' capital will dissolve, just as 'going online' became meaningless once the internet was ubiquitous — and expects this convergence within 5 years.
prediction🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric Capitalstablecoins
So what: A 5-year horizon for on-chain/off-chain convergence sets a concrete timeline for when institutional incumbents must have resolved their crypto-compliance posture — or cede ground.
“I think there will come a day, somewhere in the next decade, where we just stop using that term.” ▶ 53:40
The real yield from real-world insurance premiums flowing back to stablecoin depositors is fundamentally non-self-referential — it does not depend on crypto-asset prices, unlike Terra/Luna-type yields that collapse when the underlying asset falls.
contrarian🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highReTerraLunaUSDC
So what: This is the critical risk differentiation: the yield is uncorrelated to crypto price cycles, which means Re-type products could retain yield in a bear market where CeFi/DeFi lending yields collapse.
≈ “This is not like a Terra Luna situation where it's all you know, exogenous to crypto and it's all self-referential and when it unwinds it unwinds in a terrible way.” paraphrase
The Berkshire Hathaway / Geico / Gen Re model — using insurance float as essentially levered investment capital — is the explicit template Re is replicating on-chain; Apollo/Athene is also cited as a parallel.
framework🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highReBerkshire HathawayGeicoGen ReApolloAthene
So what: This framing suggests Re's terminal value should be benchmarked against Berkshire's insurance+asset-management moat, not just other DeFi protocols — dramatically higher valuation ceiling.
≈ “Berkshire has Geico and has Gen Re that generate all of this premium float, ends up being invested. Apollo has Athene.” paraphrase
Garg argues that the entire supply-side DeFi stack (Morpho, Aave, Pendle, Gauntlet, Steakhouse) is now fully built, and the missing piece that finally activates the flywheel is demand — specifically, real-world yield sources that justify stablecoin deployment.
thesis🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric CapitalMorphoAavePendleGauntletSteakhouse
So what: If demand (real yield) is the last piece, protocols that credibly bridge real-world cashflows to DeFi should capture disproportionate TVL growth in 2026–2027.
“The last thing that we needed was the demand side, right? It's like, why do the stable coin holders want to do this? Why do they want to put it into the plumbing in the first place? And it's to get yield.” ▶ 55:26
Fintech Disruption: On-Chain Operational Efficiency Moat 5
Garg's core investment thesis: in financial products, even a 10–30 basis point efficiency improvement causes the entire market to switch providers, because counterparties are rational and numbers are large — making DeFi's structural cost reduction a winner-take-all dynamic rather than a marginal upgrade.
thesis🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric Capital
So what: This logic applies across any high-volume financial product (mortgages, credit cards, HELOCs): the first on-chain provider to hit regulatory acceptance and competitive rates doesn't just gain share — it can price out incumbents entirely.
“a 10 basis point improvement in something like insurance is dramatic, right? Like that is enough where you can go outcompete everybody else” ▶ 14:45
Garg argues incumbents (banks, insurers, mortgage brokers) will adopt AI relatively quickly because AI vendors (OpenAI, Anthropic) are actively deploying forward engineering teams to help them — but stablecoin/on-chain capital markets adoption will lag years behind because no equivalent forward-deployment exists.
causal🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric CapitalOpenAIAnthropicMunich ReSwiss Re
So what: On-chain fintech startups have a wider and longer moat from stablecoin capital markets than from AI alone; investors should weight the capital-markets advantage heavily, not just the AI workflow story.
≈ “there's no forward-deployed engineering organization that's going into insurance companies and teaching them how to do startups to do stablecoins right now” paraphrase
The three competitive levers in any lending/insurance fintech are: (1) base rate (Fed funds — uncontrollable), (2) underwriting quality (largely converged — dangerous to over-optimize), and (3) operational efficiency (the only real startup lever).
framework🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric Capital
So what: Investors evaluating any fintech startup should ask: what is their operational efficiency advantage, not their claims of better underwriting — the latter is almost always a trap.
≈ “the place where you have leverage as a business to ultimately compete as a startup is to be more operationally efficient” paraphrase
Re operates with fewer than 12 employees heading toward multi-billion dollars in insurance market participation — cited alongside Uniswap (100 employees vs Nasdaq's 10,000) and Hyperliquid (14 people vs Coinbase's thousands) as evidence of the crypto-enabled small-firm phenomenon.
data_point🗣 David (host)conviction: highReUniswapNasdaqHyperliquidCoinbase
So what: The headcount-to-market-size ratio is an early-stage productivity benchmark; comparable ratios at Uniswap/Hyperliquid became signals of protocol moat before broad recognition.
Reinsurance's operational edge can be compounded: AI workflows that ingest risk data faster than incumbents at origination — cutting through the chain of intermediaries from insured to capital — create responsiveness advantages that translate to lower combined ratios.
causal🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: mediumRe
So what: Watch for Re (or competitors) announcing AI-driven underwriting throughput metrics as a leading indicator of competitive differentiation vs. legacy reinsurers.
Lloyd's of London as the DeFi Blueprint 4
Garg argues that Lloyd's of London — a 330-year-old institution with a governance council, diverse underwriter syndicates, a shared capital pool, and secondary market trading of risk — is structurally isomorphic to a DeFi protocol like Morpho or Aave.
framework🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highLloyd's of LondonMorphoAaveCeloRe
So what: If this framing holds, Lloyd's market share (~$60B GWP) is the benchmark for Re's protocol-layer ceiling — and the governance structure gives a template for token design that has survived centuries.
≈ “I was like, 'Holy [__] this looks like a DeFi protocol... You squint at it and you're like, This looks kind of like Morpho and Aave'” paraphrase ▶ 22:32
Re explicitly models its governance token on Lloyd's governance structure: the token controls acceptable counterparties, lines of business, capital requirements, common pool size, release timing, and economics — giving large holders enormous leverage over trillion-dollar capital flows.
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highReLloyd's of London
So what: RE token value is not speculative — it controls allocation of an eventually multi-billion capital pool. Token holders are de facto governors of a regulated capital marketplace, analogous to Lloyd's Names.
≈ “it's a governance token for the re ecosystem. It's intended, you know, as we discussed kind of emulate Lloyd's of London governance” paraphrase
Lloyd's of London holds licenses in practically every country and has country-specific legislation enabling local carriers to transact on the network — Re explicitly sets this as its North Star regulatory template.
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highReLloyd's of London
So what: The regulatory moat Lloyd's has built over 330 years is exactly what Re needs to replicate; progress on international licensing should be watched as a milestone.
“That's the North Star for us.” ▶ 22:06
Garg draws a direct historical analogy between Amazon using internal infrastructure (AWS origin story) and Re potentially becoming the platform layer for all reinsurers wanting access to on-chain capital — the 'act two' of solving your own problem then selling the solution to competitors.
framework🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: mediumReAmazonAWSMunich ReSwiss Re
So what: If Re executes this AWS-for-insurance playbook, its revenue ceiling expands from $1B-level underwriting to a platform tax on the entire $1T reinsurance market — a 10–100x revenue expansion.
“if you solve it for yourself and you really get the flywheel going, then you can go offer that as your AWS to everybody else” ▶ 19:42
RE Token: Value Accrual & Governance Design 3
The RE governance token launched at the time of this episode; it controls the common capital pool's investment policy, counterparty eligibility, lines of business, and economics — giving large token holders power over capital allocation analogous to Lloyd's Names (capital contributors).
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highReRE token
So what: Unlike most DeFi governance tokens that govern fee switches over low-revenue protocols, RE governance controls a capital pool with real insurance premiums — the economic stakes behind governance are unusually high.
Karan frames RE token value accrual as coming from controlling the world's largest insurance capital pool over time, not from a direct fee-switch — arguing that the economic power of directing tens of billions of capital is itself the value, not a token buyback.
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: mediumReRE token
So what: Investors seeking direct cash-flow yield from the token may be disappointed; the value thesis is governance optionality over a growing capital pool — more equity-like than bond-like.
“there is a a sum of economics and cash flows that can perform multitude of economic actions that that show value here” ▶ 50:57
Re has 51 active insurance trees (counterparties) as of the recording and expects to reach hundreds, which progressively dampens earnings volatility through diversification — a law-of-large-numbers argument for yield stability.
data_point🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRe
So what: Counterparty count is an underappreciated risk metric; fewer than ~30 carriers means idiosyncratic carrier failure could cause yield volatility; the 51 figure and growing trajectory are stabilizing signals.
“we've got 51 active insurance trees today. We'll get to the hundreds in short order” ▶ 39:09
Market Size & Macro Insurance Landscape 5
Global reinsurance premium is approximately $1 trillion annually; total insurance premiums (health, life, property & casualty) are $7–8 trillion annually; together this constitutes roughly 12% of global GDP.
data_point🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highMunich ReSwiss ReLloyd's of London
So what: A $1T addressable market in reinsurance alone, with Re at $500M run-rate, implies Re has captured ~0.05% of TAM — massive runway even before moving into primary insurance.
“a trillion in reinsurance premium that's processed annually” ▶ 1:08
Garg extends the insurance-as-GDP-percentage argument further: if government services (military, police, fire, Medicaid) are treated as implicit insurance, the true 'insurance' share of the economy is closer to 80% of GDP, not 12%.
framework🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: exploratory
So what: A thought-experiment framing, not a trade. But it reinforces the thesis that insurance/risk-transfer is a foundational economic primitive — and digitizing it on-chain is categorically larger than a fintech niche.
“I actually like. It's not like 12% of GDP, it's actually like 80%” ▶ 26:57
Traditional reinsurance capital comes exclusively from pension funds, sovereigns, ultra-high-net-worth individuals, and family offices — entirely inaccessible to retail or DeFi participants until Re.
contrarian🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRereinsurance
So what: Re is democratizing an asset class that was structurally gated to institutional capital; this could attract significant inflows from yield-hungry retail stablecoin holders who have no other route to insurance-linked returns.
“who's supplying capital to this market right now, it's pension funds, it's sovereigns, it's ultra-high net worth individuals, and certain family offices” ▶ 8:13
Insurance risk carved up by Re is geographically uncorrelated (US auto + home + workers comp across states), sector-uncorrelated, and crypto-price-uncorrelated — making it a genuine diversifier in a DeFi portfolio that is otherwise highly correlated to ETH/BTC.
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highRe
So what: For DeFi portfolio construction, Re exposure functions like a zero-beta yield sleeve — valuable precisely because it provides yield in bear markets when other DeFi yields compress.
“completely uncorrelated with equities, with crypto, and the like” ▶ 8:23
Re's float investment model — premium collected but not yet paid out acts as free-float invested at the risk-free rate — combined with the implicit 5–7x leverage creates a compounding return structure, not a one-time premium.
framework🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: highReBerkshire Hathaway
So what: The float model means Re's yield is partially funded by investment income (even at 4–5% risk-free), not solely underwriting margin — this makes yields more stable across insurance cycles.
“insurance companies and asset managers go together like peanut butter and jelly” ▶ 39:24
Bear Market Timing & Contrarian Infrastructure Observation 4
Garg observes that Real World Asset / DeFi infrastructure is coming together precisely in a bear market — when few are paying attention — and by the time participants notice, the bull market will have already begun, echoing the pattern of every prior DeFi cycle.
contrarian🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highElectric Capital
So what: Classic counter-cyclical entry signal: if Re and similar RWA-yield protocols are building meaningful TVL and carrier relationships in a bear market, the risk/reward for early positioning is favorable before retail attention returns.
≈ “it's the depth of the bear market, so nobody's paying attention. Uh and so by the time people figure this out, we'll be like kind of raging bull again, and people will be like, 'Oh, yeah, why wasn't I paying attention 18 months ago?'” paraphrase
Garg says all of the DeFi infrastructure supply-side components (Morpho, Aave, Pendle, Gauntlet, Steakhouse risk curation) are now production-ready and battle-tested — the rails exist and the last piece is real-yield demand, which is now arriving.
thesis🗣 Avichal Gargconviction: highMorphoAavePendleGauntletSteakhouse
So what: The implication is that TVL in real-yield DeFi protocols could inflect sharply as demand catches up to existing supply infrastructure — a non-linear rather than linear growth path.
“The plumbing is in place to make the flywheels work.” ▶ 55:22
The 'DeFi mullet' architecture (fintech in the front, DeFi capital markets in the back) — already live in Coinbase with Morpho (Bitcoin loans) and Athena (yield) — is cited as the template for Re to become a consumer savings product via similar distribution.
framework🗣 David (host)conviction: mediumReCoinbaseMorphoAthenaBankless
So what: Coinbase integration would be a step-function TVL catalyst for Re; watching for distribution partnerships with major retail on-ramps is a key trade trigger.
Karan sees a natural long-term path from reinsurance to broader insurance and eventually to asset management — the same convergence already visible in Berkshire/Apollo — but deliberately avoids designing for it now, focusing on the reinsurance vertical first.
thesis🗣 Karan Saroyaconviction: mediumReBerkshire HathawayApollo
So what: The optionality to expand into primary insurance and asset management is embedded in the protocol design even if not activated — this embedded optionality should be valued in terminal multiples.
“If you think about what reinsurers and insurance companies become in the limit, they become the largest asset managers in the world” ▶ 57:17

Joe Lonsdale 1 episode(s) · 34 insights

How AI Agents Are Creating an Infinitely Scalable Workforce39 min34 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend) describes how Blend's AI agent product 'Autopilot' is automating mortgage origination workflows in real time, cutting loan closing times from 29 to 21 days, and generating $10M+ in early ARR pipeline within two months of launch—while the stock trades at under 3x revenue despite profitability and accelerating AI adoption across its 300-bank customer base.
Blend Autopilot: Agentic AI in Mortgage Origination · 6Blend Valuation & Market Disconnect · 6Mortgage Market Structure & Rate Sensitivity · 5AI Engineering Productivity & Internal Compounding · 5Model Landscape & AI Benchmarking for Enterprise · 3Macro AI & Economic Outlook · 5Operator Lessons: Focus, Capital Discipline & SaaS Survival · 4
Blend Autopilot: Agentic AI in Mortgage Origination 6
Blend's Autopilot AI agent, launched in early March 2026, monitors every mortgage in real time—processing new documents at 2am without human intervention—and already has 70 of Blend's ~300 lender customers live on the product.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: Rapid adoption rate (23% of installed base in two months) with no churn signals strong product-market fit; watch for ARR conversion after the 3-month free trial period expires.
“We definitely have the most deployed agents in financial services right now.” ▶ 0:38
Autopilot went from concept in December 2025 to live production with real customers by early March 2026—roughly 10 weeks—demonstrating that modern LLM capability compressed what would previously have been an 18-month product cycle.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: Speed-to-production compression is now a structural moat for incumbents with rich training data; Blend's 14-year historical loan data gives it an edge that a greenfield AI startup cannot replicate quickly.
“it was only a concept in December. The team picked it up in January and by end of March or sorry, beginning of March, it was live in production with real customers.” ▶ 30:24
Blend's first Autopilot deployment reduced one lender's average mortgage closing time from 29 days to 21 days—a 28% reduction—using only the initial version of the product.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: Quantified operational ROI makes the upsell story concrete; lenders competing on speed (e.g., Rocket Mortgage) will face pressure to match, driving further adoption.
“They took their mortgage closing process... took it down from 29 to 21 days. Like just with the first version of Autopilot.” ▶ 35:22
Blend gave all lenders a 3-month free trial of Autopilot and only began pricing and selling in April 2026; within weeks it had already built $10M+ in pipeline, and management guided for 10–15% incremental revenue growth on top of prior projections.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: The free-trial window is closing; the Q2/Q3 2026 earnings calls are the inflection point to watch for Autopilot ARR conversion showing up in results.
≈ “We already have double-digit million, 10 plus million of pipeline. We told the street that we're going to have 10 to 15% incremental growth on top of whatever we projected from autopilot.” paraphrase
Autopilot functions as an ambient agentic layer: it spins up a sub-agent for each new data event (document upload, consumer action) within the full context of that specific loan, performs multi-step reasoning, and spins back down—positioning human loan officers as 'air traffic controllers' across hundreds of simultaneous AI-driven tasks.
framework🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: The 'air traffic control' staffing model implies significant reduction in per-loan labor cost for lenders; if widely adopted, it structurally compresses the unit economics of mortgage servicing.
“it has full context of the rules, full context of what that specific financial situation is for the consumer, full context of everything that's happened on that loan. Could be at 2:00 in the morning, spins up an agent, does the work.” ▶ 20:37
Blend claims to be the most deployed agentic AI platform in financial services as of mid-2026, with differentiation not in simple document-parsing but in multi-step contextual reasoning across thousands of regulatory rules.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: Verifiable leadership in deployed agents is a key differentiator from competitors; investors should look for third-party validation or case studies to confirm the claim.
“They're not just like shitty agents that are looking at one pay stub and saying, 'Here's the answer.' It's like it does everything.” ▶ 22:48
Blend Valuation & Market Disconnect 6
Blend (ticker: BLND) was trading at a market cap of approximately $350M as of the recording date (approximately June 2026) against ~$120M revenue in 2025, implying under 3x trailing revenue despite profitability and accelerating AI product momentum.
data_point🗣 Joe Lonsdaleconviction: highBlendBLND
So what: At under 3x revenue with a profitable AI-enabled SaaS model growing at 10%+ and a new product showing double-digit million pipeline, the stock appears mispriced relative to AI comps; the SaaS overhang is the primary investor concern to monitor.
“your market cap is in the range of 350 million. And so you're literally trading at less than three times last year's revenue despite having uh probably as much AI deployed as anyone in this sector.” ▶ 23:54
Blend's Autopilot, if valued as a standalone AI company using typical AI startup multiples, would be worth more than the entire current market cap of Blend—making the bundled valuation appear deeply discounted.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: mediumBlendAutopilot
So what: Potential sum-of-parts argument: if market eventually separates legacy SaaS from AI agent revenue, the re-rating could be significant; watch for management to surface this narrative.
≈ “somebody was making fun of me. They're like, 'Man, if only autopilot were a separate thing, it'd be worth a lot more than Blend is worth today.' Just as a standalone.” paraphrase
Blend was profitable in 2025 (light profit in Q1 ramping through the year) and profitability is still increasing—a sharp reversal from the 2021–2022 era when the company was burning cash aggressively.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendBLND
So what: Profitability while growing removes the dilution/liquidity risk that suppressed the stock post-IPO; cash-generative AI upsell revenue should further improve margins.
The dominant investor concern suppressing Blend's multiple is the 'SaaS apocalypse' thesis—that AI will displace legacy workflow software companies—but Blend's argument is that it is itself deploying the AI, not being replaced by it.
contrarian🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendBLND
So what: The key variable is execution: companies that successfully layer AI onto regulated-data moats will re-rate while laggards get disrupted; Blend's 14-year dataset in mortgage origination is a non-trivial barrier.
“I don't think there's a deep enough understanding in every case of what Agent Tech AI can and will do to make that bet for a lot of them.” ▶ 36:08
Blend operates in a highly regulated financial services environment, which management explicitly frames as a moat against rapid AI displacement—new entrants cannot easily substitute into regulated origination workflows overnight.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendBLND
So what: Regulatory complexity creates an 'affordance of time' for incumbents to retool with AI, but only if they move proactively; Blend's position is secure in the near term while pure-play AI mortgage startups navigate compliance.
“because we're in such a regulated space, we have the additional affordance of not having to worry about somebody just ripping us out overnight.” ▶ 33:44
Blend's stock lost over 90% from its 2021 IPO peak, driven by the rate-induced mortgage market collapse, not by competitive displacement—the origination volume decline was macro, not market-share loss.
causal🗣 Joe Lonsdaleconviction: highBlendBLND
So what: The recovery thesis depends on rate normalization reigniting mortgage origination volume; Blend's ~20% market share would translate to significant revenue leverage in a refinancing cycle.
Mortgage Market Structure & Rate Sensitivity 5
The U.S. mortgage market is running at approximately 4–5 million originations in 2026, well below the normalized 7–8 million per year—a historic low persisting since 2023 driven by elevated interest rates.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendRocket Mortgagemortgage market
So what: Blend is effectively earning 20% market share on a volume base that is 40–50% below normal; a rate normalization cycle could double Blend's addressable originations without any share gain.
Blend holds approximately 20% market share in both first-lien mortgage originations AND home equity loans/lines of credit (HELOCs) in the U.S., processing origination workflows for 300 banks and lenders.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendBLND
So what: Dual 20% share in first and second lien products provides natural hedge—HELOC demand rises when consumers are financially stressed (at high rates) while refi/purchase demand rises in rate-decline cycles.
“We're also 20% of the home equity loans and lines in the country as well as being about 20% of the mortgage market.” ▶ 11:07
Blend expanded into HELOCs as a deliberate hedge product, noting that consumer financial stress (credit card revolving debt) drives HELOC demand since the rate is approximately one-third of credit card rates—making it a counter-cyclical product relative to purchase mortgages.
causal🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendHELOC
So what: HELOC origination volumes tend to rise in high-rate environments as consumers tap equity for debt consolidation; Blend's HELOC business provides partial revenue offset during mortgage downturns.
Rising rates (as of recording in mid-2026) are pressuring mortgage volumes again, suggesting the mortgage market recovery is not imminent despite earlier optimism—the macro headwind for Blend's core business persists.
data_point🗣 Joe Lonsdaleconviction: mediumBlendmortgage market
So what: Rate trajectory is the single biggest exogenous driver for Blend's revenue outlook; investors should monitor Fed policy and 30-year mortgage rates as a leading indicator for Blend volume.
“Rates are going up again, by the way.” ▶ 25:48
Quicken Loans' 2016 Super Bowl ad for 'push button get mortgage' served as an industry catalyst that forced conservative banks to accelerate digital mortgage adoption—suggesting external marketing events can trigger latent enterprise demand.
anecdote🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highRocket MortgageQuicken LoansBlend
So what: Analogous to the current dynamic where Claude/GPT demos are triggering enterprise AI adoption; AI product demonstrations by well-funded competitors can accelerate Blend's own Autopilot sales cycle.
AI Engineering Productivity & Internal Compounding 5
Blend's engineers doubled their pull request output from 6 per month (December 2025) to approximately 12 per month (April 2026) as a direct result of AI coding tools—a measured 2x productivity gain in four months.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlend
So what: If 2x productivity translates to 2x product velocity at constant headcount, Blend can ship features and fix bugs at roughly half the prior cost—a structural improvement in R&D efficiency that should widen margins.
≈ “end of last year, our average engineer was doing six pull requests per month in December. That's already doubled 4 months later.” paraphrase
Blend's highest-adoption engineers are already reaching 80–120 pull requests per month using AI tools, versus the average of ~12—implying a 7–10x productivity multiplier is achievable for skilled practitioners.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlend
So what: This 'haves vs. have-nots' within engineering teams suggests that AI fluency is becoming a leading predictor of individual engineer output; companies that train and retain AI-fluent engineers will outcompete those that don't.
“the haves and the have-nots are like the best people are doing 80 to 120 pull requests a month.” ▶ 28:51
Blend is building internal 'ambient intelligence'—AI agents that automatically detect bugs from customer tickets, write the fix, and open a pull request for human approval—removing the traditional product-manager-to-sprint pipeline for small issues.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlend
So what: Ambient bug-fix agents compress the issue-to-resolution cycle and reduce product manager overhead; companies that deploy this internally will structurally reduce SaaS maintenance costs and improve customer satisfaction.
“The agent knows how to fix the bug. The agent goes and fixes the bug and then opens a pull request on our engineering team to say, 'Hey, we just fixed this bug. Go and approve this so we can merge.'” ▶ 28:32
Blend's internal ambient AI infrastructure is, in Ghamghami's view, a bigger long-term value driver than Autopilot itself—because it is the 'engine that creates the next 10 Autopilots' over the next two years.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilot
So what: Internal AI productivity compounding accelerates product roadmap and compounds competitive moat; investors should watch management's ability to operationalize ambient AI, not just ship Autopilot.
“that's even a bigger story than Autopilot because that's the engine that creates the next 10 Autopilots over the next couple years.” ▶ 29:14
Ghamghami considers dedicated 'AI Officers' (chief AI officers) a gimmick—arguing that AI adoption must be CEO-led and top-down or it fails, and companies that treat it as a role rather than a culture will be the losers.
contrarian🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlend
So what: A useful screen for enterprise AI adoption quality: companies with standalone CAIOs but no evidence of CEO-level AI usage are likely laggards; invest in companies where AI is a board-level strategic priority, not a title.
“companies that hire AI's are like kind of a joke, to be honest. You either it's run like that top-down or it's not.” ▶ 27:24
Model Landscape & AI Benchmarking for Enterprise 3
Blend runs its Autopilot agents against a proprietary 'agentic banking index'—a set of hundreds of historical mortgage scenarios—to benchmark different AI models on accuracy, speed, and cost before deploying them in production.
framework🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendAutopilotGeminiGoogle
So what: Domain-specific eval suites are the real moat for enterprise AI deployment; incumbents with rich historical transaction data can benchmark models far more accurately than general benchmarks, giving them an edge in model selection.
“I kind of want to release that cuz it's really cool to see that progression over time. And see what models are good at what things.” ▶ 31:12
Blend uses a mix of AI models (not exclusively one provider) for Autopilot and found Gemini 2.5 Pro to be 'quietly really good' but underutilized at launch in 2024—suggesting the model quality gap between frontier providers is narrowing.
data_point🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: mediumGeminiGoogleBlendAnthropic
So what: Multi-model enterprise deployments (rather than single-provider lock-in) are becoming standard; model providers competing on price/performance will face margin compression as enterprise buyers optimize across providers.
“Gemini 2.5 Pro, which came out in 2024, was quietly really good. Nobody was using it.” ▶ 30:44
Ghamghami identified late 2025 (specifically around Anthropic's Claude 'Opus 4/5/6' release cycle) as the inflection point where AI intelligence became sufficient for Autopilot-class complex reasoning tasks—corroborating that mid-2025 marked a step-change in agentic capability.
causal🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highAnthropicClaudeBlendAutopilot
So what: The November 2025 'moment' where agentic AI became practically deployable for complex regulated workflows is a useful timestamp for evaluating enterprise AI adoption curves across other verticals.
“I don't think the intelligence was really there for something like Autopilot to exist until mid last year.” ▶ 30:17
Macro AI & Economic Outlook 5
Ghamghami predicts the 2030s will be 'the most booming decade in 100 years,' driven by AI enabling an infinitely scalable workforce that compounds innovation capacity—unlocking infrastructure, energy, housing, and robotics opportunities previously constrained by capital and labor.
prediction🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highAIroboticsenergyhousing
So what: A decade-long secular boom thesis implies persistent demand for AI infrastructure, automation capex, and productivity-enhancing software; favors long-duration positions in AI picks-and-shovels over near-term cycle plays.
≈ “I think the 2030s are going to be the most booming decade in a hundred years.” paraphrase
Agentic AI will drive deflationary pressure on consumer costs across all industries by enabling tasks to be performed at a fraction of prior labor cost—identified as a structural tailwind for consumer purchasing power.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highAI
So what: AI-driven deflation in services (mortgage processing, healthcare admin, legal, insurance) could suppress services CPI, potentially enabling central banks to ease even in strong growth environments.
“It's going to lead to lower cost of everything for consumers.” ▶ 38:08
The speaker frames physical world applications—solar, robots building housing, machines repairing infrastructure, AI-powered bridges construction—as the next compounding wave after digital AI, with potential to unlock trillions in deferred capital spending.
prediction🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: mediumsolarroboticsinfrastructure
So what: Early-stage exposure to AI-enabled physical infrastructure (construction robotics, AI-powered energy grid management, autonomous heavy equipment) could be the next re-rating cycle after pure software AI plays.
“Think about energy, solar. Think about, you know, machines building housing, machines repairing things, machines building bridges.” ▶ 38:21
Every company across all industries will be forced to re-architect their operations around AI agents, with winners being those that make 'agents the primary user of any platform'—implying traditional SaaS UI design becomes obsolete.
thesis🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highAI
So what: Platform companies that redesign their APIs and interfaces for agent-first interaction (rather than human-first UI) will capture enterprise AI spend; companies stuck in legacy human-UI paradigms face disruption.
“every industry has to be re-architected... how do you make agents the primary user of any platform and utilize the capability of agents in the best way possible.” ▶ 0:08
Blend's early skepticism from bank customers about AI agents directly parallels the initial 2012–2015 skepticism about mobile mortgage applications, suggesting a pattern: regulated financial institutions follow technology adoption curves roughly 3–5 years behind consumer tech.
framework🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendfinancial servicesAI
So what: Banking AI adoption is likely 3–5 years behind consumer internet adoption curves; the current 'early adopter' phase (70 of 300 lenders) likely represents an inflection that will accelerate sharply once the trial period converts.
“It started with skepticism from the banks. And then by the way, we're seeing the same thing now with the genetic AI, so it's like a perfect analogue for that time.” ▶ 11:45
Operator Lessons: Focus, Capital Discipline & SaaS Survival 4
Ghamghami's key 2021 post-mortem lesson: expanding into too many product lines simultaneously (title company acquisition, home insurance agency, income verification) destroyed focus and diluted capital at exactly the wrong time—the antithesis of the 'one alpha-generating bet' framework he now employs.
framework🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendBLND
So what: A useful red flag for high-growth SaaS investors: product proliferation announcements during bull markets often signal strategic drift; prefer companies with a single named flagship product during the AI transition.
“I got us into too many pies at once. Instead of saying this is our bet this is what we're going to go all in on.” ▶ 17:59
Ghamghami argues that the right measure of a business is output per person—not absolute scale—and explicitly credits missing this metric with Blend's 2021–2022 overspend, which he connects directly to the low stock price that persists today.
framework🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highBlendBLND
So what: Revenue per employee and gross profit per employee are the metrics that separate sustainable AI-era businesses from headcount-bloated legacy firms; Blend's post-rationalization efficiency is the untold story at the current multiple.
“the interesting and important measure of a business is... not how much it gets done in an absolute sense, but how much it gets done... per person basically.” ▶ 17:26
Joe Lonsdale (8VC) explicitly states he is currently bullish on Blend stock and has been an investor for 14 years—a material disclosure that colors the framing of all financial commentary in this episode.
anecdote🗣 Joe Lonsdaleconviction: highBlendBLND8VCJoe Lonsdale
So what: All positive assertions about Blend's valuation and AI progress are from a conflicted source (host is long-term investor and board member); independent verification of Autopilot metrics and pipeline claims is warranted before acting.
“I'm obviously involved. I've been invested for... 14 years.” ▶ 23:10
Palantir's 'success-oriented' deployment model—where software quality and headcount are irrelevant and only the customer's dollar outcome matters—was the foundational operating philosophy Ghamghami exported to Blend and is now the lens for evaluating Autopilot ROI.
framework🗣 Nima Ghamghami (CEO, Blend)conviction: highPalantirBlendPLTR
So what: Companies with Palantir alumni in operator roles often prioritize measurable customer ROI over product aesthetics; this outcome-first culture is a predictor of enterprise retention in regulated verticals.
≈ “the customer's outcomes... the software doesn't matter. The quality of the product doesn't matter... did you deliver that $100 million outcome for that one or not? And that's all that mattered.” paraphrase ▶ 7:07

Sourcery with Molly O'Shea 1 episode(s) · 32 insights

Harvey Co-Founder Gabe Pereyra on the Token Pricing Reckoning Coming for AI45 min32 insights2026-06-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Harvey co-founder Gabe Pereyra and researcher Nico discuss the open-sourcing of their Legal Agent Benchmark (LAB), the imminent token-cost reckoning for enterprise AI buyers, and Harvey's strategy of becoming an organizational AI layer for law firms rather than competing with model providers on general intelligence. The central thesis: agentic AI usage is exploding in ways that will produce enormous, opaque consumption bills, and the billing/routing infrastructure to manage that is the next competitive battleground.
Token Cost Reckoning & Consumption Pricing Dangers · 5Harvey's Competitive Moat vs. Lab Providers · 5Legal Agent Benchmark (LAB) — Design & Competitive Implications · 5Inference Layer & Open-Source Model Dynamics · 5Agentic AI Transition & Product Architecture Shift · 6Research Strategy & Model Provider Intelligence · 3Billable Hour Analogy & AI Pricing Mechanics · 3
Token Cost Reckoning & Consumption Pricing Dangers 5
Gabe Pereyra argues the single biggest market misconception is that most buyers do not realize how expensive agentic AI is going to get, with individual agent runs already costing $20 for a simple document draft and up to $20,000 for a 100,000-contract review job.
contrarian🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyAI agents
So what: Enterprise SaaS multiples and margin assumptions for AI application-layer companies are underpriced for cost risk; watch for margin compression or billing blow-ups as agentic usage scales.
≈ “The big misconception right now is I don't think people realize how expensive this is going to get. And they're going to be like, what did my agent do that cost me $10 billion?” paraphrase
Harvey is simultaneously running a traditional seat-based SaaS business and a consumption-based business, and Pereyra expects pricing will eventually cycle back toward subscription because token-based billing is too opaque for enterprise buyers to forecast.
prediction🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: mediumHarvey
So what: Pure consumption-revenue models for vertical AI companies may be transitional; revenue predictability could revert, complicating growth modeling for investors in this cohort.
“It will probably at some point go back to something like subscription because it's hard to like forecast these things.” ▶ 14:22
Pereyra draws a direct analogy between law-firm billable-hour billing and token billing: both are granular, misalignment-prone mechanisms that nonetheless converge to fair pricing under competitive pressure, but both create enormous auditability burdens — he predicts a whole ecosystem of token-bill optimization and auditing tools will emerge.
framework🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyUber
So what: Opportunity signal for infrastructure companies doing token observability, routing optimization, and spend management — analogous to cloud cost-management tools (Cloudability, Spot.io) for the AI layer.
“There's going to become this whole ecosystem of like how do you optimize around this because you kind of have these weird misaligned incentives from the model providers.” ▶ 26:12
Model providers face a structural misaligned-incentive problem: they sell consumption tokens and are therefore implicitly incentivized to have agents use as many tokens as possible, creating pressure for third-party routing layers to exist.
causal🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIHarvey
So what: Neutral orchestration/routing layer companies (Harvey, Base Ten, Fireworks, Together AI) gain structural value as enterprise buyers need a trusted intermediary that is NOT incentivized to maximize token usage.
The Uber CTO example is cited as a leading indicator: enterprise CTO-level buyers are already hitting surprise consumption overruns (burning through the full annual coding-token budget in three months), signaling the billing shock wave has begun.
data_point🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highUber
So what: Tech company AI budget disclosures and CTO comments on token overruns are early signals of a broader enterprise re-pricing cycle; watch for cost-management line items in AI spending surveys.
Harvey's Competitive Moat vs. Lab Providers 5
Harvey's core differentiation is organizational-level productivity for law firms versus the individual-productivity tools (Claude for legal, Codex for legal) built by Anthropic and OpenAI, meaning they are not in direct competition for the same buyer decision.
thesis🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Harvey is not a thin wrapper on the model APIs; the moat is enterprise workflow integration, data governance, and multi-model orchestration — harder to disintermediate than a chatbot interface.
“A lot of the products that they're building are individual productivity and the products we're trying to build are organizational productivity for law firms or large in-house departments.” ▶ 7:11
Law firms serving multiple hyperscalers (OpenAI, Google, Microsoft) face a conflict-of-data risk that forces them to be multi-model by necessity: a firm representing OpenAI cannot send that client's sensitive legal data through Anthropic's models.
causal🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyAnthropicOpenAIGoogleMicrosoft
So what: This conflict-risk dynamic is a durable structural reason large law firms need a neutral orchestration layer — it is not purely cost-driven, giving Harvey pricing power that a cheaper single-provider product cannot replicate.
Harvey views model providers the same way Snowflake, Databricks, and Datadog view the cloud hyperscalers: built on top of them and competing with them in selective layers, but not substitutable by them for workflow-depth use cases.
framework🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveySnowflakeDatabricksDatadogAWSGCPAzure
So what: The cloud-layer precedent suggests vertical AI workflow companies can sustain 10–15x revenue multiples even as the underlying model commodity reprices, as long as workflow lock-in is deep.
“We think of the provider similar to cloud.” ▶ 8:14
The most sensitive and highest-value legal data (M&A, internal investigations with market-moving information) structurally cannot flow into public foundation models, which means general-purpose model providers cannot capture the premium legal workflow market.
thesis🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Private data moats in regulated verticals (legal, finance, healthcare) are the durable barrier; companies that build compliant private model infrastructure have a defensible position even as public model capability improves.
“We don't see the general legal intelligence as where the value of kind of Harvey comes from because most of the valuable legal data can't go into the general purpose models.” ▶ 8:32
Harvey's 'Shared Spaces' product lets law firms and their private-equity or corporate clients collaborate on legal projects in a shared workspace, creating a data flywheel across the firm-client relationship without violating ethical walls.
anecdote🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: mediumHarvey
So what: Network effects at the firm-client collaboration level are a second-order moat: the more PE firms and their counsel use the same platform, the harder it is to switch, reinforcing Harvey's position even if underlying models are commoditized.
Legal Agent Benchmark (LAB) — Design & Competitive Implications 5
Harvey open-sourced its Legal Agent Benchmark (LAB) covering 24 law-firm practice areas, structured like coding benchmarks (SWEBench / TerminalBench) with legal 'unit tests' that can be scored objectively — countering the misconception that legal work is too subjective to benchmark.
data_point🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveySWEBench
So what: LAB creates a publicly verifiable performance ranking that could shift enterprise buying decisions toward Harvey-endorsed model selections, embedding Harvey as the neutral referee in the legal AI competitive landscape.
Initial LAB results show Anthropic models are strongest overall, but Claude 5.5 outperforms in some sub-tasks and open-source models outperform in others, meaning no single model is universally best across legal work.
data_point🗣 Nicoconviction: highAnthropicHarveyOpenAIDeepMind
So what: No model provider wins a winner-takes-all legal AI contract; this validates multi-model routing strategies and strengthens the case for orchestration intermediaries like Harvey.
“Anthropic's models are quite strong but there's areas where 5.5 is better. There's some areas where open source is better.” ▶ 5:56
Opus 4.7 is the current LAB leader but is 3x more expensive than Claude 5.5 while only 10–20% more performant, and Gemini 3.5 Flash completes legal tasks 7x faster than frontier models — illustrating quality/cost/latency trade-offs that determine real production routing decisions.
data_point🗣 Nicoconviction: highAnthropicGoogleHarvey
So what: At 3x price for 10-20% quality gain, Opus 4.7 will rarely be the default choice at scale; Anthropic faces volume pressure on premium models as buyers route simpler tasks to cheaper alternatives.
Harvey expects the LAB benchmark to be saturated within one year as models improve, confirming that the benchmark itself is a short-lived moat while the underlying orchestration infrastructure and proprietary data pipelines are the durable asset.
prediction🗣 Nicoconviction: highHarvey
So what: Investors should not assign durable competitive value to Harvey's benchmarking position per se; the benchmark is a customer acquisition and mindshare tool, not a structural barrier.
“I do think the benchmark gets saturated within a year at least.” ▶ 43:38
The LAB benchmark data set was constructed via agent-generated synthetic legal documents, reviewed by Harvey's internal team of former Big Law attorneys, and then validated by a broader lawyer network — a scalable 'human-in-the-loop' data generation method that does not require client data.
framework🗣 Nicoconviction: highHarvey
So what: This agent-led synthetic data generation pattern is replicable for other regulated verticals (healthcare, finance) where real data cannot be used for benchmarking — a template for vertical AI evaluation infrastructure.
Inference Layer & Open-Source Model Dynamics 5
Harvey is actively shifting traffic from closed frontier models to open-source and post-trained models because the cost of serving exclusively from the largest closed models is becoming prohibitive at Harvey's scale.
thesis🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyBase TenFireworksTogether AIApplied ComputeTrajectoryEngram
So what: Inference providers serving open-source models (Base Ten, Fireworks, Together AI) are benefiting from cost-driven migration away from OpenAI/Anthropic APIs; this is a structural tailwind, not just a trend.
Pereyra believes that open-source models post-trained on domain-specific legal data can approach frontier closed-source performance for narrow tasks, meaning a domain-specific fine-tuned model at a fraction of the parameter count can match trillion-parameter models for diligence reviews.
thesis🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarvey
So what: Vertical AI companies that invest in post-training pipelines on proprietary data gain a cost structure advantage; the frontier labs' revenue from enterprise verticals is at risk as post-training matures.
“I probably don't need a trillion parameters if I just need the system to be good at diligence.” ▶ 23:34
Harvey works with Base Ten, Fireworks, Together AI, Applied Compute, Trajectory, and Engram for inference and RL-as-a-service, signaling a multi-vendor inference stack is already standard practice for large enterprise AI deployments.
data_point🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyBase TenFireworksTogether AIApplied ComputeTrajectoryEngram
So what: Smaller inference providers named here (Base Ten, Together AI, Fireworks, Engram, Trajectory) are winning real enterprise contracts; they are acquisition targets or IPO candidates as the inference market matures.
Pereyra observes that performance gains from models now come increasingly from test-time compute and reinforcement learning environments rather than pre-training scale alone, which shifts leverage toward application-layer companies that can build good RL harnesses and data sets.
causal🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarvey
So what: Companies with domain-specific RL environments and evaluation data (like Harvey's LAB) capture more of the model improvement value chain; pure pre-training scale no longer determines the winner in vertical applications.
“A lot of the like performance from the models is coming not just from you know doing this large pre-training but all of this test time compute.” ▶ 15:31
Open-source models post-trained on domain-specific data are showing early results that close the gap with closed-source frontier models, per Nico's research findings from the LAB benchmark community.
data_point🗣 Nicoconviction: mediumHarvey
So what: If open-weight post-training parity is achieved within 12–18 months, Anthropic and OpenAI face significant enterprise API revenue pressure in verticals where training data exists; monitor for benchmark parity milestones.
“We're starting to see early results that show that openweight models when post-trained kind of close the gap with the closed source frontier.” ▶ 41:32
Agentic AI Transition & Product Architecture Shift 6
Harvey pivoted from a chat-based co-pilot product to an agentic architecture approximately six months ago, triggered by the success of coding agents (Claude Code, Codex) in command-line environments, and is now building cloud infrastructure to run those agents at enterprise scale.
anecdote🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarveyAnthropicOpenAI
So what: The six-month lag from coding agent to legal agent is a template for other professional-service verticals; the transition inflection for legal AI is now, not 12–18 months away.
The legal AI inflection for true workflow adoption (not just experimentation) arrived in the past six months, parallel to coding AI inflection two years ago — Pereyra says lawyers are now seeing output quality high enough to change entrenched workflows.
thesis🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarvey
So what: Revenue acceleration at Harvey and peers should be observable in H1 2026 filings; this is a near-term catalyst for valuations in legal AI companies.
“I think we're just starting to see in the past six months that inflection for legal where the models can now generate entire documents.” ▶ 20:34
Harvey processes queries that span from a $20 single-document-draft to a $20,000 batch review of 100,000 contracts, illustrating the enormous range in agentic job sizes and the challenge of unit-economics modeling at the enterprise level.
data_point🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarvey
So what: Revenue per customer at Harvey is highly variable and will be driven by power-user adoption of long-horizon tasks; average revenue metrics will be misleading — watch median vs. top-decile cohort behavior.
Harvey has reached a scale where for some model providers it is the largest consumer of embeddings, and its token consumption has entered a phase described internally as 'just the start' — implying even current high usage is far below steady-state agent saturation.
data_point🗣 Gabe Pereyraconviction: highHarvey
So what: Harvey's token volumes are a leading indicator of the embedding and inference revenue trajectory for Anthropic and OpenAI from the legal vertical; at least one major provider relies on Harvey as a top embeddings customer.
“I think for some of the labs we are like the largest consumer of embeddings.” ▶ 22:24
The next frontier Nico identifies is moving from individual AI intelligence to organizational intelligence — how human-agent teams and lawyer-agent teams collaborate at the firm level — which requires infrastructure and AI research problems that were previously inaccessible because base model quality was insufficient.
prediction🗣 Nicoconviction: highHarvey
So what: The next product generation for Harvey (and vertical AI broadly) is multi-agent workflow orchestration and firm-level knowledge graphs, not just single-task automation — a larger and harder moat to build.
≈ “This year is the year that we see intelligence at an individual level kind of brought into intelligence at an organizational level.” paraphrase
The 'agent harness' — the scaffolding of tools, skills, and delegation rules surrounding a model — is now the buzziest research topic in legal AI, with both open-source community and labs competing to define the standard.
thesis🗣 Nicoconviction: highHarvey
